words.definition: to lower someone's rank, dignity, or self-respect; humiliate
words.mnemonic: a-BASE: shove someone down to the base, the very bottom of the stairs.
words.example: The tyrant tried to abase his rivals by forcing them to kneel.

words.definition: to lessen in force or intensity; die down
words.mnemonic: a-BATE: the debate finally dies down when the loudest kid loses his voice.
words.example: By morning the storm had abated, leaving only a light drizzle.

words.definition: to formally give up power, a throne, or responsibility
words.mnemonic: A king with an ache in his ABDomen just quits the throne.
words.example: The weary king chose to abdicate and retire to the quiet countryside.

words.definition: a departure from what is normal or expected
words.mnemonic: ab-ERR-ation has ERR inside — something has wandered into error, off the normal path.
words.example: One rainy day in the desert was a strange aberration.

words.definition: to hate intensely; regard with disgust
words.mnemonic: abHORrible — you abhor what you find horrible.
words.example: She abhors cruelty to animals in any form.

words.definition: utterly hopeless, miserable, or degrading in the extreme
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'a-reject': a thrown-down, rejected beggar sunk in misery.
words.example: The refugees lived in abject poverty with no food or shelter.

words.definition: to leave secretly and hide, often after wrongdoing
words.mnemonic: The ABSurd CON man absconded with the cash — sneaking off before anyone noticed.
words.example: The treasurer absconded with the club's entire savings.

words.definition: to free someone from guilt, blame, or obligation
words.mnemonic: Absolve = the stain of blame dis-SOLVEs away — you're washed clean.
words.example: The new evidence absolved him of any wrongdoing.

words.definition: sparing in eating and drinking; self-restrained
words.mnemonic: To get ABS, be abstemious — restrain yourself from eating and drinking too much.
words.example: The abstemious monk ate one small meal a day.

words.definition: very hard to understand; obscure
words.mnemonic: The truth buried under six-pack ABS of confusion — ABS-truse, hidden deep.
words.example: The professor's abstruse lecture on quantum fields lost everyone by slide two.

words.definition: a huge, seemingly bottomless deep pit or gap
words.mnemonic: Yell into the abyss and hear the echo fall forever — HUGE and DEEP, just like you circled.
words.example: The climbers peered nervously into the dark abyss below.

words.definition: an award or expression of high praise and honor
words.mnemonic: A knight kneels for 'a cola-ade' toast, raised to praise him.
words.example: Her debut novel earned the highest accolade in the literary world.

words.definition: sharp and biting in tone or manner; harsh
words.mnemonic: 'A-SOUR-bic': a sour, acid-tongued critic who spits vinegar.
words.example: The critic's acerbic review left the young playwright in tears.

words.definition: to accept or agree reluctantly, without protest
words.mnemonic: acqui-YES: you quietly whisper 'yes' even though everything in you says no.
words.example: Outvoted four to one, she finally acquiesced to the group's plan.

words.definition: to declare someone not guilty of a charge
words.mnemonic: The jury says 'we QUIT accusing you' — acquitted, you walk free.
words.example: The jury acquitted her after only an hour of deliberation.

words.definition: bitter and sharp in tone, especially in argument
words.mnemonic: ACRID + money: nothing turns a family dinner bitter faster than fighting over money.
words.example: The debate turned acrimonious, with both candidates trading personal insults.

words.definition: keen, quick insight or good judgment
words.mnemonic: A-CUE-men: the pool shark reads every cue shot instantly — needle-sharp mind.
words.root: acuere = to sharpen (Latin, as in acute)
words.example: Her business acumen turned a lemonade stand into a citywide chain.

words.definition: refusing to change one's mind; utterly unyielding
words.mnemonic: 'Adam Ant': a stubborn ant, hard as diamond, refuses to budge.
words.example: She was adamant that she would never sell the family farm.

words.definition: to stick firmly to a surface, rule, or belief
words.mnemonic: AD-HERE: the sticker is glued right HERE and won't come off.
words.example: Players must adhere to the rules or leave the game.

words.definition: to warn or scold someone firmly but gently
words.mnemonic: ADmonish = the ADult MONitor scolding you: 'I'm warning you, don't do that again.'
words.example: The coach admonished the players for skipping practice.

words.definition: fuss or unnecessary trouble over something minor
words.mnemonic: 'Without further ADO' — Shakespeare's Much ADO About Nothing: a giant fuss over nothing.
words.example: Without further ado, the principal announced the winners.

words.definition: skillful and clever, with hands or mind
words.mnemonic: French 'à droite' = to the right: the 'right-handed' one who never fumbles.
words.example: An adroit negotiator, she calmed both sides in under ten minutes.

words.definition: excessive, over-the-top admiration or flattery
words.mnemonic: ADULts screaming for a boy band like thirteen-year-olds — adult adoration gone overboard.
words.example: The rookie soaked up the crowd's adulation after his winning goal.

words.definition: friendly and easy to talk to
words.mnemonic: AFFable people are easy to have A FABle-worthy chat with — friendly and approachable.
words.example: Our affable neighbor greets everyone with a warm smile.

words.definition: a deliberate insult or act of disrespect
words.mnemonic: An affront is an insult thrown right in your FRONT — to your face.
words.example: Skipping her party was taken as a personal affront.

words.definition: to increase one's power, wealth, or reputation, often falsely
words.mnemonic: 'a-GRAND-ize': puff yourself up to look grand and important.
words.example: The dictator used constant propaganda to aggrandize his own heroic image.

words.definition: filled with shock or horror
words.mnemonic: Aghast = the face you make seeing A GHost — frozen in shock.
words.root: Old English gæstan (to frighten); related to 'ghost'
words.example: She stood aghast at the mess in the kitchen.

words.definition: to disturb, upset, or stir up
words.mnemonic: A washing machine on AGITATE mode shakes everything up — that's what agitating does to people too.
words.example: The loud construction noise agitated the sleeping baby.

words.definition: to worry or struggle painfully over a decision
words.mnemonic: Agonize = deciding is such AGONY that you turn it over and over in pain.
words.example: He agonized for days over which school to choose.

words.definition: to help or assist
words.mnemonic: First AID kits exist to help — aid : assist, the classic synonym pair from your notes.
words.example: Volunteers arrived quickly to aid the flood victims.

words.definition: a passage between rows of seats or shelves
words.mnemonic: Your own note nails it: an aisle is the 'road in the supermarket' — a mini street indoors.
words.example: The cereal is in aisle five, next to the snacks.

words.definition: cheerful, eager readiness or speed
words.mnemonic: 'I'LL ACT quickly!' — alacrity is that instant, happy YES with sneakers already on.
words.example: He accepted the pizza run with alacrity, keys already in hand.

words.definition: a small recessed section or nook within a larger room
words.mnemonic: An alcove is a cove carved into a wall where you can hide away.
words.example: She curled up to read in the cozy alcove by the window.

words.definition: to make a problem or pain less severe
words.mnemonic: An 'elevator' lifts the heavy weight of pain right off you.
words.example: A single aspirin can alleviate a pounding headache within minutes.

words.definition: highly attractive and tempting
words.mnemonic: A fishing LURE is alluring — it attracts the fish irresistibly toward it.
words.example: The alluring smell of fresh cookies pulled us into the bakery.

words.definition: a yearly book of facts, dates, and forecasts
words.mnemonic: The Farmer's ALmanac tells AL the farmer when to plant — a year's facts in one book.
words.example: The almanac predicted a snowy winter and listed every full moon.

words.definition: distant and uninvolved; emotionally cool and unfriendly
words.mnemonic: Standing 'a-roof' up high, far above and apart from everyone.
words.example: The new student stayed aloof, sitting alone and speaking to nobody.

words.definition: selflessly concerned with the welfare of others
words.mnemonic: ALL-TRUE-istic: truly ALL for others — keeps zero slices of the pizza for himself.
words.example: Her altruistic decision to donate the prize money surprised no one.

words.definition: a male graduate or former student of a school
words.mnemonic: An ALUMnus glows like ALUMinum foil at reunions — 'I graduated from here!'
words.example: A famous alumnus returned to give the graduation speech.

words.definition: to combine or unite separate things into one whole
words.mnemonic: Think 'a-mall-gamate': separate shops all merge into one big mall.
words.root: Latin amalgama 'alloy of mercury' — a mixed metal.
words.example: The two small banks amalgamated to form a single powerful institution.

words.definition: the mood or atmosphere of a place
words.mnemonic: Your root note works: AMBI = around — ambiance is the feeling wrapped all around you.
words.example: Candles gave the restaurant a cozy, romantic ambiance.

words.definition: having mixed, contradictory feelings about something
words.mnemonic: AMBI = both: holding two valentines with one heart — pulled both ways at once.
words.example: He felt ambivalent about the move: new adventures, but goodbye to friends.

words.definition: to make a bad situation better
words.mnemonic: Nurse AMELIA-rates: wherever Amelia goes, things get better.
words.example: Free tutoring helped ameliorate the school's slipping test scores.

words.definition: the act of making something better; improvement
words.mnemonic: a-MELIOR-ation: 'melior' sounds like 'MELLOWER' — things improve and get mellower.
words.example: The new park led to a clear amelioration of the neighborhood.

words.definition: pleasant and good-natured; likable
words.mnemonic: AMI = friend in French: AMY is everyone's amiable friend at lunch.
words.example: The amiable librarian never minds explaining the catalog twice.

words.definition: partial or total loss of memory
words.mnemonic: a- (no) + mnesia (memory) — same 'mne' as in mnemonic, but with it erased.
words.example: After the accident, amnesia erased his memory of that week.

words.definition: having no definite shape, form, or structure
words.mnemonic: 'a-morph-ous': can't morph into any shape, just a floppy blob.
words.example: The fog was an amorphous gray mass drifting over the silent harbor.

words.definition: more than enough; plentiful
words.mnemonic: AMPLE rhymes with SAMPLE — at Costco there are ample samples, more than enough for everyone.
words.example: We packed ample food for the three-day camping trip.

words.definition: something placed in the wrong historical time
words.mnemonic: A medieval knight checking his Apple Watch mid-joust — wrong century, buddy.
words.example: The stagecoach in the sci-fi film was a deliberate anachronism.

words.definition: a drug that relieves pain
words.mnemonic: AN-ALGESIC: 'an' = no + 'alges' = pain. No-pain medicine, like aspirin.
words.example: The dentist recommended an analgesic for the toothache.

words.definition: a person who rejects all government and authority
words.mnemonic: AN- (no) + ARCH (ruler): an anarchist wants NO ruler at all — zero government.
words.example: The anarchist argued that people need no rulers at all.

words.definition: a deep, settled dislike; strong aversion
words.mnemonic: ANTI + PATHY (feeling): your feelings run 100% AGAINST it, like cats versus bathtime.
words.example: His antipathy toward cilantro is legendary; he checks every menu twice.

words.definition: directly opposed or opposite in nature
words.mnemonic: ANTI-thetical: the anti of the thesis — cheating is antithetical to sportsmanship.
words.root: Greek anti- (against) + tithenai (to place) — placed against.
words.example: Lying is antithetical to everything the honor code stands for.

words.definition: lack of interest, feeling, or concern
words.mnemonic: A- (without) + pathy (feeling): apathy is shrugging 'whatever' at everything.
words.example: Voter apathy kept half the town home on election day.

words.definition: a short, wise saying expressing a general truth
words.mnemonic: 'A PHrase Of wISdoM' — aphorism, a bite-sized wise saying like 'haste makes waste.'
words.example: 'Actions speak louder than words' is a familiar aphorism.

words.definition: self-confidence and poise, especially under pressure
words.mnemonic: A 'plumb' line hangs perfectly steady and balanced, never wobbling.
words.example: She handled the hostile interview questions with remarkable aplomb.

words.definition: greatly shocked and horrified
words.mnemonic: Appalled = so shocked your face goes PALE — aPPALled has 'pall' (deathly white) inside.
words.example: The teacher was appalled by the cheating scandal.

words.definition: to attract or draw interest; also to make an earnest request
words.mnemonic: A ripe peeled orange has visual appeal that lures you in.
words.example: Bright colors and free samples appeal to hungry shoppers.

words.definition: to calm someone by giving in to demands
words.mnemonic: Offer a-PEAS: hand the screaming toddler peas (fine, cookies) to buy quiet.
words.example: She appeased her furious brother by surrendering the TV remote.

words.definition: anxious or fearful about what may happen
words.mnemonic: You boxed it yourself: FEARFUL = APPREHENSIVE — nervous about being APPREHENDED (caught) before it happens.
words.example: She felt apprehensive the night before her big audition.

words.definition: official approval, praise, or endorsement
words.mnemonic: Hidden inside is 'approve': a warm nod of approval.
words.example: The scientist's discovery met with the enthusiastic approbation of her peers.

words.definition: understood by few; mysterious or secret
words.mnemonic: Knowledge locked in an ARC (chest) only wizards have the key to.
words.example: The manual was full of arcane symbols only the engineers understood.

words.definition: intense passion or enthusiasm
words.mnemonic: Ardor sounds like 'harder' — passion is what makes you cheer harder and try harder.
words.example: He pursued astronomy with an ardor that kept him up all night.

words.definition: requiring great effort; very difficult and tiring
words.mnemonic: ARDuous is HARD-uous — so hard it takes everything you've got, like climbing a steep trail.
words.example: The hikers faced an arduous climb up the icy slope.

words.definition: clever trickery or cunning used to deceive
words.mnemonic: 'Art' turned to a 'fake' device: crafty artificial scheming.
words.example: He won her trust through flattery and cunning artifice.

words.definition: a person who gives up comforts for self-discipline
words.mnemonic: a-SET-ick: owns exactly one SET of clothes and finds luxury icky.
words.example: The ascetic slept on a wooden board and ate one meal daily.

words.definition: showing constant, careful effort; hardworking
words.mnemonic: as-SEAT-uous: glued to the seat, homework open, until every problem is done.
words.example: Her assiduous note-taking made her flashcards famous across the grade.

words.definition: to absorb and fully take in, as information or people
words.mnemonic: as-SIMIL-ate: make something SIMILar to the rest — take it in until it blends with the whole.
words.example: Immigrants often assimilate new customs while keeping old traditions.

words.definition: to help someone do something
words.mnemonic: In basketball an ASSIST is literally helping a teammate score — assist = aid.
words.example: A nurse assisted the doctor during the procedure.

words.definition: to soothe or ease pain, fear, or grief
words.mnemonic: a-SOOTHE-age: rub soothing balm on sore feelings until they stop stinging.
words.example: A quick call home assuaged his fears about the missing dog.

words.definition: great surprise or amazement
words.mnemonic: Your degree ladder: surprise → astonishment is surprise turned up to 10, jaw on the floor.
words.example: To everyone's astonishment, the rookie won the championship.

words.definition: extremely large in amount; enormous
words.mnemonic: Astronomical numbers are space-sized — distances between stars, or the price of that sneaker drop.
words.example: The repair bill was astronomical, far beyond our budget.

words.definition: sharply perceptive; clever at judging situations
words.mnemonic: An astute student is A-STUdenT with Extra sharpness — nothing gets past them.
words.example: Her astute observation solved the mystery before anyone else's.

words.definition: to waste away or weaken, especially from disuse
words.mnemonic: No 'trophy', no use: the neglected muscle shrinks and shrivels.
words.example: Without exercise, unused muscles will slowly atrophy and grow weak.

words.definition: to declare or provide evidence that something is true
words.mnemonic: At the TEST, a witness will attest — swear the answer is true.
words.example: Three neighbors attested that he was home all evening.

words.definition: recklessly bold or daring
words.mnemonic: oh-DARE-shus: the kid who DAREs to grab the principal's microphone at assembly.
words.example: Their audacious plan involved kayaking the rapids at midnight.

words.definition: to make larger; increase in size, amount, or degree
words.mnemonic: 'a-HUGE-ment': anything you augment gets huge and bigger.
words.example: He took a second job to augment his modest monthly income.

words.definition: showing signs of future success; favorable
words.mnemonic: Romans read bird flights for luck: au-SPIC-ious = 'bird-watching' for good omens. Good birds, good start.
words.root: Latin avis (bird) + specere (to look) — bird omens
words.example: Winning the first game was an auspicious start to the season.

words.definition: severely plain and simple; without comfort or decoration
words.mnemonic: Austere sounds like 'AWE, STEEL' — a bare steel room: no rug, no posters, strictly plain.
words.example: The monk's austere room held only a bed and desk.

words.definition: extreme greed for money or possessions
words.mnemonic: AVA hoards RICE she will never eat — mountains of it, just to have more.
words.example: Driven by avarice, the dragon slept atop gold it never spent.

words.definition: a wide street; also a way of approaching something
words.mnemonic: Your grouping holds: avenue is the outdoor big brother of the aisle — both are paths.
words.example: They explored every avenue before giving up the search.

words.definition: strongly opposed to; having a dislike for
words.mnemonic: a-VERSE against it: so turned off you'd write an angry VERSE about it.
words.example: He is so averse to mornings that he sets seven alarms.

words.definition: extremely eager and enthusiastic
words.mnemonic: An AVID reader devours books like an ADDict — avid is 'eager' with the intensity cranked up.
words.example: As an avid gamer, he saved every dollar for new releases.

words.definition: to completely confuse or puzzle
words.mnemonic: A baffling riddle makes your brain go 'BAFF!' — like a soft explosion of confusion.
words.example: The strange clue baffled even the best detectives.

words.definition: threatening harm; menacing and full of evil intent
words.mnemonic: A 'bale' full of pure evil: a glaring, hateful bundle.
words.example: The wolf fixed the lamb with a baleful, hungry stare.

words.definition: boring from overuse; unoriginal
words.mnemonic: Banal rhymes with canal: stale ideas drifting past like murky canal water.
words.example: The essay opened with the banal line 'since the dawn of time.'

words.definition: a cause of ruin, misery, or constant trouble
words.mnemonic: BANE the Batman villain exists to ruin Batman — he is literally Batman's bane.
words.example: Pop quizzes are the bane of every unprepared student's existence.

words.definition: to force someone to leave a place as punishment
words.mnemonic: Banish = BAN + vanISH: the king bans you and you vanish from the kingdom.
words.root: Old French banir (to proclaim an outlaw)
words.example: The king banished the traitor from the realm forever.

words.definition: an institution that keeps, lends, and manages money
words.mnemonic: Analogy anchor from your notes: bank : financial :: court : judicial — the place maps to its adjective.
words.example: She deposited her babysitting money at the local bank.

words.definition: a crude, uncivilized, or brutal person
words.mnemonic: A barbarian barges into the barber shop and smashes the chairs — no manners at all.
words.root: Greek barbaros 'foreign, babbling' — outsiders whose speech sounded like 'bar-bar'.
words.example: He ate with his hands like a barbarian at the formal dinner.

words.definition: a building where soldiers live
words.mnemonic: BARR-acks: rows of bunks behind army bars — soldiers' dorm.
words.example: The recruits marched back to the barracks after training.

words.definition: unable to produce plants or offspring; empty and lifeless
words.mnemonic: A BARREN field grows BARE-ly anything — bare land, zero crops.
words.example: Nothing grew in the barren desert except a few tough shrubs.

words.definition: a guiding light or signal fire, as from a lighthouse
words.mnemonic: A beacon is bright enough to 'beckon' lost ships toward shore.
words.example: The lighthouse beacon warned sailors away from the rocks.

words.definition: a scene of wild uproar and confusion; chaos
words.mnemonic: Imagine every BED in the house LAMmed against the walls — total chaos in the bedroom.
words.root: From 'Bethlehem' hospital in London, a famously chaotic asylum.
words.example: When the fire alarm rang, the cafeteria erupted into bedlam.

words.definition: to charm or trick, often deceptively
words.mnemonic: be-GUILE: the fox smiles sweetly while its GUILE steals your lunchbox.
words.example: The salesman beguiled tourists into buying maps of streets that don't exist.

words.definition: to give a false impression of; contradict
words.mnemonic: be-LIE: his calm face tells a flat LIE about the panic underneath.
words.example: Her steady voice belied the trembling hands hidden behind her back.

words.definition: eager to fight; warlike in attitude
words.mnemonic: BELL-icose: rings the war BELL at the slightest excuse — 'You looked at me?! WAR.'
words.example: The bellicose rooster attacked anything that entered the yard, including the mailman.

words.definition: hostile and aggressive; ready to argue or fight
words.mnemonic: BELLI (war) + GERent: carries a war around like luggage, unpacking fights everywhere.
words.example: The belligerent fan was escorted out after challenging the referee.

words.definition: gentle and harmless; kindly
words.mnemonic: BE NIGN = 'be nice' with a typo: the tumor doctors cheer about is the nice kind.
words.example: Despite its fangs, the snake turned out to be completely benign.

words.definition: to scold angrily and at length
words.mnemonic: be-RATE: screaming a zero-star RATING directly into someone's face.
words.example: The chef berated the waiter for ten minutes over one cold soup.

words.definition: deprived of or completely lacking something
words.mnemonic: 'Be-reft', as in robbed: you're left with nothing at all.
words.example: After the fire, the family was bereft of all their possessions.

words.definition: to beg or ask for something earnestly and urgently
words.mnemonic: 'Be-SEEK' desperately: hands out, seeking mercy on your knees.
words.example: The prisoners beseeched the king to grant them mercy.

words.definition: completely obvious, often offensively so
words.mnemonic: It's so obvious it's BLAring — blatant lies come with a built-in siren.
words.example: Copying his essay word for word was blatant cheating.

words.definition: carefree and cheerful, sometimes carelessly so
words.mnemonic: BLITHE = blissfully light: skipping and humming through a thunderstorm, umbrella forgotten.
words.example: With blithe unconcern, she strolled into the exam without a pencil.

words.definition: a big, careless mistake
words.mnemonic: Your own note: blunder is err with 'big' on top — a BIG err. B for Big Blunder.
words.example: Forgetting the tickets at home was a costly blunder.

words.definition: noisy, energetic, and cheerfully rowdy
words.mnemonic: A 'boy-sterous' boy causing a loud, wild, stomping racket.
words.example: The boisterous crowd cheered and stamped throughout the entire game.

words.definition: to support, strengthen, or prop up
words.mnemonic: A BOLSTER is literally a support pillow — stuff one under anything sagging, including confidence.
words.example: Two quick goals bolstered the team's confidence before halftime.

words.definition: using inflated, showy language with little meaning
words.mnemonic: BOMB-bastic: a speech that's all fireworks and explosions with nothing actually said.
words.example: His bombastic victory speech lasted twenty minutes and said absolutely nothing.

words.definition: rude, clumsy, and ill-mannered
words.mnemonic: BOOR sounds like BOAR: a wild pig at the dinner table, elbows in the mashed potatoes.
words.example: His boorish habit of talking through movies emptied the couch around him.

words.definition: given or existing in generous amounts; plentiful
words.mnemonic: A BOUNTy so big it's bounteous — treasure overflowing the chest.
words.example: The farmers celebrated a bounteous harvest that autumn.

words.definition: to wave or flourish something, often a weapon, threateningly
words.mnemonic: Wave a burning 'brand' (torch) around wildly to scare foes.
words.example: The pirate leapt aboard, brandishing a gleaming cutlass over his head.

words.definition: a bold but often false or exaggerated show of courage
words.mnemonic: A fake, showy 'bravo!' swagger meant to hide real fear.
words.example: His loud bravado hid the fear he felt facing the bully.

words.definition: a rough, noisy fight, usually among several people
words.mnemonic: B-R-AWL: everyone's fists and jaws going 'aww!' — a whole crowd fighting.
words.example: A brawl broke out in the stands after the disputed goal.

words.definition: bold and without shame
words.mnemonic: Brazen = made of brass: a face of brass can't blush, so it feels no shame.
words.root: Old English braes, 'brass' — bold as brass.
words.example: In a brazen move, the thief robbed the bank next to the police station.

words.definition: shortness in speech or writing; briefness
words.mnemonic: BREV = brief: 'brb' is brevity in action — three letters, message delivered.
words.example: The general's orders were famous for their brevity: 'Hold the bridge.'

words.definition: to bring up a topic for discussion for the first time
words.mnemonic: Like tapping a keg, you pierce open a touchy subject.
words.example: She hesitated before broaching the delicate subject of his unpaid debt.

words.definition: abrupt and blunt to the point of rudeness
words.mnemonic: BRUSQUE sounds like BRUSH: he BRUSHes you off in three words and walks away.
words.example: Her brusque 'Fine. Next question.' ended the interview early.

words.definition: a brief official announcement or news report
words.mnemonic: News fired fast as a BULLET-in: a quick shot of information.
words.example: The school posted a bulletin about the schedule change.

words.definition: a defensive wall; anything that protects against attack
words.mnemonic: A BULL doing wall WORK — a bull-strong wall that guards the castle.
words.example: The old sea wall served as a bulwark against the waves.

words.definition: able to float; cheerful and lighthearted
words.mnemonic: A BUOY bobbing on the water — buoyant things (and moods) stay up.
words.example: Despite the loss, she stayed buoyant and joked with her teammates.

words.definition: to grow or expand rapidly; flourish
words.mnemonic: BURGEONing BURGER joints: the franchise sprouts on every corner seemingly overnight.
words.example: The town's population burgeoned after the new rail line opened.

words.definition: a sculpture of a person's head, shoulders, and chest
words.mnemonic: A marble bust stops at the chest — just the top 'BUST' half of the body.
words.example: A marble bust of the philosopher stood in the museum hall.

words.definition: a harsh, unpleasant mix of loud sounds
words.mnemonic: Your root note, sharpened: caco = bad + phon = sound — a phone full of bad sound.
words.example: A cacophony of horns filled the gridlocked street.

words.definition: coax someone with flattery or gentle nagging
words.mnemonic: 'C'mon, JOE' — you keep jollying Joe until he finally lends you his bike.
words.example: She cajoled her brother into doing her chores with promises of candy.

words.definition: a disaster causing great damage or distress
words.mnemonic: CALAMity: a clam the size of a city falls from the sky — total disaster.
words.example: The flood was a calamity that left the town in ruins.

words.definition: emotionally hardened; showing no sympathy
words.mnemonic: CALLOUS is a callus — skin, and heart, grown thick and hard.
words.example: The callous landlord evicted the family without a shred of pity.

words.definition: honest, open frankness
words.mnemonic: CAN-DOOR: an open door hides nothing — the truth walks straight through.
words.root: candere = to shine, glow (Latin), as in candle
words.example: With surprising candor, he admitted he hadn't read the book.

words.definition: able to hold much; roomy and spacious
words.mnemonic: CAPACIOUS hides 'capacity' — a capacious bag has enormous capacity.
words.example: Her capacious tote swallowed books, snacks, and an umbrella easily.

words.definition: stop resisting; surrender
words.mnemonic: bow your CAPut (head) and hand over your CAPital — white flag up.
words.example: After a week of protests, the principal capitulated and restored recess.

words.definition: changing mood or behavior suddenly and unpredictably
words.mnemonic: Capri weather: sunny, then storming, then sunny — capricious skies keep changing.
words.example: Her capricious taste meant a new favorite band every week.

words.definition: to complain constantly about small things
words.mnemonic: A carp fish with its mouth always opening and closing — nag, nag, nag.
words.example: He carped about the hotel's pillows the entire trip.

words.definition: flowing or falling downward abundantly, like a waterfall
words.mnemonic: A cascade of water tumbles down; cascading light spills the same way.
words.example: Cascading sunlight poured through the tall stained-glass windows.

words.definition: scold or criticize severely
words.mnemonic: CAST out the GATE while the coach screams at you the whole way.
words.example: The editor castigated the reporter for inventing quotes.

words.definition: sharply sarcastic or biting; corrosive like acid
words.mnemonic: CAUSTIC sounds like 'cost-stick' — his caustic jab scorched the poor stick like acid.
words.example: Her caustic reply left the overconfident debater silent and stinging.

words.definition: to raise trivial, petty objections
words.mnemonic: CAVIL sounds like 'cavity' — a nagging little hole you keep poking at.
words.example: He would cavil over every comma instead of praising the essay.

words.definition: criticize harshly and officially
words.mnemonic: a CENSOR with a red pen — but censure stamps BLAME, not blackouts.
words.example: The senator was censured for misusing campaign funds.

words.definition: relating to the brain; intellectual rather than emotional
words.mnemonic: CEREBRum = brain — cerebral movies make your cerebrum sweat, not your heart.
words.example: Chess is a cerebral game that rewards deep thinking.

words.definition: a lizard that changes color; a person who changes to fit surroundings
words.mnemonic: Your analogy is the hook: chameleon changes COLOR as a nomad changes LOCATION.
words.example: He's a social chameleon, blending into any group he joins.

words.definition: a deep, wide crack or gap in the earth
words.mnemonic: The 'ch' is silent like the drop is deep — you gasp ('chasm!') looking into it.
words.example: The rope bridge swayed over a dizzying chasm.

words.definition: to scold or punish severely
words.mnemonic: CHASe + TIES: mom chases you with your bad ties, scolding the whole way.
words.example: The coach chastised the players for skipping practice.

words.definition: unwilling to spend money; stingy
words.mnemonic: Your degree ladder: frugal is careful, cheap is extreme — he 'cheeps' like a bird rather than pay.
words.example: He's so cheap he reuses birthday cards from last year.

words.definition: a large sturdy box for storing things
words.mnemonic: Treasure chest: on the SSAT, a chest holds clothes or gold, not just ribs.
words.example: She keeps old letters in a wooden chest under her bed.

words.definition: clever trickery or deception
words.mnemonic: a CHICANE is a sneaky twist in a racetrack — chicanery is sneaky twists in a deal.
words.example: The contract's fine print was pure chicanery.

words.definition: to scold mildly
words.mnemonic: You get chided for playing hide and seek in class — 'CHild, don't hIDE!'
words.example: Grandma chided us gently for tracking mud inside.

words.definition: a vertical structure that carries smoke out of a building
words.mnemonic: Your analogy is the hook: chimney releases smoke as a drain releases liquid.
words.example: Smoke curled from the cottage chimney all winter.

words.definition: courteous, honorable behavior, especially like a knight's
words.mnemonic: CHeVALry: cheval is French for horse — the polite code of knights on horseback.
words.example: In an act of chivalry, he offered the stranger his umbrella.

words.definition: rude and bad-tempered
words.mnemonic: a grumpy CHURL slams the door on the carolers — 'bah!'
words.example: It seemed churlish to refuse such a kind invitation.

words.definition: careful; weighing everything before acting
words.mnemonic: circum + spect: LOOK AROUND the corner twice before you cross.
words.example: Be circumspect about what you post online.

words.definition: to quote or mention as evidence or support
words.mnemonic: Cite your SITE: name the website you got it from, or it's stealing.
words.example: Remember to cite the article you quoted in your essay.

words.definition: a person who is not in the military or police
words.mnemonic: Your analogy: civilian is defined by what it's NOT — not a soldier, just CIVIl life.
words.example: After twenty years in the army, he returned to civilian life.

words.definition: done in secret, especially something forbidden
words.mnemonic: A secret CLAN meeting DESTINed to stay hidden — clandestine plans.
words.example: The spies arranged a clandestine meeting in the parking garage.

words.definition: the quality of being clear and easy to understand
words.mnemonic: Your analogy: illuminating something RESULTS in clarity — light makes things CLEAR.
words.example: She explained the rules with such clarity that no one had questions.

words.definition: mercy shown to an offender
words.mnemonic: clement weather is mild — Judge CLEMENT goes mild on you too.
words.example: The governor granted clemency to the elderly prisoner.

words.definition: an overused phrase or idea that has lost originality
words.mnemonic: Click-SHAY: a phrase clicked copy-paste so many times it's worn out.
words.root: French printers' term for a reusable printing plate.
words.example: The movie's 'happily ever after' ending felt like a cliche.

words.definition: to come together and merge into one
words.mnemonic: COALESCE sounds like 'coal-ess' — scattered coals coalesce into one blazing lump.
words.example: Small protests coalesced into a single massive movement overnight.

words.definition: rough in texture; crude or vulgar in manner
words.mnemonic: Coarse sounds like 'course' — but this one is a rough sandpaper course, 粗糙.
words.example: The coarse fabric of the old blanket scratched her skin.

words.definition: to force someone by threats or pressure
words.mnemonic: COERCE is 'co + force' — force pressed onto you together.
words.example: Bullies tried to coerce him into handing over his lunch money.

words.definition: clear, logical, and convincing
words.mnemonic: an argument whose COGs mesh so perfectly you're driven to nod along.
words.example: Her cogent essay changed the judges' minds.

words.definition: awareness or knowledge of something
words.mnemonic: reCOGNIZance: if you can recognize it, you have cognizance of it.
words.root: Latin cognoscere 'to know' (same root as recognize).
words.example: She acted in full cognizance of the risks involved.

words.definition: to honor and remember an important person or event
words.mnemonic: Your split works: com + MEMOR — everyone remembering together, like at a memorial.
words.example: The statue commemorates the soldiers who defended the city.

words.definition: to begin or start
words.mnemonic: Graduation is 'commencement' because it BEGINS adult life — commence = start.
words.example: The ceremony will commence at noon sharp.

words.definition: to praise formally or officially
words.mnemonic: Your pairing is the hook: commEND vs condEMN — one letter apart, opposite meanings.
words.example: The mayor commended the firefighters for their bravery.

words.definition: smugly satisfied and blind to danger
words.mnemonic: so pleased with your PLACE that you nap on the trophy.
words.example: One good test score made him complacent, and his grades slipped.

words.definition: a statement expressing dissatisfaction or annoyance
words.mnemonic: Your analogy: complaint : dissatisfaction :: pact : agreement — the word states the feeling.
words.example: The waiter apologized after hearing her complaint about the soup.

words.definition: calm control of one's emotions
words.mnemonic: Stay COMPOSed under pressure — composure is keeping your cool posed like a statue.
words.example: She kept her composure even when the judges challenged her.

words.definition: to calm someone's anger; to make peace
words.mnemonic: reCONCILe hides inside: conciliate is what you do to make angry sides reconcile.
words.example: He offered a sincere apology to conciliate his furious neighbor.

words.definition: intended to calm anger and restore goodwill; appeasing
words.mnemonic: Con-SILLY-atory: after the fight, he made a silly peace offering of cookies to reconcile.
words.root: Latin conciliare, 'to bring together' — same root as reconcile.
words.example: After the argument, she offered a conciliatory apology and a handshake.

words.definition: to criticize or declare something strongly wrong
words.mnemonic: conDEMN has 'damn' inside — to condemn is to publicly damn something.
words.example: World leaders condemned the attack on the hospital.

words.definition: acting superior; talking down to others
words.mnemonic: Con-DESCEND-ing: he thinks he must climb DOWN from his high level just to talk to you.
words.example: Her condescending tone made the new students feel stupid.

words.definition: making a result likely; helpful or favorable
words.mnemonic: A wire that CONDUCts helps electricity flow — conducive conditions help results flow.
words.example: A quiet room is conducive to studying.

words.definition: to confuse or bewilder completely
words.mnemonic: CONfusion FOUND: you found nothing but confusion — the puzzle confounded you.
words.example: The magician's trick confounded even the skeptics.

words.definition: a group of people gathered together, especially for worship
words.mnemonic: con (together) + greg (flock): the flock gathers — like Congress, a big gathering.
words.example: The congregation rose to sing the opening hymn.

words.definition: the careful protection and preservation of resources or nature
words.mnemonic: Conservation means you conserve and protect what you have.
words.example: Wildlife conservation protects endangered species from extinction.

words.definition: sudden alarm, dismay, or anxious confusion
words.mnemonic: CONSTERNATION — picture a 'stern nation' of frowning faces gripped by dismay.
words.example: To the crew's consternation, the engine sputtered and died mid-flight.

words.definition: to make up or form a whole
words.mnemonic: The Constitution is what the states CONSTITUTE — the parts that make up the whole country.
words.example: Nine players constitute a baseball team's starting lineup.

words.definition: showing supreme, complete skill; perfect and accomplished
words.mnemonic: A consummate chef has summited the peak of cooking skill.
words.example: The consummate violinist played the difficult piece flawlessly.

words.definition: causing argument; prone to quarreling
words.mnemonic: CONTENTIOUS holds 'contend' — a contentious person contends over everything.
words.example: The contentious debate over uniforms split the entire student council.

words.definition: deeply sorry for wrongdoing
words.mnemonic: the CON TRIes to cRy in court: 'I'm so, so sorry, Your Honor.'
words.root: conterere = to crush (Latin) — crushed by guilt
words.example: His contrite apology convinced the teacher to lower the penalty.

words.definition: a confusing problem or riddle with no easy answer
words.mnemonic: CONUNDRUM sounds like 'con + drum' — a con man drumming up a baffling riddle.
words.example: Choosing between two great colleges became a genuine conundrum.

words.definition: friendly, lively, and enjoyable; sociable
words.mnemonic: CONVIVIAL is 'con + vive (alive)' — the whole feast feeling alive together.
words.example: The convivial dinner rang with laughter well past midnight.

words.definition: in large amounts; plentiful
words.mnemonic: the jammed COPier spews copies until they bury the whole desk.
words.example: She took copious notes during every lecture.

words.definition: warm and friendly
words.mnemonic: Cor = heart in Latin — a cordial welcome comes straight from the heart.
words.example: Despite their rivalry, the two coaches exchanged a cordial handshake.

words.definition: very fat; bulky in body
words.mnemonic: CORPULENT is 'corpus (body) + lent' — the body was lent far too much flesh.
words.example: The corpulent king could barely squeeze into his golden throne.

words.definition: confirm or support with evidence
words.mnemonic: a CO-witness makes your story ROBUST — two voices, one alibi.
words.example: Security footage corroborated the witness's account.

words.definition: fake and made to deceive
words.mnemonic: Made at a secret COUNTER to FIT in with the real thing — a counterfeit bill.
words.root: Old French contrefaire (to make in imitation)
words.example: The cashier spotted the counterfeit twenty-dollar bill immediately.

words.definition: secret or hidden
words.mnemonic: COVERt = COVERed up — a covert mission stays under cover.
words.example: The spies planned a covert operation behind enemy lines.

words.definition: secretly; in a hidden way
words.mnemonic: Acting COVERtly means staying under COVER while you do it.
words.example: She covertly slipped the note into his backpack.

words.definition: to strongly desire something that belongs to someone else
words.mnemonic: Covet has no R — you'd Rather have your neighbor's stuff than your own.
words.root: Latin cupere (to desire), same family as Cupid
words.example: He coveted his classmate's brand-new gaming laptop.

words.definition: greedily eager for what others have
words.mnemonic: A covetous person covets everything — greedy eyes locked on other people's stuff.
words.example: The dragon cast a covetous glance at the knight's gold.

words.definition: shamefully cowardly; lacking any courage
words.mnemonic: A CRAVEN crow CAVES in and flies away from a tiny scarecrow.
words.example: His craven retreat left his teammates to face the blame alone.

words.definition: to give recognition or acknowledgment for something
words.mnemonic: When you cite a source, you give it credit — cite : credit, synonyms.
words.example: The scientist credited her mentor for the breakthrough idea.

words.definition: too ready to believe; easily fooled
words.mnemonic: gives CREDit to every chain email — 'a prince needs my allowance!'
words.example: Credulous shoppers fell for the miracle diet ad.

words.definition: sad and disappointed; dejected
words.mnemonic: CRESTFALLEN — a rooster's crest fallen flat after losing the fight.
words.example: Crestfallen, she stared at the rejection letter in silence.

words.definition: one who judges, evaluates, or finds fault with others' work
words.mnemonic: A critic is critical, quick to point out every flaw.
words.example: The film critic praised the acting but panned the plot.

words.definition: mysterious and hard to understand; obscure
words.mnemonic: CRYPTIC lives in a 'crypt' — dark, hidden messages you must decode.
words.example: His cryptic note read only 'midnight, the third door.'

words.definition: to pick out or select from a group
words.mnemonic: Cull sounds like 'call' — the coach CALLS out the players he picks.
words.example: The editor culled the best essays from hundreds of submissions.

words.definition: the highest point or final climax of something
words.mnemonic: A CLIMb CULMINates at the summit — culmination is the peak.
words.example: Winning the championship was the culmination of years of practice.

words.definition: deserving blame
words.mnemonic: the CULPrit mutters 'mea CULPA' — my fault, my fault.
words.example: The company was culpable for ignoring the safety reports.

words.definition: to grow crops; to develop or nurture something
words.mnemonic: You cultivate a plant AND a friendship — both need watering to grow.
words.example: She cultivated tomatoes in the garden and patience in herself.

words.definition: a bad-tempered, grumpy person
words.mnemonic: Picture grumpy Mr. Hutch, the classic CURmudgeon, growling 'grrr' (CUR = growling dog) at kids on his lawn.
words.example: The old curmudgeon yelled at anyone who stepped on his grass.

words.definition: quick and not thorough
words.mnemonic: your CURSOR skims down the page — scrolled, not actually read.
words.example: A cursory glance at the directions got us hopelessly lost.

words.definition: to cut short or reduce
words.mnemonic: CurTAIL = cut the TAIL off — chop it shorter.
words.example: Rain curtailed the picnic after only an hour.

words.definition: fearless and determined; not discouraged
words.mnemonic: DAUNTLESS is 'without daunt' — nothing can daunt this hero.
words.example: The dauntless firefighter charged into the collapsing building.

words.definition: a scarcity or lack of something
words.mnemonic: scarce things get DEAR (pricey) — a dearth of eggs, a dear price.
words.example: The team suffered from a dearth of experienced players.

words.definition: a sudden, complete, embarrassing failure
words.mnemonic: the buckle (BACLE) snaps and the whole parade float collapses mid-route.
words.root: débâcler = to break up, as river ice (French)
words.example: The school play became a debacle when the set fell over.

words.definition: to lower in quality, value, or dignity
words.mnemonic: DEBASE is 'de + base' — drag something down toward the base.
words.example: Flooding the market with fakes debased the artist's reputation.

words.definition: dishonesty; the act of misleading someone
words.mnemonic: Deceit is why there's 'no trust' — a con artist's whole toolkit.
words.example: His deceit was exposed when the forged signature was found.

words.definition: to slope or sink downward; to decrease or politely refuse
words.mnemonic: Something that keeps declining ends up flat — decline results in horizontal.
words.example: Ticket sales declined sharply after the star player left.

words.definition: proper, polite behavior; good manners
words.mnemonic: DECORUM is 'decor + room' — a well-decorated room demands polished manners.
words.example: Courtroom decorum requires everyone to rise for the judge.

words.definition: an official order issued by an authority
words.mnemonic: Decree = edict — the king deCREEs it and the whole kingdom obeys.
words.example: The king issued a decree banning fireworks in the city.

words.definition: to openly condemn or criticize strongly
words.mnemonic: DECRY is 'de + cry' — cry out loudly against something.
words.example: Activists decried the new law as unfair to students.

words.definition: an action; also a legal document proving property ownership
words.mnemonic: A deed represents property the way an action represents you — 'good deeds'.
words.example: The deed to the farmhouse sat in a locked drawer.

words.definition: to consider or judge something to be a certain way
words.mnemonic: DEEM rhymes with 'seem' — how something seems to you is how you deem it.
words.example: The referee deemed the field too muddy for play.

words.definition: polite respect for another's wishes or judgment
words.mnemonic: you DEFER to grandma — she picks the movie, every single time.
words.example: In deference to the coach, no one questioned the play call.

words.definition: skillful and quick, especially with the hands
words.mnemonic: Deft fingers are DEFinitely not clumsy — quick, sure, skillful.
words.example: With a deft flick, the chef flipped the pancake perfectly.

words.definition: harmful, often in a slow or hidden way
words.mnemonic: junk food hits DELETE on your health, one cell at a time.
words.example: Skipping sleep has deleterious effects on memory.

words.definition: a false belief held despite evidence against it
words.mnemonic: Delusion and illusion rhyme for a reason — both fool your mind.
words.example: He clung to the delusion that he could pass without studying.

words.definition: quiet, modest, and reserved
words.mnemonic: deMURE rhymes with pure — eyes down, hands folded, barely a whisper.
words.example: The demure student surprised everyone with a fierce debate performance.

words.definition: criticize unfairly; belittle
words.mnemonic: blacken (NIGR) someone's good name with a tar brush.
words.example: He denigrated her project just to make his own look better.

words.definition: to signify or be a sign of
words.mnemonic: De-NOTE: a note that tells you what something means — the red light denotes 'stop'.
words.example: A skull on the label denotes poison.

words.definition: to strongly disapprove of; to lament
words.mnemonic: DEPLORE sounds like 'deep + lore' — you deeply mourn a sorry state.
words.example: Teachers deplore the habit of copying answers off classmates.

words.definition: to remove from power or office
words.mnemonic: De-POSE: the king loses his throne pose — yanked off the seat, unseated.
words.root: Latin de- (down) + ponere (to place) — to put down from power.
words.example: The rebels deposed the dictator after twenty years of rule.

words.definition: to express disapproval of; to belittle
words.mnemonic: DEPRECate DEPRECiates someone — talks their value down like a used car's price.
words.example: Critics deprecated the film as lazy and unoriginal.

words.definition: mock cruelly; ridicule
words.mnemonic: DE-RIDE: laugh someone right off their bike.
words.root: ridere = to laugh (Latin), as in ridiculous
words.example: Critics derided the sequel as a lazy copy.

words.definition: a dry, barren region with little rain or life
words.mnemonic: Desert vs oasis — the empty sand vs the one green patch in it.
words.example: Nothing but cactus grew in the vast desert.

words.definition: empty, abandoned, and bleak
words.mnemonic: DESOLate ≈ deserted and ISOLated — an empty ghost town with tumbleweeds.
words.example: The desolate mining town had been empty for decades.

words.definition: extremely hopeless and discouraged
words.mnemonic: So hopeless you de-SPOND — you stop responding to anything at all.
words.example: After the third rejection letter, she grew despondent.

words.definition: a ruler with total, often cruel, power
words.mnemonic: the DESK-POT: bangs the desk, and every pot in the land is his.
words.example: The despot jailed anyone who dared to mock him.

words.definition: extremely poor; lacking basic necessities
words.mnemonic: DEStitute: your savings got DEStroyed — left standing like a statue with empty pockets.
words.example: The flood left hundreds of families destitute.

words.definition: aimless; jumping randomly between things
words.mnemonic: a circus rider leaping from horse to horse, never staying on one.
words.example: They made desultory small talk while waiting for the bus.

words.definition: something that discourages an action
words.mnemonic: A deTERrent makes you TERrified to try — the exact opposite of an incentive.
words.example: The guard dog was an effective deterrent against burglars.

words.definition: to hate intensely
words.mnemonic: You DEtest a TEST — the thing everyone loves to hate.
words.example: She detests the smell of wet gym socks.

words.definition: sneaky and dishonest; cunning
words.mnemonic: DEVIOUS is 'de + via (road)' — someone off the straight road, taking sneaky detours.
words.example: Through devious tricks, he cheated his partner out of profits.

words.definition: deeply religious; sincerely committed
words.mnemonic: Devout = DEVOTed to your faith, inside and out.
words.example: The devout pilgrim prayed at every shrine along the road.

words.definition: the identification of an illness or problem
words.mnemonic: Physician : diagnosis — the doctor's job is to name what's wrong.
words.example: The doctor's diagnosis was a simple ear infection.

words.definition: a bitter, lengthy verbal attack
words.mnemonic: an angry chief yelling at the whole TRIBE for an hour straight.
words.example: The coach launched into a diatribe about lazy defense.

words.definition: meant to teach; preachy about it
words.mnemonic: the uncle who turns every dinner into a TED talk.
words.example: The film was so didactic it felt like homework.

words.definition: shy from lack of self-confidence
words.mnemonic: conFIDent minus the trust (FID) — the exact opposite of confident.
words.example: The diffident freshman barely raised her hand all semester.

words.definition: falling apart from age or neglect
words.mnemonic: Your 'broken & old' nails it: a DILAPIDATED barn — boards sagging, roof caving in.
words.example: They explored the dilapidated farmhouse with its collapsed porch.

words.definition: slow; meant to cause delay
words.mnemonic: the 'later-y' kid: 'I'll do it laaater' — dilatory = delay-tory.
words.example: His dilatory replies stalled the group project for weeks.

words.definition: a dabbler who never commits seriously
words.mnemonic: violin Monday, painting Tuesday, quitting Wednesday — delight without discipline.
words.example: A true chef, not a dilettante, practices one dish a hundred times.

words.definition: to weaken by adding water or another substance
words.mnemonic: Dilute the juice with water and its flavor — and its power — gets weaker.
words.example: She diluted the strong lemonade with a cup of water.

words.definition: extremely small
words.mnemonic: diMINUtive has MINI hiding inside — a mini-me.
words.example: The diminutive gymnast outscored athletes twice her size.

words.definition: skill in handling people or negotiations tactfully
words.mnemonic: A DIPLOMAT never says 'your idea is dumb' — that careful phrasing is DIPLOMACY.
words.example: With great diplomacy, she calmed both angry teammates.

words.definition: disagreement and conflict between people
words.mnemonic: Dis- (apart) + cord (heart) — hearts pulled apart, everyone quarreling.
words.example: Constant discord over chores divided the roommates.

words.definition: scornful contempt; regarding as unworthy
words.mnemonic: DISDAIN is 'dis + deign' — too proud to deign, so you look down in scorn.
words.example: She looked at the cheap imitation with obvious disdain.

words.definition: talk down; treat as unimportant
words.mnemonic: DIS + PAR: rate someone below PAR — trash-talk their whole game.
words.example: Don't disparage the effort just because the result was clumsy.

words.definition: fundamentally different; too distinct to compare
words.mnemonic: DIS-PARATE = things kept sePARATE because they just don't match.
words.example: The club united students with disparate interests, from chess to skateboarding.

words.definition: the scattering of things in different directions
words.mnemonic: Scattering results in dispersal, just as illuminating results in clarity.
words.example: Wind aids the dispersal of dandelion seeds across the field.

words.definition: unhappiness or displeasure with something
words.mnemonic: Dissatisfaction : complaint — the feeling and the words it comes out as.
words.example: Customers voiced their dissatisfaction with the slow service.

words.definition: spread widely, especially information
words.mnemonic: fling SEEDs (SEMIN) of news into the wind — they sprout everywhere.
words.example: The club disseminated flyers across every hallway.

words.definition: harsh, clashing sound or disagreement
words.mnemonic: DISSONANCE is 'dis + sonance (sound)' — sounds that dis each other, clashing.
words.example: The choir's dissonance made the audience wince in discomfort.

words.definition: causing disagreement or division between people
words.mnemonic: DiVISive DIVIDES the room — the opposite of unity.
words.example: The divisive new rule split the team into two camps.

words.definition: to reveal secret or private information
words.mnemonic: Di = reveal, vul = veil — divulge is lifting the veil off a secret.
words.root: Latin divulgare (to spread among the people)
words.example: He refused to divulge the location of the surprise party.

words.definition: easily managed; obedient and compliant
words.mnemonic: DOCile like DOC the dog — sits, stays, and obeys every command.
words.example: The docile pony let the toddler pat its nose.

words.definition: stating opinions as unquestionable facts
words.mnemonic: a DOGmatic dog barks one rule at everyone: MY way. Woof.
words.example: He's too dogmatic to consider anyone else's method.

words.definition: a small wheeled platform for moving heavy objects
words.mnemonic: Even a giant fridge rides a little 'Dolly' — the wheeled cart that carries the heavy stuff.
words.example: The movers wheeled the refrigerator out on a dolly.

words.definition: gloomy, stern, and unsmiling
words.mnemonic: rhymes with SOUR — a sour-faced man glowering in the rain.
words.example: The dour librarian never once cracked a smile.

words.definition: to drench with liquid; to extinguish
words.mnemonic: Douse = dump a whole DOSE of water — drench the campfire out.
words.example: The firefighters doused the flames before they spread.

words.definition: to draw liquid off; to empty or deplete gradually
words.mnemonic: Chimney : smoke as drain : liquid — each releases its own thing.
words.example: She drained the pasta and set the pot aside.

words.definition: hard, dull, tiresome, and often menial work
words.mnemonic: The daily drudgery is a dreary drag of endless chores.
words.example: Washing dishes for hundreds felt like endless drudgery.

words.definition: doubtful, uncertain, or questionable
words.mnemonic: DUBIOUS sounds like 'do-be-us?' — you keep doubting whether it should be.
words.example: His excuse for the missing homework sounded highly dubious.

words.definition: deceitful; deliberately dishonest in a two-faced way
words.mnemonic: DUPLI = double — a duplicitous person keeps two faces, one for lying.
words.example: The duplicitous salesman praised the car while hiding its broken engine.

words.definition: deceitfulness; two-faced behavior
words.mnemonic: DUPLIcate yourself: one face for your friend, another behind her back.
words.example: Her duplicity surfaced when both friends compared her stories.

words.definition: to keep thinking or talking about something, especially something negative
words.mnemonic: To dwell is to live somewhere — dwelling on a mistake means moving in and living inside it.
words.example: Don't dwell on one wrong answer; focus on the next question.

words.definition: full of energy and activity; constantly changing
words.mnemonic: Dynamite is dynamic — packed with explosive energy.
words.example: Her dynamic personality made every class discussion exciting.

words.definition: a stand that holds an artist's canvas while painting
words.mnemonic: Painting is EASy when your canvas rests on an easel.
words.example: The artist set her canvas on the easel and began to paint.

words.definition: overflowing with excitement and enthusiasm
words.mnemonic: a pot that E-BOILS over — joy bubbling past the lid
words.example: The ebullient fans stormed the court after the buzzer-beater.

words.definition: unconventional and slightly strange; odd in behavior
words.mnemonic: EC-CENTRIC = off-center — an eccentric person is off-center from normal.
words.example: The eccentric inventor wore a raincoat indoors on sunny days.

words.definition: an odd or unconventional habit or quality
words.mnemonic: You wrote it 20 times — that itself was a charming eccentricity.
words.example: Collecting old doorknobs was just one of his many eccentricities.

words.definition: drawing from many different sources or styles
words.mnemonic: an eclectic playlist: opera, rap, polka — E-CLICKED from everywhere
words.example: Her eclectic bookshelf holds comics, physics texts, and medieval poetry.

words.definition: an official order or command issued by an authority
words.mnemonic: E-DICT: the emperor DICTates it and everyone must obey.
words.example: The king's edict banned all markets from opening on festival days.

words.definition: to erase, rub out, or wipe away completely
words.mnemonic: ef-FACE: rub out a penciled face until it's totally gone.
words.example: Time had effaced the inscription carved into the ancient stone.

words.definition: shameless boldness; insulting nerve
words.mnemonic: shoving your bare forehead in someone's FRONT — zero shame
words.example: He had the effrontery to demand a refund for food he'd eaten.

words.definition: expressing feelings in an unrestrained, gushing way
words.mnemonic: 'effusive' sounds like a blown FUSE — emotions burst out and pour everywhere.
words.example: Her effusive thanks embarrassed the shy volunteer, who preferred quiet praise.

words.definition: shockingly bad; standing out for awfulness
words.mnemonic: EGG-REACH-US: a blunder so bad the whole crowd throws eggs
words.root: e + grex = out of the flock (Latin)
words.example: Leaving the goalie unguarded was an egregious mistake.

words.definition: extremely happy and excited
words.mnemonic: E-LATED: so happy you feel LIFTED up — elevated with joy.
words.example: She was elated when the acceptance letter finally arrived.

words.definition: explain clearly; shed light on
words.mnemonic: flip on the LUCID light switch inside a confusing idea
words.example: The diagram elucidated how the engine actually works.

words.definition: hard to catch, find, or pin down
words.mnemonic: Elusive and evasive are twins — both slip away when you reach for them.
words.example: The elusive fox vanished before the hunters could even spot it.

words.definition: abnormally thin and weak from hunger or illness
words.mnemonic: 'e-MASH-ee-ated': so thin you could be mashed down to nothing.
words.example: The rescued dog was emaciated, its ribs showing through thin fur.

words.definition: to set free from control or slavery
words.mnemonic: The Emancipation Proclamation freed the slaves — emancipate = set free.
words.root: Latin e 'out of' + manus 'hand' — taken out of another's hand
words.example: The new law emancipated thousands of workers from unfair contracts.

words.definition: an official ban on trade with a country
words.mnemonic: EMBARGO bars cargo — no ships in, no ships out.
words.example: The embargo stopped all oil shipments between the two nations.

words.definition: to decorate; to make more interesting by adding details
words.mnemonic: Embellish a story the way you decorate a cake — extra frosting on top.
words.example: He embellished the fishing story until the minnow became a shark.

words.definition: to correct errors in a written text
words.mnemonic: E-MEND: you MEND a text by fixing its errors.
words.example: The editor emended the manuscript before sending it to print.

words.definition: to leave one's own country to live elsewhere
words.mnemonic: E for Exit: you Emigrate out of a country, Immigrate Into one.
words.example: Her grandparents emigrated from Italy in search of work.

words.definition: famous and highly respected in a field
words.mnemonic: Eminem is eminent — a renowned rapper; don't confuse with imminent (about to happen).
words.example: An eminent scientist delivered the keynote speech at the conference.

words.definition: to imitate someone admired in order to equal them
words.mnemonic: 'emu-late': a young emu copies its parent's exact wobbly walk.
words.example: Young pianists emulate their teachers before finding a style of their own.

words.definition: the hard glossy outer layer of a tooth
words.mnemonic: Your dentist nags: soda melts the enamel, the tooth's shiny armor.
words.example: Brushing too hard can wear away the enamel on your teeth.

words.definition: to gradually intrude on someone's territory or rights
words.mnemonic: A cockROACH encroaches — creeping inch by inch into your kitchen.
words.example: The growing city slowly encroached on the surrounding farmland.

words.definition: public approval or support of someone or something
words.mnemonic: A star athlete's shoe endorsement: signing your name on the back (dorsum) to say 'this is good.'
words.root: Latin in + dorsum (back) — originally signing the back of a document.
words.example: The candidate won the newspaper's endorsement before the election.

words.definition: to give money or a quality to someone or something
words.mnemonic: END-ow: at the END of his life the donor gave a big DOWry of money to the school.
words.root: Old French endouer 'to provide with a dower'
words.example: The alumnus endowed the library with a million-dollar gift.

words.definition: drain of energy; weaken
words.mnemonic: sounds like 'energize' but is its evil twin — it steals the energy
words.example: The muggy afternoon hike enervated even the fittest campers.

words.definition: cause or give rise to
words.mnemonic: shares genes with 'generate' — it births new feelings and situations
words.example: The referee's blown call engendered boos from both benches.

words.definition: a puzzling or mysterious person or thing
words.mnemonic: The WWII Enigma machine — a code so mysterious armies fought to crack it.
words.example: Even after years of friendship, her quiet brother remained an enigma.

words.definition: mysterious and hard to interpret
words.mnemonic: the Mona Lisa's smile — AN ENIGMA in a frame, forever unread
words.example: She answered every question with the same enigmatic smile.

words.definition: to give someone knowledge or understanding
words.mnemonic: To enlighten is to switch on a LIGHT in someone's mind.
words.example: The documentary enlightened viewers about life on the ocean floor.

words.definition: deep hatred or hostility
words.mnemonic: eNMity = eNeMy-ity — the grudge enemies carry in their pockets
words.example: Years of enmity between the families ruined every holiday dinner.

words.definition: showing eager excitement or intense interest
words.mnemonic: Enthusiasm comes from Greek 'god inside' — so excited it's like a god is in you.
words.root: Greek entheos, 'possessed by a god, inspired'.
words.example: The enthusiastic crowd cheered every point of the match.

words.definition: to tempt or attract by offering something desirable
words.mnemonic: Dangle something NICE to enTICE — cheese entices the mouse.
words.example: The bakery's smell enticed customers in from the street.

words.definition: a scientist who studies insects
words.mnemonic: 'ento' looks like 'ant' — an entomologist studies ants and other bugs.
words.example: The entomologist spent years cataloging beetles in the rainforest canopy.

words.definition: lasting a very short time
words.mnemonic: a mayfly's diary: 'Day 1: born. Day 1: died.'
words.example: Fame from one viral video is usually ephemeral.

words.definition: a person with refined taste in food and drink
words.mnemonic: An epicure's cure for everything is EPIC food — fine dining, not just eating a lot.
words.root: From Epicurus, Greek philosopher of pleasure
words.example: As an epicure, he refused to eat cheese from a plastic wrapper.

words.definition: to be a perfect example of something
words.mnemonic: The E-PITOME sits at the TOP of the category — the perfect example of it.
words.example: Her calm speech epitomized grace under pressure.

words.definition: a distinct, notable period in history or a person's life
words.mnemonic: An epoch is an epic-long stretch of history.
words.example: The moon landing marked a new epoch in human exploration.

words.definition: calm composure, especially under stress
words.mnemonic: EQUAl ANIMation winning or losing — heartbeat stays flatline calm
words.example: The pilot announced the engine trouble with startling equanimity.

words.definition: use vague language to avoid committing
words.mnemonic: EQUal VOICes saying yes AND no at once — committing to neither
words.example: Asked who broke the vase, he equivocated for ten minutes.

words.definition: gradually wearing something away
words.mnemonic: Erosive rivers ERASE the rock — grinding it away grain by grain.
words.example: The erosive waves slowly carved caves into the cliff.

words.definition: to make a mistake
words.mnemonic: "To err is human" — a small slip; a blunder is an err gone BIG.
words.example: The referee erred by awarding the goal to the wrong team.

words.definition: having or showing deep scholarly knowledge
words.mnemonic: The erudite professor has READ IT all — every book in the library.
words.root: Latin eruditus, 'polished, educated' (e 'out of' + rudis 'rude, raw')
words.example: The erudite historian could quote ancient texts from memory.

words.definition: understood by only a small, specialized group
words.mnemonic: knowledge locked in the inner circle's clubhouse — outsiders never get the joke
words.example: His hobby is esoteric: translating medieval accounting manuals.

words.definition: extremely delicate, light, and seemingly heavenly
words.mnemonic: 'ether + real': thin as the sky's upper air, an airy heavenly lightness.
words.example: The dancer moved with an ethereal grace that seemed to defy gravity.

words.definition: a person who delivers a speech praising someone, often at a funeral
words.mnemonic: EU = good, LOG = words — the eulogist speaks good words at a funeral.
words.example: The eulogist described his grandfather's kindness and famous pancake breakfasts.

words.definition: a mild word substituted for one considered harsh or offensive
words.mnemonic: 'Passed away' is a euphemism, a 'good-sounding' (eu-) phrase for died.
words.example: 'Letting someone go' is a euphemism for firing them.

words.definition: vanishing quickly; fleeting
words.mnemonic: EVAporating scent: perfume in the wind — there, then gone
words.example: The rainbow was evanescent, gone before she found her camera.

words.definition: avoiding a question or issue; tending to escape
words.mnemonic: Evasive = always EVADING — dodging questions like dodgeballs.
words.example: The senator gave evasive answers to every direct question.

words.definition: make a bad situation worse
words.mnemonic: EX-ACID-bate: pouring acid on a wound — congratulations, it's worse
words.example: Scratching the bug bite only exacerbated the itching.

words.definition: made to seem larger or greater than it really is
words.mnemonic: Exaggerated is the opposite of understated — the minnow becomes a whale.
words.example: His exaggerated account turned a drizzle into a raging storm.

words.definition: to inspect closely and carefully
words.mnemonic: Analogy hook: inspector : examine — the job names the action.
words.example: The doctor examined the X-ray for any sign of a fracture.

words.definition: so excellent it serves as a model for others
words.mnemonic: Exemplary work is an EXAMPLE the teacher shows the whole class.
words.example: Her exemplary conduct earned her the school citizenship award.

words.definition: to urge someone strongly with encouraging words
words.mnemonic: a coach EXits the bench to SHOUT, exhorting the team to push harder.
words.example: The captain exhorted his tired crew to row a little harder.

words.definition: an urgent speech or plea strongly encouraging someone to act
words.mnemonic: A coach's exhortation exhorts the team to charge harder.
words.example: The coach's fiery exhortation drove the team to victory.

words.definition: to force someone to leave their country as punishment
words.mnemonic: EX = out — exiled means kicked OUT of your homeland, an ex-resident.
words.example: The defeated general was exiled to a remote island.

words.definition: officially clear of blame
words.mnemonic: EX-ONUS-rate: the court lifts the burden (onus) off your back
words.example: New camera footage exonerated the accused shoplifter.

words.definition: far exceeding what is reasonable, especially in price
words.mnemonic: ex-ORBIT-ant: the price flew right out of orbit, sky-high.
words.example: The tourist trap charged exorbitant prices for ordinary bottled water.

words.definition: to erase or remove something completely
words.mnemonic: 'ex-SPONGE': a sponge wipes the whole record perfectly clean.
words.example: The judge ordered the arrest expunged from her permanent record.

words.definition: praise enthusiastically
words.mnemonic: EX-TOLL: climb the tower, ring the bell, shout the praises
words.example: Coaches extolled her hustle more than her scoring.

words.definition: irrelevant or unrelated to the matter at hand
words.mnemonic: 'extra' stuff from outside — beyond what actually belongs.
words.example: The editor cut every extraneous detail that slowed the story down.

words.definition: filled with lively energy and enthusiasm
words.mnemonic: EXtra UBER energy — an exuberant puppy bouncing off the walls.
words.example: The exuberant fans cheered long after the game had ended.

words.definition: to ooze out; to display a quality abundantly
words.mnemonic: EX-UDE: stuff EXits like goo — a tree exudes sap, a champ exudes confidence.
words.example: The pine trees exude sticky sap all summer long.

words.definition: joking, often at an inappropriate time
words.mnemonic: brags all five vowels in order (a-e-i-o-u) — a joke of a word, for jokers
words.example: 'Nice parking,' he said, facetious as ever, eyeing the crooked car.

words.definition: based on false reasoning; misleading
words.mnemonic: FALLacy-ous: an argument that FALLS the moment you lean on it
words.example: The ad's fallacious claim collapsed under one simple question.

words.definition: plowed but left unplanted; inactive or dormant
words.mnemonic: 'fellow lying low': a field lying low, resting, growing nothing.
words.example: Farmers leave fields fallow so the soil can recover its nutrients.

words.definition: an intense interest or attraction
words.mnemonic: Degree ladder: interest → fascination — interest turned up to full volume.
words.example: His fascination with volcanoes began after a trip to Hawaii.

words.definition: extremely attentive to detail; hard to please
words.mnemonic: FAST + TIDY + OUS: fastidious people tidy everything fast and fuss over every crumb.
words.example: The fastidious chef rejected any tomato with the slightest bruise.

words.definition: extreme tiredness; exhaustion
words.mnemonic: Analogy hook: fatigue is to energy as isolation is to companionship — the lack of it.
words.example: After the marathon, fatigue forced her to sleep twelve hours.

words.definition: smugly foolish; silly without realizing it
words.mnemonic: a FAT ego on a vacUOUS head — proudly saying nothing
words.example: His fatuous grin didn't fade even as his plan failed.

words.definition: artificial; made in imitation of something genuine
words.mnemonic: Faux sounds like 'foe' — a fake friend is a foe in faux fur.
words.example: Her faux leather jacket looked real but cost half the price.

words.definition: flatter someone excessively to win favor
words.mnemonic: picture a baby deer (fawn) nuzzling the judge for a better score
words.example: Interns fawned over the CEO, laughing at every stale joke.

words.definition: to pretend or fake a feeling or condition
words.mnemonic: FEIGN sounds like FAKE-n: FEIGNing sick to skip school is faking a fever.
words.root: Latin fingere, 'to shape, invent' (same root as 'fiction').
words.example: He feigned surprise even though he had planned the party himself.

words.definition: a deceptive move meant to mislead an opponent
words.mnemonic: A boxer's feint makes you think he'll faint left, then he strikes right.
words.example: Her feint to the left fooled the goalkeeper completely.

words.definition: intensely passionate or enthusiastic
words.mnemonic: FERVent = FEVER-ent: beliefs running a 104-degree fever
words.example: She is a fervent defender of the school recycling program.

words.definition: having an offensive, rotten smell
words.mnemonic: 'FEE-tid' like sweaty FEET — that gym locker smells fetid.
words.example: A fetid odor rose from the neglected garbage bins all summer.

words.definition: to restrain or chain; to hold back
words.mnemonic: 'fet-ter' clamps the FEET — chains that hold you in place.
words.example: Strict rules fettered the artist's imagination and stifled every new idea.

words.definition: constantly changing one's mind or loyalties; not dependable
words.mnemonic: Fickle rhymes with pickle — one minute they love pickles, the next they hate them.
words.example: Fickle fans cheered the team one week and booed it the next.

words.definition: a leader in name only, without real power
words.mnemonic: The carved figure on a ship's bow looks important but steers nothing — a figurehead.
words.example: The king was a mere figurehead; parliament made every real decision.

words.definition: to steal something of small value
words.mnemonic: 'filch' rhymes with pinch — to pinch a small item off a shelf.
words.example: He would filch cookies from the jar when no one was looking.

words.definition: shockingly obvious and offensive
words.mnemonic: a foul so obvious it waves a FLAG at the referee
words.example: Tripping the runner was a flagrant violation, whistled instantly.

words.definition: an inexperienced beginner
words.mnemonic: a baby bird wobbling on the LEDGE, feathers barely FLEDGEd
words.example: The fledgling reporter fumbled her first three interviews.

words.definition: disrespectfully casual about serious matters
words.mnemonic: he FLIPs serious questions like pancakes — flip, joke, next
words.example: Her flippant reply to the principal earned a week of detention.

words.definition: excessively ornate or flowery
words.mnemonic: FLORal + horrID: prose so buried in flowers you can't find the sentence
words.example: His florid thank-you note used 'resplendent' three times.

words.definition: openly disobey or show contempt for rules
words.mnemonic: strut OUT past the 'No Exit' sign — and don't confuse with flaunt
words.example: Skaters flouted the park's clearly posted curfew every night.

words.definition: the leaves of a plant or tree collectively
words.mnemonic: A FOLIO is a leaf of paper — foliage is all a tree's leaves together.
