words.example: In October the foliage turns brilliant shades of red and gold.

words.definition: stir up trouble or rebellion
words.mnemonic: FOaM-ENT: shake the crowd like soda until it foams over
words.example: Smuggled pamphlets fomented unrest among the sailors.

words.definition: patient self-control; restraint under provocation
words.mnemonic: for-BEAR-ance: you 'bear' the annoyance calmly, holding back like a patient bear.
words.example: She showed remarkable forbearance with the rude, impatient customer.

words.definition: sad and lonely from being abandoned; hopeless
words.mnemonic: The forlorn puppy looked forsaken and lost in the rain.
words.example: The forlorn child waited alone at the empty station.

words.definition: to abandon or give up completely
words.mnemonic: for-SAKE: you walk away 'for the sake' of leaving — forsaking them.
words.example: He refused to forsake his friends even when it cost him everything.

words.definition: to strengthen, especially against attack
words.mnemonic: FORT-ify: build a fort around it — ramparts fortify the castle.
words.example: The soldiers fortified the town with walls and deep trenches.

words.definition: happening by lucky chance
words.mnemonic: FORTUne parachuting in uninvited — luck you never scheduled
words.example: A fortuitous rain delay let their injured pitcher rest.

words.definition: irritable and hard to control
words.mnemonic: FRACTIons make the whole class fractious — cranky mutiny by lunch
words.example: The fractious toddlers turned storytime into a wrestling match.

words.definition: wildly fast, frantic, and uncontrolled
words.mnemonic: 'frantic + kinetic' — frantic energy in constant motion.
words.example: The kitchen turned frenetic as orders piled up during the dinner rush.

words.definition: careful and sparing with money; economical
words.mnemonic: Degree ladder: frugal → cheap — frugal is smart saving; cheap is stingy.
words.example: The frugal traveler packed sandwiches instead of buying airport food.

words.definition: a person fleeing from capture, especially from the law
words.mnemonic: FUGitive — someone who FLED; think of prison sirens and a man sprinting into the woods.
words.root: Latin fugere = to flee (also in refuge, fugue)
words.example: The fugitive hid in barns while marshals searched the county.

words.definition: the fixed point on which a lever pivots or balances
words.mnemonic: A seesaw's FULCRUM is the FULL center that CRUMbles nothing — everything balances on it.
words.example: Place the fulcrum closer to the rock and the lever lifts it easily.

words.definition: an outburst of public anger or excitement; uproar
words.mnemonic: FURor = FURY going public — one angry person has fury, a whole crowd makes a furor.
words.root: Latin furere = to rage (same family as fury, furious)
words.example: The referee's call caused a furor among the fans.

words.definition: done secretly and sneakily to avoid notice
words.mnemonic: Your pairing works: FURtive vs. overt — a cat in FUR sneaking furtively, versus strutting overtly.
words.root: Latin furtum = theft — moving like a thief
words.example: He cast a furtive glance at the answer sheet.

words.definition: pointless; incapable of producing any useful result
words.mnemonic: FUTILE rhymes with 'few-tile' — laying a few tiles on a collapsing floor: totally pointless.
words.root: Latin futilis = leaky, hence pouring effort into a leaky vessel
words.example: Arguing with the storm was futile; the picnic was ruined.

words.definition: deny or contradict
words.mnemonic: to say AGAINst — 'gain' is Old English for 'against'
words.example: No one dared gainsay the captain's version of events.

words.definition: shock into sudden action
words.mnemonic: named for Galvani, who zapped frog legs and made them jump
words.root: from Galvani, pioneer of electric currents
words.example: The upset loss galvanized the team into dawn practices.

words.definition: to run and leap about playfully
words.mnemonic: sounds like 'gamble,' but picture lambs that 'gam-BOL' — bouncing across a field.
words.example: Lambs gamboled across the meadow in the warm spring sunshine.

words.definition: unpleasantly bright and showy; tastelessly flashy
words.mnemonic: GARish = a neon GARage sign blinking hot pink and lime green at 3 a.m.
words.example: She winced at his garish orange suit and glitter tie.

words.definition: items of clothing or articles of dress
words.mnemonic: A garment is what you 'garnish' your body with.
words.example: The tailor pressed the silk garments before the show.

words.definition: to gather or collect, especially gradually
words.mnemonic: a garden-er 'garners' the harvest, storing grain in the granary.
words.example: Her film garnered praise from critics and won several awards.

words.definition: excessively talkative, especially about trivial things
words.mnemonic: GARRulous — a parrot named GARRy who never, ever stops squawking.
words.example: The garrulous barber told three stories before starting the haircut.

words.definition: socially awkward, tactless, and lacking grace
words.mnemonic: 'gauche' is French for 'left' — tripping over your left foot socially.
words.example: It was gauche of him to ask how much her ring had cost.

words.definition: tastelessly bright, showy, and flashy
words.mnemonic: 'GAWD-y': so loud you gasp 'oh gawd' at the neon colors.
words.example: He wore a gaudy gold suit covered in sequins to the party.

words.definition: a person who stares openly and stupidly at something
words.mnemonic: GAWK sounds like a goose's honk — a goose freezing mid-honk to stare, beak open: a gawker.
words.example: Gawkers slowed traffic as they stared at the crashed truck.

words.definition: friendly, warm, and cheerful in manner
words.mnemonic: A GENIE with a GENIAL grin — he'd grant you wishes just because he likes you.
words.root: Latin genialis = festive, of one's spirit
words.example: Our genial host greeted every guest with a warm handshake.

words.definition: relevant and fitting to the topic
words.mnemonic: a GERM at a hospital meeting — painfully on-topic
words.example: Keep your comments germane to tonight's budget question.

words.definition: to begin to grow or develop, as a seed sprouting
words.mnemonic: A GERM of an idea GERMINATES — picture a tiny seed cracking open and a green shoot poking up.
words.example: The bean seeds germinated three days after we planted them.

words.definition: a hot spring that shoots up jets of water and steam
words.mnemonic: GEYSER sounds like 'gee, sir!' — what you yell when boiling water erupts out of the ground.
words.root: From Geysir, a famous erupting spring in Iceland
words.example: Old Faithful, the famous geyser, erupts roughly every ninety minutes.

words.definition: to prepare oneself for action; to brace or encircle
words.mnemonic: 'gird' sounds like 'guard' — you brace and guard yourself, tightening your belt.
words.example: The town girded itself against the coming storm by boarding windows.

words.definition: fluent but shallow or insincere
words.mnemonic: words that slide out greased — fast, smooth, gripping nothing
words.example: His glib apology convinced no one he was actually sorry.

words.definition: an oversupply; far more of something than is needed
words.mnemonic: GLUT is what a GLUTTON creates — a table so overloaded with food it groans.
words.root: Latin gluttire = to swallow (same root as glutton)
words.example: A glut of pumpkins after Halloween drove prices way down.

words.definition: to provoke or prod someone into acting, often into anger
words.mnemonic: A goad is literally a pointed stick for poking cattle — to goad someone is to poke them until they react.
words.root: Old English gad = pointed stick for driving cattle
words.example: His brother goaded him into jumping off the high dive.

words.definition: extremely light, thin, and delicate
words.mnemonic: GAUZE-summer: spider silk drifting on warm August air
words.root: 'goose summer' — season of floating spider silk (Middle English)
words.example: Gossamer threads of fog clung to the harbor at dawn.

words.definition: using pompous, high-flown language
words.mnemonic: GRAND + ELOQUENT: renting a throne just to announce lunch
words.example: His grandiloquent toast used ten words where two would do.

words.definition: impressive in an exaggerated, pretentious way
words.mnemonic: a 'grand' plan blown up so absurdly big it's over the top.
words.example: His grandiose scheme to build a private zoo never left the drawing board.

words.definition: uncalled for and unjustified; also, given free of charge
words.mnemonic: GRATUitous shares roots with GRATIS (free) — a 'free' insult nobody asked for or paid for.
words.example: The movie's gratuitous violence added nothing to the story.

words.definition: sociable; loving company
words.mnemonic: GREG invites the whole conGREGation — the flock parties together
words.example: Gregarious by nature, she befriended the whole bus by noon.

words.definition: a twisted facial expression showing pain or disgust
words.mnemonic: GRIM + FACE = grimace: the grim face you make biting into a lemon.
words.example: She swallowed the bitter medicine with a grimace.

words.definition: act with excessive humility to please someone
words.mnemonic: crawling through the GRAVEL at the boss's feet
words.example: He groveled for a deadline extension, apologizing six times.

words.definition: sly cunning used to trick or deceive people
words.mnemonic: Your 'opposite of honesty' works: beGUILE means to trick — guile is the trickster's toolkit.
words.root: Related to Old French guile = deception (cf. beguile)
words.example: The fox used guile, not strength, to steal the crow's cheese.

words.definition: to swallow quickly in large amounts
words.mnemonic: Your 'drink, degree up' is the SSAT pattern: sip < drink < GULP — the word even sounds like the throat noise.
words.example: He gulped the whole glass of water after the race.

words.definition: relating to the sense of taste
words.mnemonic: GUSTatory — eating with GUSTO: your taste buds throwing a party.
words.root: Latin gustare = to taste (also in gusto, disgust)
words.example: The chef's tasting menu was a gustatory adventure.

words.definition: overused and unoriginal; worn out from repetition
words.mnemonic: A HACKNEY cab drives the same worn route ten thousand times — like every 'hero saves the world' plot.
words.root: from Hackney horses, hired out until worn out
words.example: The movie's hackneyed plot bored everyone by the second scene.

words.definition: Calm, peaceful, and happy; prosperous.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'Hal-see-on': picture sailor Hal seeing a glassy, dead-calm ocean.
words.root: Greek halkyon (kingfisher, thought to calm the seas)
words.example: She fondly recalled the halcyon days of childhood summers by the lake.

words.definition: Regarded as holy or greatly respected.
words.mnemonic: A hallowed hall is holy: picture a hushed, echoing cathedral.
words.example: They walked quietly through the hallowed halls of the ancient temple.

words.definition: unlucky; marked by constant misfortune
words.mnemonic: HAP means luck — hapless is luck-LESS. His happiness left with it.
words.root: hap = luck (Old Norse), as in 'perhaps' and 'happen'
words.example: The hapless camper pitched his tent on an anthill during a thunderstorm.

words.definition: a long, angry, scolding speech
words.mnemonic: Har-ANGRY: the hour-long harangue about your messy room keeps RANGing in your ears.
words.example: Coach delivered a twenty-minute harangue after we lost by thirty points.

words.definition: a sign that something is coming; a forerunner
words.mnemonic: HARBOR-BRINGER: the first ship into the harbor brings news of the whole fleet behind it.
words.root: Old French herbergere, a scout sent ahead to find lodging
words.example: Dark clouds over the mountains were a harbinger of the storm.

words.definition: arrogantly superior; looking down on others
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'HOTTY': thinks she's SO hot she won't even glance at you.
words.example: The haughty senior refused to sit anywhere near the freshmen.

words.definition: a person who lives only for pleasure
words.mnemonic: HE-DONUT-IST: he'd reorganize his entire life around the next donut.
words.example: A true hedonist, he planned every vacation around food and naps.

words.definition: to pay careful attention to; take notice of
words.mnemonic: HEED rhymes with NEED — advice you NEED to HEED, like 'Bridge Out Ahead'.
words.example: Sailors who heed storm warnings stay in the harbor.

words.definition: shockingly evil or wicked
words.mnemonic: HEINous sounds like 'HATE-ness' — a crime so evil it's pure concentrated hate.
words.example: The villain's heinous plot shocked even his own henchmen.

words.definition: to announce or signal the coming of something
words.mnemonic: Newspapers are named 'The Herald' because they announce the news — a herald trumpets what's coming.
words.example: Dark clouds heralded the approaching thunderstorm.

words.definition: a person who lives alone, withdrawn from society
words.mnemonic: Your pairing sticks: a HERMIT crab hides alone in its shell — a hermit is reclusive by definition.
words.root: Greek eremites = of the desert (desert-dwelling monks)
words.example: The old hermit lived in a mountain cabin, avoiding all visitors.

words.definition: a pause or gap in an activity
words.mnemonic: Your favorite show goes on HIATUS — HI, wait for us — a long gap between seasons.
words.example: The band took a two-year hiatus before recording again.

words.definition: Stubbornly narrow-minded and resistant to change.
words.mnemonic: Skin (hide) bound so tight around an ox it cannot move; a mind stuck in stiff leather.
words.example: The hidebound committee rejected every fresh idea the interns proposed.

words.definition: overly dramatic; theatrical
words.mnemonic: A ham actor rubbing an ONION to weep over HISTORY — fake stage tears on demand.
words.example: Her histrionic gasp over a paper cut silenced the whole cafeteria.

words.definition: to lift or raise something, especially with effort or equipment
words.mnemonic: HOIST has that grunt sound — 'hoi!' as sailors heave a sail up the mast.
words.example: The crew hoisted the flag as the ship left port.

words.definition: to trick or deceive someone
words.mnemonic: Literally: pull a HOOD over someone's eyes and WINK to your partner — they can't see they're being conned.
words.root: hood + wink (old sense: to close the eyes)
words.example: The con artist hoodwinked tourists into buying fake tickets.

words.definition: flat and level; parallel to the ground
words.mnemonic: The HORIZON is the flattest line you'll ever see — horizontal means lying like the horizon.
words.root: Greek horizon = boundary line where sky meets earth
words.example: Push the lever until it is perfectly horizontal.

words.definition: excessive pride or self-confidence, often causing downfall
words.mnemonic: Mr. HUBRIS thinks he's the HUB of the universe — right until he trips on stage.
words.root: hybris = pride that offends the gods (Greek)
words.example: It was pure hubris to skip studying because he'd never failed before.

words.definition: Dull, monotonous, and lacking excitement.
words.mnemonic: A drum humming the same 'hum-hum-hum' beat forever until you yawn.
words.example: He quit his humdrum office job to travel the world.

words.definition: deliberate, obvious exaggeration
words.mnemonic: HYPER kid with a BOWL: 'I could eat a MILLION bowls of this cereal!'
words.example: 'I've told you a billion times' is classic hyperbole.

words.definition: claiming beliefs or standards one's own behavior contradicts
words.mnemonic: A HYPOcrite is an actor — Greek hypokrites literally meant stage actor, playing virtue they don't have.
words.example: Preaching honesty while cheating on taxes is pure hypocrisy.

words.definition: a person who attacks cherished beliefs or traditions
words.mnemonic: ICON + CLAST (breaker) — someone smashing sacred statues, then any 'sacred' tradition.
words.example: As an iconoclast, she questioned every rule the school treasured.

words.definition: an odd habit or quirk unique to one person
words.mnemonic: IDIO-SINK: he washes exactly one sock at a time in the sink — that's HIS thing.
words.example: Wearing wool gloves in July is just one of his idiosyncrasies.

words.definition: publicly shameful; humiliating
words.mnemonic: in (not) + NOMEN (name): so disgraced they scratch your name off the trophy.
words.example: The champions suffered an ignominious loss to the last-place team.

words.definition: to light up; also to make clear or explain
words.mnemonic: Your two groups ARE the trick: a LAMP illuminates a room; a good teacher illuminates an idea — same word, light for eyes or mind.
words.example: Streetlights illuminated the path; her diagram illuminated the proof.

words.definition: highly distinguished and famous for achievement
words.mnemonic: ILLUSTRIOUS shares 'lustr-' (shine) with luster — a career so brilliant it gleams.
words.example: The illustrious scientist won nearly every award in her field.

words.definition: about to happen very soon
words.mnemonic: IMMINENT = 'in a minute' — the storm is imminent, arriving in a minute.
words.example: With the siren wailing, a tornado seemed imminent.

words.definition: to pierce through with a sharp, pointed object
words.mnemonic: im-PALE: PALE means a pointed fence stake — to impale is to run something onto a stake, like a kebab.
words.example: The knight impaled the dragon with his lance.

words.definition: to pass on information, knowledge, or a quality to others
words.mnemonic: im-PART = give a PART of what you have — a teacher imparts a part of her knowledge to you.
words.root: Latin impartire = to share, give a part
words.example: Grandfather loved to impart wisdom during our fishing trips.

words.definition: treating all sides equally; not biased
words.mnemonic: Your breakdown is the whole word: im (not) + PARTIAL (favoring a part) — a referee who favors no part.
words.example: A judge must remain impartial no matter who is on trial.

words.definition: a deadlock; a situation with no way forward
words.mnemonic: im-PASSE = no PASSage — two arguing sides stuck in a dead-end alley, neither can pass.
words.example: Talks reached an impasse when neither side would compromise.

words.definition: deserving of accusation or removal for misconduct; open to challenge
words.mnemonic: An 'impeachable' official can be 'impeached' — charged and put on trial.
words.root: Latin impedicare 'to catch, entangle' (as by the foot).
words.example: The senators argued his bribery was a clearly impeachable offense.

words.definition: formally charging a public official with misconduct
words.mnemonic: Impeachment ACCUSES, it doesn't remove — imagine hurling a PEACH at a corrupt official as the formal charge.
words.root: Old French empechier = to hinder, entangle
words.example: The senator's bribery scandal led to calls for impeachment.

words.definition: having little or no money
words.mnemonic: IM-PECAN-ious: so broke he can't even afford the pecans for the pie.
words.example: The impecunious artist paid his rent in paintings.

words.definition: absolutely necessary; commanding
words.mnemonic: Your 'command' note is the key: an EMPEROR gives imperatives — orders that must be obeyed.
words.root: Latin imperare = to command (also in emperor)
words.example: It is imperative that you arrive before the gates close.

words.definition: bossy and domineering, like an emperor
words.mnemonic: EMPEROR-ious little sister on the couch: 'Fetch my juice. NOW.'
words.example: With an imperious wave, she ordered everyone out of her room.

words.definition: impossible to penetrate; unaffected by something
words.mnemonic: im-PER-VIOUS = no way (via) through — a raincoat impervious to rain, a champ impervious to boos.
words.example: The tank's armor was impervious to ordinary bullets.

words.definition: acting suddenly without thinking; rash
words.mnemonic: IM-PET-uous: buying a PET octopus at midnight because it 'felt right.'
words.example: In an impetuous moment, he dyed his hair green before picture day.

words.definition: impossible to calm, satisfy, or stop
words.mnemonic: im-PLACATE-able: no apology, gift, or pizza can placate this grudge. Ever.
words.example: The implacable rival refused every peace offering we made.

words.definition: To attack as false; call into question.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'im-PUNE': you jab a pin to puncture someone's argument.
words.example: The lawyer tried to impugn the witness's honesty before the jury.

words.definition: silly and empty of meaning
words.mnemonic: Rhymes with NO BRAIN: chatter so empty it echoes.
words.example: The meeting dissolved into inane chatter about nothing at all.

words.definition: to make someone extremely angry
words.mnemonic: Your crossed-out 'fire' was on track: incense burns — to incense someone is to set their temper on fire.
words.root: Latin incendere = to set on fire (also in incendiary)
words.example: The unfair penalty incensed the entire home crowd.

words.definition: something that motivates or encourages action
words.mnemonic: Your pair is the test trick: incentive pulls you toward action, deterrent pushes you away — carrot vs. stick.
words.root: Latin incentivus = setting the tune, rousing
words.example: The bonus was a strong incentive to finish the project early.

words.definition: continuing without stopping
words.mnemonic: in-CESSant = not CEASING — a dripping faucet at 2 a.m. that never, ever stops.
words.example: The incessant barking next door kept us awake all night.

words.definition: Just begun and not fully formed.
words.mnemonic: 'In-KO-it': an incomplete cake, still just batter, not yet a cake.
words.example: His plans were still inchoate, more daydream than strategy.

words.definition: to stir up or provoke someone to act, often violently
words.mnemonic: Keep your goad pairing: goad pokes one person, INCITE ignites a crowd — the speech incited a riot.
words.root: Latin incitare = to urge on, set in motion
words.example: The fiery speech incited the crowd to storm the gates.

words.definition: Out of place; inconsistent with its surroundings.
words.mnemonic: Not congruent: a waddling penguin in a blazing desert looks incongruous.
words.example: His loud tie looked incongruous with the somber funeral suit.

words.definition: so sad that no one can comfort you
words.mnemonic: in- (not) + consolable — you CANNOT console them; the hugs bounce right off.
words.example: After losing the championship, the goalie was inconsolable for days.

words.definition: impossible to correct or reform
words.mnemonic: in-CORRECT-able: the teacher's red pen took one look and retired early.
words.example: Detention never worked; the incorrigible prankster struck again by Friday.

words.definition: increasing gradually in small steps
words.mnemonic: Increments = tiny additions — a piggy bank fills coin by coin, incrementally.
words.example: Her incremental improvements added up to a huge jump in her score.

words.definition: make someone appear guilty of a crime
words.mnemonic: in-CRIMIN-ate — put the CRIMINAL label on someone.
words.example: The muddy footprints seemed to incriminate the gardener.

words.definition: To instill an idea through persistent repetition.
words.mnemonic: 'In-CULL-cate': you stamp a lesson in with your heel until it sticks.
words.root: Latin inculcare (to tread in, from calx, heel)
words.example: Coaches inculcate discipline by repeating the same drills daily.

words.definition: tireless; refusing to give up
words.mnemonic: in-de-FATIGUE-able: the Energizer Bunny — fatigue simply cannot be installed.
words.example: The indefatigable reporter chased the story for three straight years.

words.definition: anger aroused by something unjust, unworthy, or mean
words.mnemonic: Losing your dignity to unfair treatment sparks indignation.
words.example: She felt indignation when the referee ignored the obvious foul.

words.definition: habitually lazy; avoiding all effort
words.mnemonic: inDOLENt has a DENT in it — the permanent couch dent where he never moves.
words.example: The indolent cat watched the mouse stroll past and just yawned.

words.definition: too lenient; letting someone have whatever they want
words.mnemonic: An indulgent grandma lets you have ice cream for breakfast — she spoils you.
words.example: His indulgent parents never made him do a single chore.

words.definition: the quality of being hard-working and diligent
words.mnemonic: Like a busy INDUSTRY, an industrious person keeps the factory of work running.
words.example: Her industriousness meant she finished tasks long before anyone else.

words.definition: impossible to stop or persuade; relentless
words.mnemonic: You cannot beg lava to turn around — it cannot be 'exora-ed' (begged) into stopping.
words.root: exorare = to win over by begging (Latin)
words.example: Nothing slowed the inexorable advance of the rising floodwater.

words.definition: cleverly inventive and original
words.mnemonic: An inGENIUS idea — a genius-level invention like a mousetrap made of Legos.
words.example: She devised an ingenious way to water the plants while on vacation.

words.definition: innocently honest and trusting; naive
words.mnemonic: NOT ingenious — the GENUINE kid who cheerfully tells burglars where the spare key is.
words.root: ingenuus = freeborn, frank (Latin); opposite of disingenuous
words.example: Too ingenuous to suspect a trick, she handed the stranger her ticket.

words.definition: harmless; not likely to offend or injure
words.mnemonic: Sounds like INNOCENT — an innocuous garden snake just wants to sunbathe.
words.example: The spider looked scary but was completely innocuous.

words.definition: eagerness to learn or know things; curiosity
words.mnemonic: An INQUISITIVE mind runs a constant INQUIRY — question after question after question.
words.root: Latin quaerere, 'to ask, seek' (same root as 'question').
words.example: Her inquisitiveness led her to take apart every gadget in the house.

words.definition: impossible to satisfy
words.mnemonic: in- (not) + SATIS-fy-able — a bottomless stomach that no buffet can defeat.
words.example: His insatiable curiosity led him to read three books a week.

words.definition: Impossible to understand or interpret.
words.mnemonic: You cannot scrutinize it (in-scrutable): a poker face you simply can't read.
words.example: The judge kept an inscrutable expression as the verdict was read.

words.definition: Harmful in a gradual, hidden way.
words.mnemonic: 'In-SID-ious': a sneaky snake named Sid slithers in unnoticed to strike.
words.example: The insidious disease spread for years before any symptoms appeared.

words.definition: dull, flat, and flavorless
words.mnemonic: in-SIP-id: one SIP of that soup tells you it needs... everything.
words.example: The lecture was so insipid even the teacher kept checking the clock.

words.definition: boldly rude and disrespectful toward authority
words.mnemonic: The IN-SOLE of a sneaker sticking its tongue out at the principal.
words.example: The insolent student answered the principal with a smirk and shrug.

words.definition: an official who examines things carefully
words.mnemonic: Analogy anchor: an inspector EXAMINES the way a predator HUNTS — it's their job.
words.example: The health inspector examined every corner of the restaurant kitchen.

words.definition: honesty and strong moral principles
words.mnemonic: A person of integrity stays whole (integer = whole number) — no cracks in their honesty.
words.example: She showed integrity by admitting the mistake nobody else had noticed.

words.definition: extreme in strength, degree, or feeling; passionate
words.mnemonic: An intense fan is IN TENTS outside the stadium three days early — that's enthusiasm.
words.example: Her intense devotion to chess meant six hours of practice daily.

words.definition: a feeling of curiosity or attention toward something
words.mnemonic: Degree ladder: interest → fascination, like drink → gulp; fascination is interest turned up loud.
words.example: His mild interest in chess grew into a true fascination.

words.definition: a temporary trainee working to gain experience
words.mnemonic: An INTERNal newbie — inside the company, but only temporarily, fetching coffee.
words.example: The summer intern sorted files and learned how the office ran.

words.definition: having the form or force of a question; questioning
words.mnemonic: An interrogatory tone interrogates, firing question after question.
words.example: The detective fixed him with an interrogatory stare.

words.definition: very close and personal
words.mnemonic: Intimate friends know your IN-most secrets — the inner circle.
words.example: Only a few intimate friends were invited to the small wedding.

words.definition: stubbornly refusing to compromise
words.mnemonic: in- (not) + transigent (willing to deal) — NO TRANSaction, no deal, won't budge an inch.
words.root: Latin in- (not) + transigere (to come to an agreement)
words.example: The intransigent senator refused every version of the compromise bill.

words.definition: fearless and adventurous
words.mnemonic: in- (not) + trepid (trembling) — no trembling: the intrepid explorer high-fives a volcano.
words.example: The intrepid climber pressed on despite the howling storm.

words.definition: to flood; to overwhelm with a huge amount
words.mnemonic: in + UNDA (wave): waves rolling IN through your living room window.
words.example: Fans inundated the singer with letters after the concert.

words.definition: Firmly established by long habit; habitual.
words.mnemonic: 'In-VET-erate': a habit so old it's a grizzled veteran that never retires.
words.example: He was an inveterate gambler who could not resist a single bet.

words.definition: easily angered; hot-tempered
words.mnemonic: I-RASCAL-ible: the old rascal erupts if you so much as breathe near his lawn.
words.example: Our irascible neighbor yells at squirrels for trespassing.

words.definition: traveling from place to place, never settling
words.mnemonic: His whole life is one endless ITINERARY — new town every single week.
words.example: The itinerant salesman knew every diner along the highway.

words.definition: a planned route or schedule for a trip
words.mnemonic: IT-inerary: IT lists where the trip goes — hour by hour, city by city.
words.example: Our itinerary includes two days in Rome and one in Florence.

words.definition: worn out and bored from too much of something
words.mnemonic: So many JADE jewels that even treasure makes him yawn now.
words.example: After fifty theme parks, the jaded critic yawned at the coaster.

words.definition: Bitter or prejudiced from resentment or envy.
words.mnemonic: Jaundice turns skin yellow; a jaundiced view sees everything soured and sickly.
words.example: Years of betrayal gave her a jaundiced view of politicians.

words.definition: a short pleasure trip
words.mnemonic: Your aunt Jumped in the car for a quick J-AUNT to the beach.
words.example: They took a weekend jaunt to the lake just for fun.

words.definition: to throw off or discard something burdensome
words.mnemonic: A JET tossing cargo out mid-flight so the JET stays up.
words.example: To save the sinking ship, the crew jettisoned the heavy cargo.

words.definition: Extreme, aggressive patriotism favoring war.
words.mnemonic: Crowds chanting 'by jingo!' while waving flags and rattling sabers for war.
words.root: from the phrase 'by jingo' in a warlike song
words.example: The newspaper's jingoism pushed the nation eagerly toward war.

words.definition: joking; playfully humorous
words.mnemonic: JOKE-ular: a molecule made entirely of puns.
words.example: His jocular toast had the whole wedding laughing.

words.definition: Cheerful and light-hearted.
words.mnemonic: 'JOCK-und': a jolly jock cracking jokes and bouncing with good cheer.
words.example: The jocund host kept every guest laughing all evening.

words.definition: feeling or showing great happiness
words.mnemonic: Analogy anchor: joyful people REJOICE — the feeling and the action, same root JOY.
words.example: The joyful crowd rejoiced when the home team scored.

words.definition: feeling triumphant joy; extremely happy
words.mnemonic: Jubilant sounds like a JUBILEE — a whole celebration parade of happiness.
words.example: The jubilant fans stormed the field after the championship win.

words.definition: showing careful, sound judgment; wise
words.mnemonic: JUDGE-icious: pause and rule like a judge before you leap.
words.example: A judicious saver, she kept half her allowance untouched.

words.definition: to place side by side, usually to compare
words.mnemonic: JUXT (just) A POSE: two models posing shoulder to shoulder so you compare the outfits.
words.example: The exhibit juxtaposed baby photos with graduation portraits.

words.definition: sharp or eager; highly perceptive or enthusiastic
words.mnemonic: A keen knife and a keen mind are both SHARP; keen ON something = really want it.
words.example: Her keen eyes spotted the tiny typo instantly.

words.definition: The range of one's knowledge or understanding.
words.mnemonic: 'Beyond my ken': Ken only knows so much, and past that it's beyond Ken.
words.example: Quantum physics lies far beyond the ken of most students.

words.definition: To set alight or arouse a feeling.
words.mnemonic: Strike a match to kindling wood; it kindles a fire and kindles excitement.
words.root: Old Norse kynda (to set fire to)
words.example: Her encouraging words kindled new hope in the discouraged team.

words.definition: A dishonest, unscrupulous man; a rogue.
words.mnemonic: The silent 'k' hides like the knave hides his tricks; a cheating card-deck jack.
words.example: The charming knave swindled the villagers out of their savings.

words.definition: press and work dough with the hands
words.mnemonic: You NEED to KNEAD the dough or the bread won't rise — silent k, busy hands.
words.example: The baker kneaded the dough until it turned smooth and springy.

words.definition: slow bell toll announcing a death or an end
words.mnemonic: The bell KNELLS while mourners KNEEL — same sound, same gloomy scene.
words.example: The factory's closing sounded the death knell for the town.

words.definition: Lacking energy, care, or enthusiasm.
words.mnemonic: 'Lack-a-daisy': too listless to even pick a daisy, sighing 'alack a day.'
words.example: His lackadaisical effort earned him a spot on the bench.

words.definition: a servant who obeys without question
words.mnemonic: A lackey LACKS a spine — the villain's yes-man who fetches everything.
words.example: The count sent a lackey to carry his luggage upstairs.

words.definition: using very few words
words.mnemonic: LACK-onic: he LACKS words. Ask about his whole day: 'Fine.'
words.root: from Laconia (Sparta), whose warriors wasted no words
words.example: Her laconic reply — 'Nope' — ended the interview instantly.

words.definition: To criticize harshly; scold severely.
words.mnemonic: 'Lamb-baste': instead of basting a lamb, you roast someone with a fiery scolding.
words.example: The coach lambasted the players for their careless mistakes.

words.definition: express deep sorrow or grief
words.mnemonic: LA-MENT: sadly singing 'laaaa' over what you MEANT to save but lost.
words.example: The villagers lamented the loss of their ancient oak tree.

words.definition: a device that gives light
words.mnemonic: Analogy anchor: a lamp is USED to illuminate, as a rampart is USED to fortify.
words.example: She switched on the lamp to illuminate her desk.

words.definition: slow, weak, and drained of energy
words.mnemonic: A LANGUID lizard on hot LAND — too melted to move a muscle.
words.example: The languid afternoon heat made even the dogs refuse to move.

words.definition: To grow weak or fail to make progress.
words.mnemonic: 'LANG-uish': a plant with a long anguished droop, wilting for lack of water.
words.example: The forgotten project languished in a drawer for years.

words.definition: generous giving of money or gifts
words.mnemonic: Giving LARGE-SSS: bills hissing out of the billionaire's wallet toward everyone nearby.
words.example: The library's new wing exists thanks to one donor's largess.

words.definition: A state of weariness and lack of energy.
words.mnemonic: 'LASS-i-tude': a tired lass with a listless attitude, slumped and sleepy.
words.example: The summer heat filled everyone with a heavy lassitude.

words.definition: present but hidden; not yet active or visible
words.mnemonic: The talent showed up LATE — LATE-NT, hiding underground until tryouts.
words.example: Her latent talent for chess surfaced the first time she played.

words.definition: to praise highly
words.mnemonic: apLAUD minus the 'ap': LAUD out LOUD.
words.example: Critics lauded the rookie's fearless performance.

words.definition: extravagantly rich or generous; given in great amounts
words.mnemonic: LAVISH rhymes with 'have-ish' — having way more than anyone needs.
words.example: The billionaire threw a lavish party with fireworks and ice sculptures.

words.definition: an area of mowed, cultivated grass
words.mnemonic: Analogy anchor: lawn is to meadow as pool is to lake — the man-made version.
words.example: He mows the lawn every Saturday before it gets too hot.

words.definition: not strict or careful enough; loose
words.mnemonic: Lax is reLAXed rules — a lax guard naps while the museum gets robbed.
words.example: Security at the gate was so lax that anyone could walk in.

words.definition: a stand that holds a speaker's notes or book
words.mnemonic: You give a LECTure from a lecTERN — the stand where the notes live.
words.example: The speaker gripped the lectern and began her speech.

words.definition: a person who makes laws; a member of a legislature
words.mnemonic: LEGIS = law, LATOR = carrier: a LEGISLATOR carries laws into existence.
words.example: The legislator introduced a bill to fund school libraries.

words.definition: unhurried and relaxed
words.mnemonic: LEISURE time has no clock — a leisurely stroll means the ice cream melts and nobody cares.
words.example: They took a leisurely walk along the river after dinner.

words.definition: sluggish and lacking energy
words.mnemonic: One sip of the river LETHE and souls forget everything and drift — pure 'let-her-sleep' energy.
words.example: The flu left him too lethargic to lift the TV remote.

words.definition: lightness or humor, often at a serious moment
words.mnemonic: LEVITY makes the room LEVITATE — a giggle so light it floats up during the sermon.
words.example: The coach's joke added welcome levity to the tense locker room.

words.definition: set free from captivity or control
words.mnemonic: LIBERty-ate: give something its liberty — open the cage and watch it fly.
words.example: The soldiers liberated the town after months of occupation.

words.definition: a large, luxurious chauffeur-driven car
words.mnemonic: The 'rich car' — so long it needs its own zip code, driven by someone in a cap.
words.example: A white limousine pulled up to take the prom-goers downtown.

words.definition: taking words in their exact, actual meaning
words.mnemonic: LITERal sticks to the LETTERs — 'raining cats and dogs' taken literally means falling pets.
words.example: In a literal sense, 'break a leg' would be terrible advice.

words.definition: extremely angry; furious
words.mnemonic: So angry your face turns a LIVID bruise-purple — anger you can see.
words.example: Mom was livid when she found the kitchen covered in flour.

words.definition: extremely talkative
words.mnemonic: A LOud QUACKing duck narrating its entire day — LOQUI means talk.
words.example: My loquacious aunt turned a quick call into a two-hour saga.

words.definition: faithful devotion to a person, group, or cause
words.mnemonic: Opposites anchor: loyalty vs treachery — the faithful dog vs the backstabbing spy.
words.example: The knight proved his loyalty by refusing the enemy's gold.

words.definition: crystal clear; easy to understand; clear-headed
words.mnemonic: Flip on the LUX (light) and the foggy explanation suddenly LIGHTS up.
words.example: Her lucid explanation finally made calculus click for me.

words.definition: childishly grumpy over small annoyances
words.mnemonic: A PET that hisses because dinner is two minutes late.
words.example: The petulant star stormed off when his latte arrived lukewarm.

words.definition: calm and unemotional; hard to excite
words.mnemonic: So calm that even flying PHLEGM can't make him flinch.
words.root: phlegm = the 'calm' humor in old Greek medicine
words.example: The phlegmatic pilot yawned while landing in the storm.

words.definition: a medical doctor
words.mnemonic: Analogy anchor: a physician gives a diagnosis, like a judge gives a verdict.
words.root: Greek physis = nature; one who studies the body
words.example: The physician listened to her heartbeat before making a diagnosis.

words.definition: scientist who studies matter, energy, and forces
words.mnemonic: Don't mix them: physiCIST does physics; physiCIAN fixes your body.
words.example: The physicist explained why the ball curves in flight.

words.definition: long sadly for someone or something absent
words.mnemonic: A lonely PINE tree pining for the forest it was taken from.
words.example: The dog pined by the door for weeks after its owner left.

words.definition: brief but full of meaning; concise and forceful
words.mnemonic: A peach PIT is tiny but packed — a pithy saying is small yet has real punch.
words.example: Her pithy advice fit on a sticky note yet changed my whole approach.

words.definition: turn or rotate around a central point
words.mnemonic: Basketball pivot: one foot planted, the rest of you turns.
words.example: She pivoted on her heel and walked out.

words.definition: to calm someone down; soothe their anger
words.mnemonic: PLAY-cake: hand the screaming toddler cake and press PLAY on cartoons.
words.example: She placated the angry customer with a refund and free cookies.

words.definition: calm and peaceful; not easily upset
words.mnemonic: A placid lake is as smooth and flat as a 'placemat.'
words.example: The placid pond mirrored the mountains without a single ripple.

words.definition: a stale, overused saying with no real meaning
words.mnemonic: PLATE-itude: reheated leftover 'wisdom' served on a plate: 'just be yourself!'
words.example: The speech was nothing but platitudes about following your dreams.

words.definition: an overabundance; more than enough of something
words.mnemonic: 'PLETH-ora' sounds like 'plenty-ora' — way past plenty.
words.example: The buffet offered a plethora of desserts, far more than anyone could eat.

words.definition: all the feathers covering a bird
words.mnemonic: Your pair: foliage is to tree as plumage is to bird — the whole covering.
words.example: The peacock's plumage shimmered green and gold.

words.definition: deeply touching; causing a sharp sense of sadness
words.mnemonic: POIGNant has a hidden POINT — emotion sharp enough to poke your heart.
words.example: The soldier's last letter home was almost too poignant to read.

words.definition: calm, confident self-control
words.mnemonic: Your pair: poise = composure. Picture a gymnast frozen mid-beam, perfectly calm.
words.example: She answered the tough question with surprising poise.

words.definition: self-important; showing off how grand you think you are
words.mnemonic: POMPous = all POMP: parades and trumpets for someone who loves himself.
words.example: The pompous duke expected everyone to bow twice.

words.definition: full of tiny holes that let liquid or air through
words.mnemonic: PORous = full of PORES, like skin or a sponge — stuff seeps through.
words.example: The porous rock soaked up the rain like a sponge.

words.definition: developed or skilled unusually early for one's age
words.mnemonic: Pre-COOKED: ripe before its time, like a five-year-old doing calculus.
words.example: The precocious toddler corrected her father's chess moves.

words.definition: animal that hunts other animals for food
words.mnemonic: Your analogy: predator hunts as inspector examines — the doer and its job.
words.example: The hawk is a patient predator, circling before it strikes.

words.definition: a difficult, tricky situation with no easy way out
words.mnemonic: PREDICAment: even a cat stuck up a tree can't PREDICT a way out.
words.example: Losing his ticket at the gate left him in a real predicament.

words.definition: be a sign or warning of something to come
words.mnemonic: PRE + SAGE: a wise sage speaking BEFORE it happens.
words.root: Latin prae = before + sagire = to sense
words.example: Dark clouds presaged the coming storm.

words.definition: based on likelihood; presumed but not yet confirmed
words.mnemonic: The presumptive winner is PRESUMED to win before it's official.
words.example: She was the presumptive nominee long before the final vote.

words.definition: to dodge the truth; speak evasively
words.mnemonic: Pre-VARY-cate: his story VARIES a little every time you ask.
words.example: Stop prevaricating and tell me who broke the window.

words.definition: easily irritated; touchy (also covered in spines)
words.mnemonic: A prickly person is a human cactus — poke them and you get stung.
words.example: He gets prickly whenever anyone mentions his messy locker.

words.definition: clean and fresh as if new; unspoiled
words.mnemonic: 'Pris-TEEN' — a pristine teen keeps a spotlessly clean room.
words.example: The pristine beach had no footprints or trash anywhere.

words.definition: hardships from lacking basic necessities
words.mnemonic: Privation = deprivation minus the 'de' — being deprived of food, warmth, comfort.
words.example: The settlers endured the privations of a brutal first winter.

words.definition: value highly; treasure
words.mnemonic: SSAT trap: prize as a VERB means to treasure something like a prize.
words.example: She prizes the watch her grandfather gave her.

words.definition: complete honesty and integrity
words.mnemonic: PROBE him all you want; the probe finds nothing dirty.
words.example: The judge's probity made bribing her unthinkable.

words.definition: a natural tendency toward something
words.mnemonic: Pro-CLIFF-ity: you lean off a CLIFF toward what you love.
words.example: The puppy has a proclivity for chewing expensive shoes.

words.definition: wastefully extravagant with money
words.mnemonic: Spends like a PRO: DIGs through the wallet until it's empty.
words.example: The prodigal heir burned through his inheritance in one summer.

words.definition: remarkably great in size, amount, or degree
words.mnemonic: A child 'prodigy' shows prodigious talent — a huge, amazing amount.
words.example: The elephant ate a prodigious quantity of hay each morning.

words.definition: declare openly, sometimes without it being true
words.mnemonic: A proFESSor proFESSes — declares things loudly to the whole class.
words.example: He professed his loyalty to the team, then skipped practice.

words.definition: prediction of how something, especially an illness, will develop
words.mnemonic: PRO (before) + GNOSIS (knowing): the doctor 'knows before' how you'll recover.
words.example: The doctor's prognosis was hopeful: fully healed in six weeks.

words.definition: a prediction or forecast
words.mnemonic: Prognosis wearing a fancy costume — same 'know before' root, longer word.
words.example: Her prognostication that the sequel would flop proved correct.

words.definition: natural tendency to behave a certain way
words.mnemonic: ProPENSity: your PEN naturally leans toward doodling — a built-in tilt.
words.example: My cat has a propensity for knocking cups off tables.

words.definition: giving a good chance of success; favorable
words.mnemonic: 'Pro-PISH-us' — it's PRO you, pushing you toward success.
words.example: Clear skies made it a propitious day to launch the boat.

words.definition: dull and ordinary; lacking imagination
words.mnemonic: PROSE-aic: plain prose, zero poetry; beige oatmeal in word form.
words.example: After the safari, mowing the lawn felt painfully prosaic.

words.definition: success and wealth; thriving condition
words.mnemonic: Your circle: prosperity = success. Think of a PROSPERing shop stacking coins.
words.example: The gold rush brought sudden prosperity to the small town.

words.definition: person or thing authorized to act for another; substitute
words.mnemonic: Voting by proxy: your stand-in raises a hand FOR you while you nap.
words.example: She couldn't attend, so her brother voted as her proxy.

words.definition: showing careful, sensible judgment; cautious
words.mnemonic: A 'prude' is careful about behavior; a prudent person is carefully careful about decisions.
words.example: A prudent saver keeps some money aside for emergencies.

words.definition: childish and silly; immature
words.mnemonic: PEW-rile: boys in the back PEW giggling at every burp.
words.example: The critics dismissed the film's puerile bathroom jokes.

words.definition: eager to fight or argue
words.mnemonic: PUG-nacious: a tiny pug picking fights with every dog at the park.
words.root: Latin pugnare = to fight (cf. pugilist, a boxer)
words.example: The pugnacious rooster charged anyone who entered the yard.

words.definition: raised platform from which a preacher speaks
words.mnemonic: Analogy fuel: preacher is to pulpit as judge is to bench.
words.example: The minister climbed to the pulpit to deliver the sermon.

words.definition: extremely careful about details and correct behavior
words.mnemonic: The PUNCTUATION police: measures every comma with a tiny ruler.
words.example: The punctilious butler ironed the newspaper before serving it.

words.definition: to pierce and make a small hole in something
words.mnemonic: A 'punc'-ture is a 'point' pushed through — think a nail puncturing a tire.
words.root: Latin punctura 'a pricking', from pungere 'to prick'.
words.example: A sharp rock punctured the bicycle tire on the mountain trail.

words.definition: having a sharp, strong smell or taste
words.mnemonic: A 'pun' so sharp it stings — pungent smells sting your nose.
words.example: The pungent onions made everyone's eyes water at the table.

words.definition: a feeling of doubt or uneasiness about an action
words.mnemonic: A QUALM makes you feel un-CALM about what you're about to do.
words.example: She had no qualms about reporting the cheating she saw.

words.definition: a state of uncertainty over a difficult choice
words.mnemonic: In a QUANDARY you keep asking 'WANDA, what do I do?' — stuck between choices.
words.example: Choosing between the two schools left him in a quandary.

words.definition: to isolate to prevent the spread of disease
words.mnemonic: Quarantine comes from 'forty' — ships once waited forty days in isolation before docking.
words.root: Italian quaranta giorni, 'forty days' of ship isolation.
words.example: The sick passengers were quarantined until doctors cleared them.

words.definition: put down or suppress by force; calm
words.mnemonic: QUELL sounds like QUIET + SQUASH: squash the riot until it's quiet.
words.example: Guards were sent to quell the prison uprising.

words.definition: constantly complaining; whiny
words.mnemonic: QUERY-ulous: files a complaint QUERY about everything, even free cake.
words.example: The querulous passenger whined about the seats, the snacks, the clouds.

words.definition: to argue about small, unimportant details
words.mnemonic: 'QUI-bble' sounds like nibble — you nibble at tiny, petty points.
words.example: Instead of solving it, they quibbled over who used the last pen.

words.definition: quiet and inactive; at rest
words.mnemonic: QUIET-escent: a volcano taking a quiet nap, for now.
words.example: The virus can stay quiescent in the body for years.

words.definition: the purest, most perfect example of something
words.mnemonic: The 'fifth essence' — ancients thought a fifth pure element beyond earth/air/fire/water.
words.example: That tiny cafe is the quintessence of Parisian charm.

words.definition: a witty or clever remark
words.mnemonic: A quick 'quip' zips out of your mouth like a whip.
words.example: She answered the tough question with a quip that made everyone laugh.

words.definition: wildly idealistic and impractical
words.mnemonic: Don QUIXOTE charging windmills he is sure are giants.
words.root: from Don Quixote, Cervantes's dreamer knight
words.example: His quixotic plan to end traffic with kites went nowhere.

words.definition: everyday; ordinary and routine
words.mnemonic: Your daily QUOTA of DIshes ANd laundry: the same grind every day.
words.example: The film finds beauty in quotidian chores like folding socks.

words.definition: defensive wall built to protect a fort or city
words.mnemonic: Your analogy: rampart fortifies as lamp illuminates. Also in the anthem — 'o'er the ramparts we watched'.
words.example: Archers lined the ramparts as the enemy approached.

words.definition: smelling or tasting foul from being spoiled
words.mnemonic: RANCid: butter left out so long it makes you RUN from the kitchen.
words.example: The milk turned rancid after a week out of the fridge.

words.definition: deep, long-lasting bitterness or hatred
words.mnemonic: RANK ODOR: old hatred that stinks like week-old fish.
words.example: Years after the lawsuit, the brothers still spoke with rancor.

words.definition: mischievous, playfully dishonest person
words.mnemonic: The little rascal hid your homework and grinned about it.
words.example: That rascal swapped the salt for sugar again.

words.definition: harsh-sounding; loud and rowdy
words.mnemonic: RAW-COUGH: a crowd screaming until their throats are raw.
words.example: Raucous fans shook the bleachers after the winning goal.

words.definition: extremely hungry
words.mnemonic: Your circle: ravenous = voracious. A RAVEN so hungry it eats the birdfeeder too.
words.example: After the swim meet, the team was ravenous.

words.definition: to destroy completely; level to the ground
words.mnemonic: Trap! Sounds like RAISE but means the opposite: razed to the ground.
words.example: Bulldozers razed the old theater in a single afternoon.

words.definition: to scold sharply
words.mnemonic: RE-BOOK: the coach throws the rule BOOK at you, again.
words.example: The teacher rebuked him for texting during the fire drill.

words.definition: stubbornly refusing to obey
words.mnemonic: A mule KICKING its heels back at every single order.
words.example: The recalcitrant mule sat down in the middle of the trail.

words.definition: to take back something you said or believed
words.mnemonic: RE-CAN'T: 'I said it, but now I CAN'T have said it.'
words.root: cantare = to sing (Latin); sing a new tune
words.example: Under pressure, the witness recanted his earlier testimony.

words.definition: container that holds or receives things
words.mnemonic: A RECEPTacle RECEIVES stuff — a trash can receiving your lunch leftovers.
words.example: Place all recyclables in the blue receptacle.

words.definition: given or felt by each side toward the other; mutual
words.mnemonic: Reciprocal is like RECYCLE-back: whatever you send, comes back to you both ways.
words.example: Their reciprocal respect meant each helped the other without keeping score.

words.definition: avoiding other people; living in isolation
words.mnemonic: Your pairing: reclusive = hermit-like. A RECLUSE RE-CLOSES the door on visitors.
words.example: The reclusive author hadn't given an interview in twenty years.

words.definition: morally correct behavior; uprightness
words.mnemonic: RECT = straight, like RECTangle: a spine so upright it's a ruler.
words.example: The librarian's rectitude was legendary; she once returned a found nickel.

words.definition: strongly smelling of something; strongly suggestive
words.mnemonic: 'RED-olent' — a red rose is redolent with sweet perfume.
words.example: The kitchen was redolent of cinnamon and warm baked apples.

words.definition: giving off a strong, unpleasant smell
words.mnemonic: Your pair: reeking goes with rancid. Gym socks reeking so hard the locker wilts.
words.example: He came home reeking of smoke from the campfire.

words.definition: purify; improve by removing flaws
words.mnemonic: RE + FINE: work it again and again until it's fine.
words.example: Crude oil must be refined before it becomes gasoline.

words.definition: safe place; shelter from danger or trouble
words.mnemonic: A REFUGEE runs to a REFUGE — the person is named after the safe place.
words.root: Latin refugium = place to flee back to
words.example: The hikers took refuge in a cave during the storm.

words.definition: entertain or amuse, especially with stories or feasting
words.mnemonic: Regale = treat like ROYALTY (regal): feasts and great stories for a king.
words.example: Grandpa regaled us with tales of his sailing days.

words.definition: a planned routine of diet, exercise, or treatment
words.mnemonic: A regimen rules your day like a REGIME rules a country: 6am run, no sugar, lights out at 10.
words.root: Latin regere, 'to rule' — same root as regime and regulate.
words.example: Her training regimen includes daily swims and a strict diet.

words.definition: feel or show great joy
words.mnemonic: Your pair: rejoice = be joyful. RE-JOY: joy on repeat.
words.example: The whole town rejoiced when the team won the title.

words.definition: to push down to a lower rank or place
words.mnemonic: Losing soccer teams get RELEGATED: sent DOWN a league, to the kids' table.
words.example: The old champion was relegated to the bench all season.

words.definition: objects surviving from the past; ancient remains
words.mnemonic: Your analogy: relics are to archaeologists as fossils are to paleontologists.
words.example: The dig uncovered relics from a Roman marketplace.

words.definition: to give up or let go of something
words.mnemonic: 'Re-LINK-wish' — you unlink your grip and wish it goodbye.
words.example: The champion had to relinquish her title after the injury.

words.definition: cure or treatment that fixes an illness or problem
words.mnemonic: Your chain: remedy is the general cure; antidote is the cure for poison specifically.
words.example: Hot tea with honey is her favorite remedy for a sore throat.

words.definition: think or talk fondly about the past
words.mnemonic: ReMINISce: your MIND hits rewind and replays the good old days.
words.example: The old friends reminisced about their middle school pranks.

words.definition: to break a promise or go back on a deal
words.mnemonic: RE-NEG: flipping your YES into a NEGative after shaking hands.
words.example: He reneged on his promise to split the lottery winnings.

words.definition: to feel sorry for a wrong and resolve to do better
words.mnemonic: RE-PENT: you PEN-t up your regret, then re-PEN a better path — a sinner trying to get better.
words.example: He came to repent his harsh words and apologized sincerely.

words.definition: feeling or showing sorrow for wrongdoing
words.mnemonic: RE-PENT-ant: you keep re-paying penance because you regret what you did.
words.root: Latin paenitere = to regret (same root as 'penitence').
words.example: The repentant thief returned the wallet with a written apology.

words.definition: completely filled; well stocked
words.mnemonic: RE-PLATE: went back for a third PLATE, stuffed to the eyebrows.
words.root: plere = to fill (Latin, as in complete)
words.example: The pantry was replete with enough snacks for a blizzard.

words.definition: deserving strong blame or criticism
words.mnemonic: If police 'apprehend' you, your act was reprehensible — blame-worthy.
words.example: Cheating on the exam was a reprehensible thing to do.

words.definition: a temporary delay of punishment or trouble
words.mnemonic: RE-BREATHE: the pardon call comes at 11:59 and you breathe again.
words.example: The rain gave the wilting garden a brief reprieve.

words.definition: to blame or express disapproval of someone
words.mnemonic: Re-apPROACH: she keeps approaching you AGAIN with that disappointed look.
words.example: Her silence reproached him more than any lecture could.

words.definition: to reject or refuse to accept completely
words.mnemonic: Re-PEW!-diate: shove it away yelling 'PEW, it stinks!'
words.example: The candidate repudiated the endorsement from the hate group.

words.definition: causing strong disgust
words.mnemonic: It RE-PULSES you — pushes you back like a wrong magnet pole.
words.example: A repulsive smell of rotten eggs drifted from the drain.

words.definition: to officially cancel or take back
words.mnemonic: Re-SCISSOR: the contract gets scissored back out of existence.
words.example: The school rescinded its offer after discovering the plagiarism.

words.definition: a supply kept back for future use
words.mnemonic: RE-SERVE: food you can serve again later because you saved some.
words.example: The hikers kept a reserve of water for the final climb.

words.definition: the place where a person lives
words.mnemonic: RESIDEnts live in a residence — where you re-SIT (sit back down) every night.
words.root: Latin residere = to sit back, remain.
words.example: The mayor's official residence overlooks the town square.

words.definition: firmly determined; unwavering
words.mnemonic: Resolute = full of resolve: a New Year's resolution you actually keep.
words.root: Latin resolvere, 'to loosen, decide' — settled firmly on a decision.
words.example: She remained resolute about finishing the marathon despite the pain.

words.definition: clever at finding ways to solve problems
words.mnemonic: Full of resources — MacGyver fixing a boat with gum and a shoelace.
words.example: The resourceful camper built a shelter from branches and a tarp.

words.definition: a short period of rest or relief
words.mnemonic: 'RES-pit' — a rest pit stop, a quick break from the race.
words.example: The rain gave the firefighters a brief respite from the heat.

words.definition: shining brilliantly; dazzling in appearance
words.mnemonic: It literally hides 'splendid' — resplendent is super-splendid and shining.
words.example: The queen appeared resplendent in a gold, jewel-covered gown.

words.definition: restless and hard to control; impatient
words.mnemonic: Trap! Sounds like RESTful but means CAN'T rest: a horse dancing at the gate.
words.example: The restive crowd began chanting when the concert ran late.

words.definition: reluctant to speak; keeping quiet
words.mnemonic: The RETIcent turtle keeps its head, and its words, inside.
words.root: tacere = to be silent (Latin, like taciturn)
words.example: Usually chatty, he turned reticent when asked about the trophy.

words.definition: to improve or redesign something old
words.mnemonic: RE-VAMP: even a vampire's crumbling castle needs a makeover.
words.root: vamp = the upper front of a shoe; to revamp was to patch a shoe.
words.example: The school revamped its cafeteria with new tables and a salad bar.

words.definition: to respect deeply; regard with awe
words.mnemonic: You wrote it ten times — revere REVERE like fans revere Paul Revere.
words.root: Latin revereri = to stand in awe of.
words.example: Villagers revere the old teacher who taught three generations.

words.definition: to attack with harsh, abusive language
words.mnemonic: To revile is to call someone 'vile' over and over.
words.example: Fans reviled the referee after the unfair final call.

words.definition: to mock or make fun of cruelly
words.mnemonic: RIDICULE = making someone look RIDICULous with your laughter.
words.example: The other kids ridiculed his homemade costume until it won first prize.

words.definition: widespread; full of something, usually bad
words.mnemonic: 'Rife' rhymes with 'life' — the rumor spread through all of school life.
words.example: The abandoned house was rife with rats and cobwebs.

words.definition: a formal ceremony, often religious or traditional
words.mnemonic: A rite must be done RIGHT — every step of the ceremony in order.
words.example: Lighting the lanterns is an ancient rite performed every spring.

words.definition: payment to a creator for each use or sale of their work
words.mnemonic: Your note nails it: 'copyright money' — the author gets paid like a king.
words.example: The songwriter earns a royalty every time her song is streamed.

words.definition: basic or elementary; only partly developed
words.mnemonic: 'ROOT-imentary' — only the roots, the basic beginnings.
words.example: He had only a rudimentary grasp of Spanish, just a few words.

words.definition: to regret bitterly
words.mnemonic: 'You'll RUE the day!' — the classic villain line means you'll regret it.
words.example: He rued the day he sold his grandfather's watch.

words.definition: to think deeply about something over time
words.mnemonic: Cows ruminate: they chew cud over and over — chewing on a thought.
words.example: She ruminated on the coach's advice all weekend.

words.definition: a clever trick meant to deceive
words.mnemonic: A ruse is a trick you USE — the fake pizza delivery was just a ruse.
words.example: The surprise party was hidden behind the ruse of a study session.

words.definition: excessively sweet or sentimental to the point of being fake
words.mnemonic: Saccharin is artificial sweetener — saccharine praise is fake-sweet and sickly.
words.example: The movie's saccharine ending left the critics rolling their eyes.

words.definition: having sharp judgment; wise from experience
words.mnemonic: SAGE-acious: the wise old SAGE in every movie who saw it all coming.
words.example: The sagacious coach predicted exactly how the rival team would play.

words.definition: promoting health or well-being
words.mnemonic: SALAD-brious: a giant salad bar of pure health.
words.example: Doctors praised the mountain town's salubrious air.

words.definition: a soothing ointment for wounds; anything that soothes
words.mnemonic: Salve SAVES your skin — an ointment that rescues a scrape.
words.root: Latin salvus = safe, healthy (same family as 'salvation').
words.example: She rubbed a cooling salve on the burn.

words.definition: acting morally superior in a hypocritical, showy way
words.mnemonic: SAINT + MONEY: preaches like a saint while pocketing your money.
words.example: His sanctimonious lecture on honesty rang hollow after he cheated.

words.definition: cheerfully optimistic, even in bad situations
words.mnemonic: Blood (sanguis) in your cheeks: rosy-faced and sure it'll work out.
words.example: Despite failing the first quiz, she remained sanguine about the semester.

words.definition: grimly mocking or cynical
words.mnemonic: A SARDINE smirking up from the can: 'nice life, buddy.'
words.example: He greeted the losing team with a sardonic slow clap.

words.definition: gloomy, sullen, or grim in temperament
words.mnemonic: Born under cold, slow planet Saturn — a Saturn-faced grump who never smiles.
words.root: from Saturn, planet once thought to cause gloom
words.example: His saturnine expression made the whole room feel heavy.

words.definition: harshly and bitingly critical
words.mnemonic: SCA(L)DING words: a review so hot it leaves burn marks.
words.example: The critic's scathing review closed the play within a week.

words.definition: to throw or move things in many different directions
words.mnemonic: Analogy logic: scatter is the action, dispersal is the result.
words.example: The wind scattered the leaves across the yard.

words.definition: spread out over an area in a disorderly way
words.mnemonic: Scattered = strewn: socks strewn all over a messy room.
words.example: Scattered papers covered the floor after the fan tipped over.

words.definition: a shallow wound made by rubbing against something rough
words.mnemonic: The 'scr-' sound is the sound of skin meeting pavement.
words.example: He got a scrape on his knee sliding into second base.

words.definition: extremely careful to be honest and thorough
words.mnemonic: A scruple was a sharp pebble in your shoe; you feel every tiny wrong.
words.example: A scrupulous accountant, she reported even the smallest error.

words.definition: close, careful examination
words.mnemonic: SCREW-tiny: examining tiny screws under a magnifying glass.
words.example: The referee's call came under intense scrutiny after the replay.

words.definition: making abusive, vulgar, or defamatory attacks on someone
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'squirrel' — picture a foul-mouthed squirrel flinging dirty insults from a tree.
words.example: The tabloid printed scurrilous rumors about the mayor's private life.

words.definition: showing steady, tireless dedication
words.mnemonic: SEE-DEW-lous: up studying so early you watch the dew form.
words.example: His sedulous note-taking filled six notebooks by December.

words.definition: to carefully choose from a group
words.mnemonic: Your pair: select = cull — both mean picking out the best (or worst).
words.example: The coach selected twelve players for the traveling team.

words.definition: to isolate or set apart from others
words.mnemonic: A jury put in 'sequence' away in a separate locked room, cut off from the world.
words.example: The jury was sequestered in a hotel to avoid outside influence.

words.definition: excessively eager to serve and obey
words.mnemonic: SERVant + VILE: bows so low his nose polishes your shoes.
words.example: The servile assistant agreed with every word the director said.

words.definition: something fake presented as genuine
words.mnemonic: A sham is a SHAMeful fake — a scam without the c.
words.example: The 'antique' vase turned out to be a sham from a factory.

words.definition: embarrassed from shame; also meekly timid and submissive
words.mnemonic: Caught in a lie, he gave a sheepish grin like a timid sheep.
words.example: He looked sheepish after forgetting his own mother's birthday.

words.definition: to cover, conceal, or hide from view
words.mnemonic: Fog shrouds the hills, wrapping them like a burial cloth.
words.example: Thick mist shrouded the mountain peak all morning.

words.definition: easy to understand; not complicated
words.mnemonic: Antonym anchor from your notes: simple is to complicated as divisive is to unity.
words.example: She gave a simple answer to a very tricky question.

words.definition: to satisfy or quench a thirst or desire
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'lake' — you'd drink a whole lake to slake your thirst.
words.example: Nothing could slake his thirst for adventure and distant places.

words.definition: a zigzag downhill race between poles, especially in skiing
words.mnemonic: Say it while swerving: sla-LOM, sla-LOM — left, right, left between the flags.
words.example: She clipped the last gate but still won the slalom.

words.definition: false spoken statements that damage someone's reputation
words.mnemonic: S-LANDER: lies that land on someone and stain their name.
words.example: Calling him a cheater without proof was pure slander.

words.definition: messy and careless in appearance or habits
words.mnemonic: SLOB-enly: how a slob dresses — stained shirt, untied shoes.
words.example: His slovenly desk was buried under crumbs and crumpled papers.

words.definition: an insulting remark that harms someone's reputation
words.mnemonic: A slur SLings mud — one ugly word that stains a name.
words.example: The candidate apologized for the slur against his opponent.

words.definition: to damage someone's reputation with false charges
words.mnemonic: Your arrow chain: smear -> damage -> reputation — like wiping mud on a portrait.
words.example: The article tried to smear the scientist with made-up quotes.

words.definition: showing eager, attentive care or concern for someone
words.mnemonic: A solicitor (lawyer) fussing anxiously over your case — overly caring about every detail.
words.example: The nurse was solicitous, checking on every patient twice an hour.

words.definition: the state of being alone
words.mnemonic: SOL- means alone, like a SOLO performance or SOLitaire — a one-player game.
words.example: He read in the solitude of the empty library.

words.definition: having a deep, full, resonant sound
words.mnemonic: 'Sonoro' hides 'sonic/sound' — a booming snore that resonates through the whole house.
words.example: The preacher's sonorous voice filled the vast cathedral.

words.definition: causing sleep or drowsiness
words.mnemonic: SO-PORridge-ific: a warm-porridge lecture... zzz.
words.example: The soporific lecture had half the class nodding off.

words.definition: a singer with the highest vocal range
words.mnemonic: Soprano sings SO-PRA-NOOO — the note so high only dogs finish it.
words.root: Italian sopra = above — the voice above all others.
words.example: The soprano hit the final high note and the crowd erupted.

words.definition: marked by strict simplicity, discipline, and lack of luxury
words.mnemonic: The ancient Spartans lived in bare, tough, no-luxury barracks.
words.example: The monk's spartan room held only a cot and a candle.

words.definition: seeming true or fair but actually false
words.mnemonic: SPECIAL + suspicious: looks special, smells fishy.
words.example: The ad's specious claims fooled thousands of buyers.

words.definition: a humorous imitation that mocks something
words.mnemonic: Even the word sounds silly — a SPOOF makes fun by copying.
words.example: The skit was a spoof of every superhero movie ever made.

words.definition: happening at irregular, scattered intervals
words.mnemonic: 'Spore-adic' — mushroom spores pop up here and there at random spots.
words.example: Sporadic gunfire echoed through the night, then long silences.

words.definition: moving nimbly and energetically, especially when old
words.mnemonic: Spry sounds like 'spring' — a spry person springs around quickly.
words.example: Despite being ninety, the spry grandmother still danced at every wedding.

words.definition: to urge or prompt into action
words.mnemonic: A rider's spur pokes the horse to go — a spur pokes you into action.
words.example: The losing score spurred the team to play harder.

words.definition: fake; not genuine
words.mnemonic: A cowboy selling fake gold SPURS.
words.example: Experts exposed the painting as a spurious copy.

words.definition: extremely dirty and unpleasant from neglect or poverty
words.mnemonic: A SQUID rotting in an alley: slimy, filthy, forgotten.
words.example: The inspectors condemned the squalid apartment building.

words.definition: a sudden violent gust of wind, often with rain or snow
words.mnemonic: A SQUALL makes sailors SQUEAL — a sudden blast of wind hits the boat.
words.example: A sudden squall nearly capsized the small sailboat.

words.definition: to waste something recklessly or foolishly
words.mnemonic: 'Squid-wander' — a squid wanders off squirting away all its precious ink.
words.example: He squandered his inheritance on fast cars and gambling.

words.definition: serious, dull, and set in one's ways
words.mnemonic: He STAYED home alphabetizing his socks on prom night.
words.example: The staid banker wore the same gray suit for thirty years.

words.definition: loyal, reliable, and sturdily dependable
words.mnemonic: 'Stall-wart' — a sturdy wart that stays firmly put and never leaves your hand.
words.example: A stalwart supporter, she attended every rally for thirty years.

words.definition: to stop the flow of something, especially blood
words.mnemonic: STANCH the blood: make the flow STAND still — press hard and it stops.
words.example: The medic pressed a cloth to stanch the bleeding.

words.definition: unwilling to spend or give; not generous
words.mnemonic: Ask a stingy person for a dollar and the answer STINGs.
words.example: He was too stingy to tip even a single dollar.

words.definition: a fixed regular payment, often for interns or trainees
words.mnemonic: STudent-Interns-PENDing real jobs get a stiPEND.
words.root: Latin stips (small payment) + pendere (to pay out).
words.example: The summer intern received a small monthly stipend.

words.definition: showing no emotion, especially during pain or hardship
words.mnemonic: Stoic = face of STONE — steps on a Lego, doesn't even blink.
words.root: From the Stoics, Greek philosophers who preached calm endurance.
words.example: She stayed stoic while the nurse stitched the cut.

words.definition: calm and showing little emotion
words.mnemonic: STONE-solid face: shows nothing, ever.
words.example: The guard remained stolid while tourists tried to make him laugh.

words.definition: requiring great effort or energy
words.mnemonic: STRAIN-uous: so hard you strain every muscle.
words.example: The strenuous hike left everyone gasping at the summit.

words.definition: scattered untidily over an area
words.mnemonic: Clothes strewn everywhere — a room that looks like a laundry explosion.
words.example: Confetti lay strewn across the gym floor after the dance.

words.definition: loud, harsh, and grating
words.mnemonic: A triDENT dragged screeching across a chalkboard.
words.example: The strident alarm made conversation impossible.

words.definition: strict, demanding, and precise in requirements
words.mnemonic: 'String-ent' — rules pulled tight like a taut string that refuses to stretch.
words.example: The lab enforces stringent safety rules with zero exceptions.

words.definition: a room where an artist works
words.mnemonic: Analogy classic: painter is to studio as chef is to kitchen.
words.root: Latin studere = to be diligent (same root as 'study').
words.example: The painter's studio smelled of turpentine and fresh canvas.

words.definition: to block or frustrate someone's plans
words.mnemonic: Old golf term: an opponent's ball STuck in your line, so you can't putt.
words.example: Bad weather stymied the climbers' attempt at the summit.

words.definition: to bring under complete control; conquer
words.mnemonic: Shoved UNDER (sub) the YOKE (jugum) like an ox pulling a plow.
words.example: The empire subjugated every village along the coast.

words.definition: of the highest, awe-inspiring quality or beauty
words.mnemonic: SUB-LIMIT? No — ABOVE the limit: beauty beyond ordinary, like dry ice rising as vapor.
words.example: The sunset over the canyon was simply sublime.

words.definition: a trick used to hide something or escape blame
words.mnemonic: Flee (fuge) UNDERground (subter): the secret subway getaway.
words.example: She used subterfuge, a fake meeting, to plan the surprise party.

words.definition: delicate and not obvious; hard to notice
words.mnemonic: Subtle is so quiet even its 'b' is silent.
words.example: She gave a subtle nod that only her partner noticed.

words.definition: expressed clearly in very few words
words.mnemonic: SUC-CINCHED with a belt: no extra words hanging out.
words.example: Her succinct answer covered everything in two sentences.

words.definition: to give in to pressure; to die from illness or disaster
words.mnemonic: SUCC-UMB: you're sucked under and go numb — you give in.
words.root: Latin sub- (under) + cumbere (lie down) — to lie down under.
words.example: He finally succumbed to the temptation of the last cookie.

words.definition: silently bad-tempered, sulky, and gloomy
words.mnemonic: 'Sulk-in' — a teenager sulking in the corner, silent and gloomy.
words.example: The sullen boy refused to speak throughout the entire dinner.

words.definition: splendid and expensive-looking; luxurious
words.mnemonic: A SUMPTUOUS feast costs a huge SUM — every dish dripping with luxury.
words.example: They served a sumptuous banquet with seven courses.

words.definition: behaving as if everyone else is beneath you
