words.mnemonic: Cilium = eyebrow: one SUPER-raised eyebrow, judging you forever.
words.example: The supercilious waiter sneered at our pronunciation of the menu.

words.definition: more than is needed; unnecessary
words.mnemonic: The tub SUPER over-FLOWS: way more than anyone needs.
words.example: Cut the superfluous adjectives; the essay is over the limit.

words.definition: to ask or beg humbly and earnestly for something
words.mnemonic: A 'supplicant' kneels — 'supple' knees bent low to beg.
words.root: Latin supplicare 'to kneel down, plead' (sub 'under' + plicare 'to bend').
words.example: The peasants supplicated the king to lower their crushing taxes.

words.definition: to hold back or stop by force
words.mnemonic: SUB-PRESS: press it down under — like suppressing a laugh in class.
words.example: She suppressed a giggle during the solemn ceremony.

words.definition: an excessive amount, especially of food or drink
words.mnemonic: SUR-FEAST: a feast that goes over the top — too much of everything.
words.example: A surfeit of Halloween candy left him with a stomachache.

words.definition: bad-tempered, rude, and unfriendly
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'snarly' — a snarling dog, grumpy and rude to everyone.
words.example: The surly waiter slammed our plates down without a word.

words.definition: a feeling caused by something unexpected
words.mnemonic: Ladder of shock: surprise is the bottom rung, astonishment the top — degree up.
words.example: To her surprise, the test was postponed a week.

words.definition: done secretly or stealthily to avoid notice
words.mnemonic: A SURREPTITIOUS snack: you sneak chips so quietly even the bag doesn't crinkle.
words.example: He took a surreptitious glance at her test answers.

words.definition: a large moving group, especially of insects
words.mnemonic: Group words: a swarm of bees is like a mob of people — same analogy pattern.
words.example: A swarm of bees drifted across the meadow toward the hive.

words.definition: someone who flatters powerful people for personal gain
words.mnemonic: A SICK-O FAN of the boss who applauds his sneezes.
words.example: Surrounded by sycophants, the king never heard honest advice.

words.definition: a brief summary of a longer work
words.mnemonic: Syn-OPsis = one quick OPtical sweep of the whole plot — the plot in a glance.
words.example: The back cover gives a one-paragraph synopsis of the novel.

words.definition: forbidden by custom or social rules
words.mnemonic: Ta-BOO! Society boos you the moment you mention the forbidden topic.
words.root: from Tongan tabu, a Polynesian word for sacred and forbidden
words.example: In some households, discussing salaries at dinner is taboo.

words.definition: understood without being openly stated
words.mnemonic: Tacere = be silent: the deal everyone knows but nobody says aloud.
words.example: Their tacit agreement was that nobody mentioned the lost trophy.

words.definition: habitually silent; saying very little
words.mnemonic: TACIT-turn: at his turn to speak, he stays tacit — silent again.
words.root: Latin tacere, to be silent (same root as 'tacit')
words.example: The taciturn farmer answered every question with a single nod.

words.definition: to tease by showing something desirable but out of reach
words.mnemonic: Tantalus stood in water that shrank away whenever he bent to drink — tantalized forever.
words.example: The bakery's smell tantalized the hungry children pressed against the window.

words.definition: equal in effect to; virtually the same as
words.mnemonic: TANT-AMOUNT = 'as much amount': it adds up to the exact same thing.
words.root: tant amunter = amount to as much (Old French)
words.example: Skipping rehearsal is tantamount to quitting the play.

words.definition: showy but cheap and of poor quality
words.mnemonic: Cheap 'Audrey' costume jewelry — flashy and glittery but totally worthless.
words.example: She wore tawdry plastic jewelry that glittered under the cheap lights.

words.definition: reckless boldness; shameless nerve
words.mnemonic: TEH-MERIT-y: zero merit, all nerve — asks for a raise while napping at work.
words.example: He had the temerity to correct the judge's grammar.

words.definition: a person's natural disposition or usual mood
words.mnemonic: Your temperaMENT is your MENTal weather — sunny, stormy, or calm by nature.
words.root: Latin temperare, to mix in proper proportion
words.example: Her calm temperament made her the ideal referee for sibling fights.

words.definition: self-restraint, especially in eating or drinking
words.mnemonic: Don't confuse: temperaMENT = your mood; temperANCE = your self-control at the dANCE buffet.
words.root: Latin temperare, to restrain or moderate
words.example: He praised temperance and never took a second helping of dessert.

words.definition: holding firmly; stubbornly persistent
words.mnemonic: Hangs on with 'ten' claws — ten-acious grip that never lets go.
words.example: A tenacious reporter, she chased the story for three straight years.

words.definition: very weak or flimsy; barely holding together
words.mnemonic: Tenuis = thin: a whole TENT held up by one thread.
words.example: Her tenuous excuse fell apart under one question.

words.definition: lukewarm; lacking enthusiasm
words.mnemonic: TEA-pid: tea left out for an hour — technically warm, totally meh.
words.example: The proposal received a tepid response and quietly died.

words.definition: brief and abrupt in speech or writing
words.mnemonic: TERSE is the 'k.' of vocabulary words.
words.example: His terse reply, 'Fine,' ended the discussion.

words.definition: to prevent someone's plan from succeeding
words.mnemonic: A WART on the villain's trigger finger thwarted his whole evil plan.
words.example: A sudden storm thwarted their plan to camp on the ridge.

words.definition: showing nervousness or fear
words.mnemonic: TIMid Timothy Mouse, trembling at his own shadow.
words.example: The timorous kitten hid whenever the doorbell rang.

words.definition: a long, angry speech of criticism
words.mnemonic: He got TIRED during the coach's endless angry tirade — a TIRing tirADE.
words.root: French tirer, to draw out — a speech drawn out long
words.example: The coach launched into a tirade after the careless third loss.

words.definition: to work long and hard; exhausting labor
words.mnemonic: Farmers toil in the soil from sunrise to sunset.
words.example: They toiled all summer to build the barn by hand.

words.definition: a state of physical or mental sluggishness
words.mnemonic: A TORPedo-shaped sloth that absolutely refuses to launch.
words.example: A heavy lunch sank the office into afternoon torpor.

words.definition: a violent, rushing stream of water or forceful outpouring
words.mnemonic: A torrent tears through the valley like a raging river.
words.example: A torrent of rainwater swept the small bridge away.

words.definition: full of twists and turns; overly complicated
words.mnemonic: Torquere = twist: a road so twisty it's torture for your GPS. (Not 'torturous'!)
words.example: The tortuous mountain road took three hours to climb.

words.definition: to move or stand unsteadily, about to fall
words.mnemonic: A TOT taking first steps totters — wobbly, tipping, about to plop down.
words.example: The old ladder tottered as he reached for the top shelf.

words.definition: easily offended or irritated
words.mnemonic: One light touch and he explodes — touchy people bruise at a tap.
words.example: He's touchy about his height, so nobody jokes about it.

words.definition: easy to manage, control, or teach
words.mnemonic: TRACTOR-able: steers as easily as a tractor in a straight furrow.
words.example: The new puppy proved surprisingly tractable during training.

words.definition: going beyond ordinary limits; surpassingly great
words.mnemonic: TRANS-ASCEND: it climbs across and above everything ordinary — beyond the top.
words.example: The violinist gave a transcendent performance that silenced the hall.

words.definition: to move from one place, school, or position to another
words.mnemonic: Analogy pair: you transfer schools the way you emigrate countries — same 'change' relation.
words.example: She will transfer to a new school across town this fall.

words.definition: to hold motionless with fascination; also to pierce through
words.mnemonic: Trans-FIX: fascination FIXES you in place, as if a spear pinned you there.
words.example: The audience sat transfixed as the magician floated above the stage.

words.definition: to violate a rule or moral boundary
words.mnemonic: 'Trans-gress' — to step (gress) across (trans) a line you must not cross.
words.example: Anyone who transgresses the club's rules is immediately expelled.

words.definition: lasting only a short time
words.mnemonic: A TRAIN passenger: on the platform at 3:05, gone at 3:06.
words.example: Fame from the viral video proved transient.

words.definition: painful, exhausting labor or suffering
words.mnemonic: TRAVAIL = TRAVEL gone terribly wrong: dragging luggage uphill in the rain for days.
words.root: Old French travailler, 'to toil' — from a Latin torture device.
words.example: After years of travail, the settlers finally built a thriving town.

words.definition: a distorted, mocking imitation of something
words.mnemonic: A TRAgic VEST: dressing justice in a clown's vest — a mocking, false version.
words.root: Latin trans + vestire, to dress up in disguise
words.example: The rigged election was a travesty of democracy.

words.definition: betrayal of trust; deceitful disloyalty
words.mnemonic: Opposite anchor: treachery is loyalty flipped — the trusted friend who sells you out.
words.root: Old French trichier, to trick or cheat
words.example: The spy's treachery cost his own side the battle.

words.definition: trembling or quivering, often from nervousness
words.mnemonic: A 'tremor' makes your hands and voice quiver and shake.
words.example: In a tremulous voice, the nervous witness described what she saw.

words.definition: trembling fear or anxiety about what is coming
words.mnemonic: Trepidare = tremble: knees knocking outside the principal's office.
words.example: She opened the report card with trepidation.

words.definition: something given or said to show respect or admiration
words.mnemonic: Your note nails it: tribute = payment made in respect — you 'pay' tribute.
words.root: Latin tribuere, to give or allot
words.example: The team wore black armbands as a tribute to their retired coach.

words.definition: unimportant, trivial, or of little value
words.mnemonic: A tiny 'trifle' dessert — small, light, and of little importance.
words.example: They argued for hours over a trifling sum of money.

words.definition: worn out from overuse; unoriginal
words.mnemonic: So overused it's TIREd — the phrase itself needs a nap.
words.example: The essay's trite ending ruined an original argument.

words.definition: aggressively defiant; quick to argue or fight
words.mnemonic: A monster-TRUCK driver revving his engine, looking for a fight.
words.example: The truculent defender picked up three fouls in five minutes.

words.definition: to announce loudly and proudly
words.mnemonic: Imagine announcing your test score through an actual trumpet — loud, proud, everyone hears.
words.example: The mayor trumpeted the news of the new stadium downtown.

words.definition: to shorten something by cutting off a part
words.mnemonic: Chop the 'trunk' of a tree — truncate it short.
words.example: The editor truncated the long essay to fit one page.

words.definition: loud, confused noise and disorder
words.mnemonic: TUM-ble + MULTitude: a multitude tumbling over each other — pure noisy chaos.
words.root: Latin tumultus, an uproar (related to tumere, to swell)
words.example: In the tumult after the winning goal, she lost her hat.

words.definition: swollen; pompously overblown in style
words.mnemonic: A TURkey stuffed until it bursts — like the essay's puffed-up prose.
words.example: The scholar's turgid prose buried a simple idea.

words.definition: depravity; wicked or vile behavior
words.mnemonic: 'Turp-itude' — chugging turpentine is a vile, depraved thing to do.
words.example: The senator resigned in disgrace after his moral turpitude was exposed.

words.definition: a cruel ruler who wields power oppressively
words.mnemonic: Contrast anchor: tyrant vs meek — the meek bow, the tyrant makes them bow.
words.example: The tyrant jailed anyone who dared question his decrees.

words.definition: a beginner or novice
words.mnemonic: TIE-ROW: the rookie who can't even tie a row of laces yet.
words.example: A tyro at chess, he still beat two seasoned players.

words.definition: present or found everywhere at once
words.mnemonic: U-BIKE-WIT-US — that one rental bike is on literally every street corner.
words.example: Smartphones are so ubiquitous that even toddlers swipe at screens.

words.definition: offense or resentment taken at a slight
words.mnemonic: UMBRella RAGE — you borrowed my umbrella and bent it, and I am deeply offended.
words.root: umbra = shadow (Latin); a shadow falls over your mood
words.example: She took umbrage at being left off the invitation list.

words.definition: not embarrassed, ashamed, or apologetic
words.mnemonic: un-a-BASHED = not bashed down by shame; struts in grinning, totally unashamed.
words.root: a- (intensive) + abash, from Old French esbahir 'to astound'
words.example: She gave an unabashed grin after cracking the terrible pun.

words.definition: unrestrained; not controlled or held back
words.mnemonic: A horse with no bridle bolts wildly, reined in by nothing.
words.root: bridle, the strap used to control a horse
words.example: His unbridled enthusiasm knocked over three chairs and a lamp.

words.definition: lacking manners or refinement; crude
words.mnemonic: Your chain works: uncouth → barbarian → rude — a barbarian slurping soup at a wedding.
words.example: His uncouth table manners embarrassed everyone at the banquet.

words.definition: excessively flattering; oily and insincere in manner
words.mnemonic: From UNCTION (ointment) — a salesman so greasy his compliments leave oil stains.
words.example: The unctuous waiter praised every dish we ordered, angling for a tip.

words.definition: deliberately restrained; not showy or exaggerated
words.mnemonic: Opposite anchor: understated : exaggerated = subtle : obvious — saying less on purpose.
words.example: Her understated dress stood out among all the sequined gowns.

words.definition: move in a smooth, wave-like motion
words.mnemonic: UNDA the sea — picture a stadium crowd doing the wave forever.
words.example: Heat made the air undulate above the desert sand.

words.definition: the state of being joined together as one
words.mnemonic: UNI = one: unity glues the group into one; divisive saws it apart.
words.example: The crisis brought a rare sense of unity to the town.

words.definition: messy and uncared for; not combed
words.mnemonic: 'Kempt' literally meant combed — un-KEMPT hair has a bird nesting in it.
words.example: After a week of camping, his unkempt hair stuck out wildly.

words.definition: absolute; not lessened or softened at all
words.mnemonic: To mitigate is to soften; un-mitigated means nothing softened it, a total disaster.
words.example: The rained-out picnic was an unmitigated disaster from start to finish.

words.definition: never stopping or easing; refusing to yield
words.mnemonic: It never RELENTS — rain that won't take even a coffee break.
words.example: The unrelenting rain flooded the fields for a week straight.

words.definition: having no conscience or moral principles; dishonest
words.mnemonic: A scrupulus was a sharp pebble of guilt in your sandal — this person's shoe is empty.
words.example: The unscrupulous dealer sold broken bikes as brand new.

words.definition: to remove someone from a position of power
words.mnemonic: Literal: pull the chair out from under the champion — knocked off the seat of power.
words.example: The young senator hoped to unseat the longtime incumbent.

words.definition: impossible to defend or keep holding
words.mnemonic: Un-TEN-able — a fort you couldn't hold for even TEN seconds.
words.example: Faced with the new evidence, his theory became untenable.

words.definition: unfavorable, improper, or unexpectedly awkward
words.mnemonic: un-TOWARD = not moving toward the good; things turned the wrong way.
words.example: Nothing untoward happened at the party, despite the ominous forecast.

words.definition: to scold or criticize sharply
words.mnemonic: Mom yanks your BRAID UP while scolding you — upbraid = scold hard.
words.example: The captain upbraided the crew for ignoring the safety checks.

words.definition: polished and sophisticated in manner
words.mnemonic: URBAN-e: city-smooth manners — the big-city gentleman who charms every room.
words.root: Latin urbanus, of the city (city dwellers seen as refined)
words.example: The urbane diplomat put everyone at ease with effortless charm.

words.definition: to seize power or a position without right
words.mnemonic: YOU-SLURP the king's throne like a milkshake — grab it though it's not yours.
words.root: Latin usurpare, to seize for use
words.example: The duke plotted to usurp his brother's throne.

words.definition: designed for usefulness rather than beauty
words.mnemonic: A utility room is plain and handy, all use and no prettiness.
words.example: The utilitarian backpack had no style but a dozen handy pockets.

words.definition: to keep switching between opinions; be indecisive
words.mnemonic: Vacillate sounds like 'oscillate' — your mind swings back and forth like a pendulum.
words.example: He vacillated between the two colleges until the deadline day.

words.definition: empty-headed; showing no thought or intelligence
words.mnemonic: A VACUUM between the ears — nothing in there but dust bunnies.
words.example: Her vacuous stare showed she hadn't heard a single word.

words.definition: showing courage and determination
words.mnemonic: You drilled it in capitals: VALIANT = COURAGEOUS — think Prince Valiant charging in.
words.example: The firefighters made a valiant effort to save the barn.

words.definition: to deliberately damage or deface property
words.mnemonic: Named for the Vandals, the tribe that trashed Rome — vandals still trash property.
words.root: from the Vandals, a Germanic tribe that sacked Rome
words.example: Someone vandalized the bus stop with spray paint overnight.

words.definition: dull and flat; lacking liveliness or flavor
words.mnemonic: Soda whose fizz eVAPorated — flat, warm, and deeply boring.
words.example: The interview was full of vapid answers about 'following your dreams.'

words.definition: having patches or streaks of different colors
words.mnemonic: A 'variety' of colors; a variegated leaf is patched green and cream.
words.example: The variegated ivy climbed the wall in green and cream stripes.

words.definition: to boast about proudly
words.mnemonic: 'Vaunt' rhymes with 'flaunt' — he vaunts his trophies to everyone.
words.example: He loved to vaunt his grades to anyone who would listen.

words.definition: to change direction suddenly
words.mnemonic: VEEEER! — the sound of tires as the car swerves off its line.
words.example: The cyclist veered left to avoid the fallen branch.

words.definition: showing strong, intense feeling; forceful
words.mnemonic: VEHE-MENT: a vehicle with its engine roaring — full force, full feeling.
words.example: She was vehement in her denial of the accusation.

words.definition: willing to be bribed; corrupt for money
words.mnemonic: VENd-AL: Official Al has a vending-machine slot in his palm — insert cash, receive favor.
words.example: The venal inspector approved the unsafe building for a fee.

words.definition: a superficial or deceptive outer appearance
words.mnemonic: Furniture veneer is a thin fancy layer hiding cheap wood beneath.
words.example: Beneath his veneer of calm, he was panicking about the test.

words.definition: worthy of respect through age or dignity
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'veteran-able' — an old veteran everyone salutes and respects.
words.example: The venerable professor had taught at the school for fifty years.

words.definition: to regard with deep respect; revere
words.mnemonic: VENERate the VENERable ancestors — bow deeply to the temple elders.
words.example: The villagers venerate the old teacher who built their school.

words.definition: punishment inflicted in retaliation for a wrong; revenge
words.mnemonic: He sought vengeance to avenge his fallen brother.
words.example: Swearing vengeance, the hero hunted down the traitor.

words.definition: truthful; accurate and honest
words.mnemonic: VERAcious = VERY truthful. Don't confuse with voracious (very hungry)!
words.example: The judge trusted the veracious witness completely.

words.definition: truthfulness; accuracy of a statement
words.mnemonic: VER = true, as in VERify and VERdict — a fact-checker's favorite noun.
words.example: The lawyer questioned the veracity of the witness's story.

words.definition: using far more words than needed
words.mnemonic: VERB OVERDOSE — a speaker who swallowed a thesaurus and won't stop.
words.example: His verbose essay took ten pages to make one point.

words.definition: green and lush with vegetation
words.mnemonic: 'Verd' echoes French vert (green); a verdant lawn is very green.
words.example: After the rains, the once-brown hills turned verdant and soft.

words.definition: the edge or point just before something happens
words.mnemonic: On the verge = toes over the edge of the diving board, one breath from jumping.
words.example: The tired hikers were on the verge of giving up.

words.definition: the appearance or quality of being true or real
words.mnemonic: 'Veri' (truth) + 'similitude' (likeness) = looks convincingly like the truth.
words.example: The novel's rich detail gave the fantasy world surprising verisimilitude.

words.definition: a container for holding liquids; also a ship
words.mnemonic: Two holders in one: a vessel holds water inside it, or holds sailors on it.
words.example: She poured the broth into a clay vessel to cool.

words.definition: small remaining trace of something that has vanished
words.mnemonic: A VEST is the vestige of a three-piece suit that lost its jacket and pants.
words.example: The crumbling wall is the last vestige of the ancient fort.

words.definition: experienced through imagining someone else's experience
words.mnemonic: Watching your friend ride the coaster and screaming anyway from the bench.
words.example: She felt vicarious joy watching her sister accept the medal.

words.definition: speak about someone cruelly and unfairly; defame
words.mnemonic: VILLAIN-ify — draw devil horns on someone's yearbook photo with words.
words.example: The tabloids vilified the actor over an invented rumor.

words.definition: clear from blame; prove right after doubt
words.mnemonic: The WIN inside vindicate: the security tape finally proves you didn't do it.
words.example: New fingerprints vindicated the janitor after years of suspicion.

words.definition: seeking revenge; spiteful
words.mnemonic: Vin Diesel with a grudge and a laminated revenge list.
words.example: A vindictive classmate spread lies after losing the election.

words.definition: extremely poisonous or bitterly hostile
words.mnemonic: A VIRUs gone viral — spreading poison through every comment section.
words.example: The senator faced virulent attacks from both parties.

words.definition: bitterly harsh or caustic in tone
words.mnemonic: Vitriol was the old name for sulfuric acid — a vitriolic comment melts your inbox.
words.example: His vitriolic review called the film 'an insult to popcorn.'

words.definition: bitterly abusive; harshly and insultingly critical
words.mnemonic: 'Vitu-' sounds like spitting venom; a vituperative rant spews insults.
words.example: His vituperative review left the young chef in tears.

words.definition: lively and full of spirit
words.mnemonic: 'Viva' means life (viva la vida); a vivacious person bursts with life.
words.example: Her vivacious laughter filled the room the moment she arrived.

words.definition: loud and insistent in voicing opinions
words.mnemonic: VOICE + FEROCIOUS — opinions delivered at lion volume.
words.example: Vociferous fans drowned out every word from the announcer.

words.definition: changing rapidly and unpredictably; easily becoming dangerous
words.mnemonic: Your chemistry note scales up: what evaporates in a flash — liquids, moods, markets — is volatile.
words.root: Latin volare, to fly — it flies off quickly
words.example: His volatile temper made the negotiations tense.

words.definition: the power of using one's own will; a choice
words.mnemonic: 'Vol-' as in voluntary; acting of your own volition means by choice.
words.example: She left the team of her own volition, not because anyone pushed her.

words.definition: talking rapidly, easily, and nonstop
words.mnemonic: Volvere = to roll — words ROLL out nonstop, a human bowling lane of chatter.
words.example: Our voluble guide narrated every single mile of the bus ride.

words.definition: occupying much space; very large or lengthy
words.mnemonic: Turn the 'volume' way up; voluminous robes have enormous volume.
words.example: The lawyer carried voluminous files stuffed with years of records.

words.definition: having a huge appetite; extremely eager
words.mnemonic: Your pair holds: voracious = ravenous — a vacuum-mouthed reader devouring books whole.
words.root: Latin vorare, to devour (as in carnivore, devour)
words.example: A voracious reader, she finished three novels every week.

words.definition: pale and appearing ill or weary
words.mnemonic: 'Wan' rhymes with 'gone' — the color has gone from a wan face.
words.example: After the long flu, he looked wan and thinner than usual.

words.definition: shrink or weaken gradually
words.mnemonic: The waning moon is a cookie someone nibbles smaller each night.
words.example: His enthusiasm waned after the third hour of drills.

words.definition: deliberate and unprovoked; recklessly cruel
words.mnemonic: Knocking over someone's WONTON soup for zero reason — pure pointless destruction.
words.example: The vandals showed wanton disregard for the new playground.

words.definition: coax someone with flattery to get something
words.mnemonic: A NEEDLE of flattery poking until you cave: 'pleeease, you're the BEST.'
words.example: He wheedled his sister into doing his chores again.

words.definition: sharpen; stimulate appetite or interest
words.mnemonic: A WHETstone sharpens knives; a movie trailer whets your appetite till your mouth goes WET.
words.example: The free sample whetted our appetite for the full meal.

words.definition: playfully fanciful and unpredictable
words.mnemonic: On a WHIM she painted the cow purple — because it was Tuesday.
words.example: The garden's whimsical statues included a frog in a top hat.

words.definition: the cord in a candle or lamp that burns
words.mnemonic: The WICK is what you'd flick with a match — the candle's burning string.
words.example: Trim the wick before lighting the candle again.

words.definition: clever at tricking others; cunning
words.mnemonic: WILE E. Coyote — endless sneaky schemes (minus the success rate).
words.example: The wily fox slipped past three locked henhouse doors.

words.definition: to sift out and separate the good from bad
words.mnemonic: The wind blows the chaff away, leaving only the good grain behind.
words.example: The judges winnowed a thousand entries down to three finalists.

words.definition: charming in a sweet, innocent way
words.mnemonic: A WINning SMile that WINs SOME hearts — the puppy-dog look that melts strict librarians.
words.example: Her winsome grin instantly won over the stern principal.

words.definition: thin, faint, and delicate
words.mnemonic: A WISP of smoke — so thin one whisper of wind erases it.
words.example: Wispy clouds streaked the sky just before sunset.

words.definition: full of gentle longing or sad yearning
words.mnemonic: WISHful with a sigh — staring out a rainy window at old summer photos.
words.example: He grew wistful looking through pictures from summer camp.

words.definition: shriveled and wrinkled with age
words.mnemonic: The 900-year-old WIZard's face: raisin-level wrinkles, still wise.
words.example: A wizened farmer told stories of the town's first harvest.

words.definition: a person's customary habit or usual practice
words.mnemonic: As is his wont, he 'won't' change his usual habit.
words.example: She rose at dawn, as was her wont, to jog before work.

words.definition: sophistication and experience gained from the wider world
words.mnemonic: A WORLD traveler gains worldliness — polished, cultured, and hard to surprise.
words.example: Years abroad gave her a worldliness the sheltered students lacked.

words.definition: a ghost or ghostly apparition
words.mnemonic: 'Wraith' rhymes with 'faith' — a pale wraith drifts through the haunted hall.
words.example: A wraith seemed to drift across the fog-covered graveyard.

words.definition: to pull or take away by force
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'wrestle' — you wrest the keys away in a struggle.
words.example: She wrested the remote from her brother's tight grip.

words.definition: to twist the body, as in pain
words.mnemonic: 'Writhe' like a worm on the sidewalk twisting in pain.
words.example: He writhed on the field, clutching his sprained ankle.

words.definition: dryly humorous, often with gentle irony
words.mnemonic: Humor as dry as RYE bread, served with a smile twisted to one side.
words.example: She gave a wry smile when the 'shortcut' added an hour.

words.definition: fear or hatred of foreigners or strangers
words.mnemonic: XENO = alien (think Xenomorph) + PHOBIA = fear: fearing anyone 'alien.'
words.example: Rising xenophobia made the new immigrant families feel unwelcome.

words.definition: join together, often under a shared burden
words.mnemonic: The wooden YOKE locks two oxen into one team — yoked means stuck together.
words.example: The farmer yoked the oxen together before plowing the field.

words.definition: unsophisticated person from the countryside
words.mnemonic: A YOKEL yodels beside his YOKEd oxen, straw dangling from his teeth.
words.example: City critics unfairly dismissed the young mayor as a yokel.

words.definition: amusingly unconventional and silly
words.mnemonic: Named for Zanni, the goofy clown-servant doing ridiculous stage stunts.
words.root: Italian Zanni, a comic servant character
words.example: His zany antics turned an ordinary lecture into a comedy show.

words.definition: great energy and enthusiasm for a cause
words.mnemonic: A ZEALot SEALs every envelope for the campaign at 2 a.m. — that fire is zeal.
words.example: She tackled the fundraiser with the zeal of a true believer.

words.definition: person with extreme, fanatical devotion to a cause
words.mnemonic: ZEAL by the LOT — camps outside the store a month before the launch.
words.example: Only a zealot would tattoo the team logo on his forehead.

words.definition: the spirit or mood of a particular era
words.mnemonic: German Zeit (time) + Geist (spirit) = literally the spirit of the time.
words.example: The song perfectly captured the restless zeitgeist of the 1960s.

words.definition: highest point; peak of success or power
words.mnemonic: The noon sun sits at its ZENITH, straight overhead — its opposite is the nadir.
words.example: Winning the national title marked the zenith of her career.

words.definition: gentle, mild breeze
words.mnemonic: Zephyros, the west-wind god, exhales sooo softly — a blimp named Zephyr drifting by.
words.example: A cool zephyr drifted through the open classroom window.

words.definition: to give up or deny something to yourself, especially a right, comfort, or desire
words.mnemonic: ab- (away) + negate = to negate away your own wants; a monk abnegates pleasure
words.example: The devoted nurse chose to abnegate her free time so she could care for the sickest patients.

words.definition: to officially cancel or do away with a law, agreement, or right
words.mnemonic: 'ab-ROW-gate' — the mayor rowed away from the old gate rule and abrogated it
words.example: The new government moved quickly to abrogate the unfair treaty signed by its predecessor.

words.definition: to approach and speak to someone boldly or aggressively
words.mnemonic: sounds like 'a cost' — being accosted on the street costs you your peace
words.root: Latin accostare 'to come up to the side' (costa = rib, side)
words.example: A stranger tried to accost us near the station, demanding money and blocking our path.

words.definition: sharply bitter or unpleasant in taste or smell
words.mnemonic: 'acrid' hides 'acid' — an acrid smell burns your nose like acid
words.example: The acrid smoke from the burning tires stung our eyes and made us cough.

words.definition: sharpness or keenness of thought, vision, or hearing
words.mnemonic: 'a-CUTE-ity' — a cute-sharp mind; acute means sharp, so acuity is sharpness
words.example: The chess master's mental acuity let him see the winning move ten turns ahead.

words.definition: a short, well-known saying that expresses a general truth
words.mnemonic: 'a-DAGE' sounds like 'a sage' — a wise sage speaks an adage
words.example: My grandmother loved the old adage that a stitch in time saves nine.

words.definition: to give a faint outline of something, or to foreshadow it
words.mnemonic: 'a-DUMB-rate' contains 'umbra' (shadow) — you cast a shadowy sketch of an idea
words.example: In the opening chapter the author adumbrates the tragedy that will strike the family later.

words.definition: happening by chance rather than by plan; accidental
words.mnemonic: shares a root with 'adventure' — adventures are adventitious, unplanned events
words.example: Their meeting was purely adventitious: two strangers who happened to share an umbrella in the rain.

words.definition: a natural attraction, liking, or close connection to something
words.mnemonic: 'a-FIN-ity' — fish with fins have an affinity for water
words.example: She felt a strong affinity for the ocean and spent every summer near the coast.

words.definition: to calm or reduce a fear, worry, or pain
words.mnemonic: 'a-LAY' — you lay a worry to rest to allay it
words.root: Old English alecgan 'to lay down, put aside'
words.example: The pilot's steady voice helped allay the passengers' fears during the turbulence.

words.definition: an official pardon that frees people from punishment for past offenses
words.mnemonic: shares roots with 'amnesia' — both mean forgetting; amnesty means the law 'forgets' your crime
words.example: The new leader granted amnesty to political prisoners jailed under the old regime.

words.definition: something or someone that is intensely disliked or condemned
words.mnemonic: 'a-NATH-uh-muh' — a nasty thing you curse and reject
words.root: Greek anathema 'a thing devoted to evil, accursed'
words.example: For a lifelong vegetarian, the idea of a steak dinner was simple anathema.

words.definition: strong hostility or hatred toward someone
words.mnemonic: 'animo' (spirit) turned sour — a spirited grudge; think an 'animal-hostility'
words.example: Years of rivalry created a deep animosity between the two neighboring families.

words.definition: something that differs from what is normal or expected
words.mnemonic: 'a-NOM-uh-lee' — 'a-normal-y'; not normal, an anomaly
words.example: A daytime temperature below freezing in July is a real anomaly for this region.

words.definition: extremely old or hopelessly out of date
words.mnemonic: ante- (before) + diluvium (the biblical flood) — older than Noah's flood
words.example: My uncle still uses an antediluvian phone with a rotary dial and a cord.

words.definition: of doubtful truth or authenticity, though widely repeated
words.mnemonic: related to the Apocrypha, texts of questioned authenticity — apocryphal stories are questionable
words.example: The tale that Washington chopped down a cherry tree is almost certainly apocryphal.

words.definition: very fitting or appropriate to the situation
words.mnemonic: looks like 'opposite' but means the opposite of off-topic — perfectly on point
words.example: Her quotation from the poem was so apposite that the whole room nodded in agreement.

words.definition: a person with the power to settle a dispute or judge what is correct
words.mnemonic: root of 'arbitrate' — an arbiter arbitrates a fight
words.example: For decades the critic was the ultimate arbiter of taste in the fashion world.

words.definition: a perfect or original model on which others are based; a typical example
words.mnemonic: 'arch-' (chief, first) + type — the first, chief type of something
words.example: The wise old wizard is a familiar archetype in fantasy novels.

words.definition: very enthusiastic or passionate about something
words.mnemonic: 'ARD' like 'ardor' and 'burning' — an ardent fan burns with excitement
words.example: An ardent supporter of the team, she never missed a single home game.

words.definition: a damaging or false remark that attacks someone's reputation
words.mnemonic: 'a-SPER-shun' — to spray dirt on someone's name; you 'cast aspersions'
words.example: He resented the aspersions cast on his honesty by his rivals.

words.definition: to make something weaker, thinner, or less intense
words.mnemonic: 'a-TEN-you-ate' — thinning something down to a tenth; makes it thin and weak
words.example: Thick walls attenuate the noise from the busy street outside.

words.definition: to be a sign of a future outcome; to predict or foretell
words.mnemonic: an 'augur' was a Roman fortune-teller; think 'auger' drilling into the future
words.root: Latin augur 'a diviner who read omens'
words.example: The team's strong preseason performance seemed to augur a successful year ahead.

words.definition: to state firmly that something is true
words.mnemonic: 'a-VER' — to make something VERy true by stating it; 'ver' = truth (verify)
words.example: The witness continued to aver that she had seen the car run the red light.

words.definition: a hobby or activity done for pleasure, apart from one's regular work
words.mnemonic: NOT a vocation (job) — an avocation is what you do away from your calling, for fun
words.example: A lawyer by profession, he pursued painting as a serious avocation on weekends.

words.definition: a statement accepted as obviously true without needing proof
words.mnemonic: 'AX-iom' — a truth so sharp and solid it cuts through debate like an ax
words.root: Greek axioma 'that which is thought worthy, self-evident'
words.example: It is a basic axiom of geometry that two parallel lines never meet.

words.definition: away from the planned or correct course; wrong
words.mnemonic: 'a-RYE' — like a wry, twisted smile, things went crooked and awry
words.example: Their carefully organized trip went completely awry when the flight was canceled.

words.definition: a person who abandons a religion, cause, or set of beliefs
words.mnemonic: apo- (away) + stasis (standing) — one who stands away from the faith
words.root: Greek apostates 'one who stands off, deserter'
words.example: Once a loyal member, he was branded an apostate after publicly rejecting the party.

words.definition: light, playful, teasing conversation
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'bad-in-age' — but it's actually a GOOD-natured, witty back-and-forth chat.
words.example: The two old friends filled dinner with easy badinage, trading jokes and gentle insults all night.

words.definition: a person's specific area of skill, knowledge, or authority
words.mnemonic: A 'bailiff's' 'wick' (district) — the turf that's officially his to handle.
words.example: Medieval history is her bailiwick, so we always ask her about knights and castles.

words.definition: to hesitate or refuse to go along with something
words.mnemonic: A horse comes to a 'wall' (balk) and won't jump — it stops short.
words.example: She was excited to skydive until she reached the open door and balked at the last second.

words.definition: a stronghold or a place that firmly defends a belief or way of life
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'bash-tion' — a fort built to bash back attackers.
words.example: The tiny library remained the last bastion of quiet in the noisy, crowded town.

words.definition: showing complete, blissful, peaceful happiness
words.mnemonic: 'Beati' = blessed (as in 'the Beatitudes') — a blessed, glowing smile.
words.example: A beatific smile spread across the baby's face as she drifted off to sleep.

words.definition: wet, dirty, and messy, as if dragged through mud
words.mnemonic: 'Be-DRAGGLED' — literally dragged through puddles until soaked and grimy.
words.root: be- + draggle 'to trail in the wet.'
words.example: The bedraggled cat crept in from the storm, fur plastered flat and dripping.

words.definition: something enormous and powerful
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'be-HE-moth' — imagine a moth grown to the size of a whale.
words.root: Hebrew behemot, a giant beast in the Book of Job.
words.example: The new shopping mall was a behemoth that dwarfed every other building downtown.

words.definition: to explain or repeat something far more than necessary
words.mnemonic: To put too much 'labor' into a point — beating it long after we get it.
words.example: We understood the rule after one example, but the teacher kept belaboring it for ten minutes.

words.definition: to complain about or express deep sorrow over something
words.mnemonic: 'Be' + 'moan' — to moan and groan over a loss.
words.example: He bemoaned his bad luck after missing the bus by only a few seconds.

words.definition: puzzled, confused, or lost in thought
words.mnemonic: NOT 'amused' — 'be-mused' means your muse (mind) is muddled and confused.
words.example: She looked bemused by the strange directions, unsure whether to turn left or right.

words.definition: doing good and being generous to others
words.mnemonic: 'Bene' = good (like 'benefit') + 'ficent' = doing — doing good.
words.example: The beneficent stranger paid for the family's groceries and quietly walked away.

words.definition: to leave something to someone, especially in a will
words.mnemonic: 'Be-QUEATH' — a queen 'bequeaths' her crown to the next heir.
words.root: Old English becwethan 'to say, leave by will.'
words.example: Her grandmother chose to bequeath the antique piano to whichever grandchild learned to play.

words.definition: to damage or stain someone's reputation
words.mnemonic: 'Be-SMIRCH' sounds like 'smear' + 'smudge' — smearing dirt on a good name.
words.example: He spread rumors to besmirch his rival's honest reputation before the election.

words.definition: to cheat someone out of money or something owed
words.mnemonic: Rhymes with 'milk' — a con artist 'milks' victims for money, bilking them dry.
words.root: Origin uncertain, first used as a cribbage term.
words.example: The scam artist bilked elderly customers out of thousands with a fake charity.

words.definition: flattering or coaxing speech used to persuade someone
words.mnemonic: Contains 'bland' — but it's smooth, buttery flattery meant to win you over.
words.example: No amount of blandishment could persuade the strict coach to cancel practice.

words.definition: unimpressed or indifferent because of overfamiliarity
words.mnemonic: 'Blah-zay' — a bored shrug that says 'blah, seen it all before.'
words.example: After traveling the world, she was oddly blase about visiting yet another famous landmark.

words.definition: something that spoils, ruins, or causes decay
words.mnemonic: Rhymes with 'bite' — a bite of rot that spreads through a crop until it's ruined.
words.root: Origin uncertain; first used of plant disease.
words.example: Litter and broken windows were a blight on the once-beautiful neighborhood.

words.definition: slightly salty and unpleasant to drink (of water)
words.mnemonic: 'Brackish' sounds like 'brine' + 'yuckish' — salty, murky water.
words.example: Where the river met the sea, the water turned brackish and undrinkable.

words.definition: to react with sudden anger or irritation
words.mnemonic: Like an angry cat whose 'bristles' (stiff hairs) stand up when provoked.
words.example: He bristled at the suggestion that he had cheated to win the match.

words.definition: to think anxiously or gloomily about something for a long time
words.mnemonic: A hen 'broods' over her eggs for ages — you brood over a worry the same way.
words.example: Instead of moving on, he sat alone all evening brooding over the argument.

words.definition: to strike or knock about repeatedly and forcefully
words.mnemonic: Not the food line — 'BUFF-it' means to be beaten, as waves buffet a boat.
words.example: Fierce winds buffeted the little plane, tossing it from side to side.

words.definition: annoyingly arrogant and pushy
words.mnemonic: 'Bump' + 'pompous' — someone who bumps ahead of everyone, full of himself.
words.example: The bumptious new intern interrupted the manager to explain how the company should run.

words.definition: to polish something until it shines by rubbing
words.mnemonic: 'Burn-ish' — rub metal so hard it heats up and gleams.
words.example: He burnished the old trophy until it gleamed like new.

words.definition: to support, strengthen, or reinforce something
words.mnemonic: A stone 'buttress' props up a cathedral wall — to buttress is to prop up an argument.
words.example: She buttressed her essay with statistics and quotes from three experts.

words.definition: excessively complicated, intricate, or full of hidden schemes
words.mnemonic: From the maze-like politics of the ancient Byzantine Empire — dizzyingly complex.
words.root: From Byzantium, famed for elaborate intrigue.
words.example: The company's byzantine refund process required six forms and four different offices.

words.definition: bad-tempered and irritable
words.mnemonic: From 'bile,' the bitter fluid — a bilious person is bitter and sour.
words.root: Latin bilis 'bile,' once thought to cause anger.
words.example: The bilious old landlord snapped at anyone who dared knock on his door.

words.definition: extravagantly ornate, elaborate, and highly decorated
words.mnemonic: 'Ba-ROKE' — so over-decorated it looks like it cost a fortune (til you're broke).
words.example: The palace ceiling was a baroque swirl of gold leaf, angels, and painted clouds.

words.definition: a wild, drunken celebration or party
words.mnemonic: Named for Bacchus, the Roman god of wine — his parties were wild.
words.example: What began as a quiet dinner turned into a noisy bacchanal that lasted till dawn.

words.definition: a large group or collection of people or things
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'bevy of beauties' — a whole flock gathered together.
words.root: Origin uncertain; long used for a flock of birds.
words.example: A bevy of reporters swarmed the actor the moment he left the theater.

words.definition: A false statement made to damage someone's reputation; slander.
words.mnemonic: 'CALL you MEAN names' behind your back — that lie is calumny.
words.example: The candidate's rivals spread calumny about her, inventing scandals that never happened.

words.definition: Tending to find trivial faults; eager to raise petty objections.
words.mnemonic: A CAPTIOUS critic tries to CAPTURE every tiny mistake.
words.root: Latin captiosus, from capere 'to seize (on faults).'
words.example: His captious remarks about the comma placement ignored the essay's brilliant argument.

words.definition: Swiftness of movement or action; speed.
words.mnemonic: A CELERITY racer moves fast — think 'accelerate.'
words.example: The waiter cleared the table with such celerity that we barely noticed him.

words.definition: Severely critical of others; quick to condemn.
words.mnemonic: A CENSOR censors things; a censorious person censures everyone.
words.root: Latin censorius, from censor, the official who judged public morals.
words.example: Her censorious aunt disapproved of everything, from his haircut to his handshake.

words.definition: A feeling of distress or embarrassment caused by failure or humiliation.
words.mnemonic: You SHAH-GRIN a fake grin to hide your chagrin after tripping onstage.
words.example: To his chagrin, he realized he had been mispronouncing his boss's name for months.

words.definition: Cautiously reluctant; wary about doing something.
words.mnemonic: Be CHARY — it sounds like 'wary,' and means the same.
words.root: Old English cearig, 'sorrowful, careful'; related to 'care.'
words.example: Burned by a past scam, she was chary of any deal that sounded too good.

words.definition: A person who falsely claims special knowledge or skill; a fraud.
words.mnemonic: A CHARLATAN 'charms' you with a 'tan' from a fake miracle cream.
words.example: The so-called doctor was a charlatan who sold colored water as a cure for every disease.

words.definition: The use of many vague words when few clear ones would do; roundabout speech.
words.mnemonic: CIRCUM (around) + LOCUTION (speech) = talking in circles around the point.
words.example: Instead of admitting he forgot, he offered a paragraph of circumlocution about 'unforeseen memory circumstances.'

words.definition: Taking a long and indirect route rather than the direct one.
words.mnemonic: A CIRCUIT loops around; a circuitous path loops the long way.
words.example: He took a circuitous route home to avoid the crowded main streets.

words.definition: A concluding section that rounds off a piece of music, writing, or an event.
words.mnemonic: A CODA is the CODE that signals 'the end' at the close of a song.
words.example: The novel's brief coda revealed what happened to the characters ten years later.

words.definition: To think deeply and carefully about something; to ponder.
words.mnemonic: COGITATE shares 'cog' with brain gears turning — 'I think, therefore cogito.'
words.example: She sat by the window to cogitate on the choice before making a decision.

words.definition: Secret cooperation between people in order to deceive or cheat others.
words.mnemonic: CO- (together) + 'illusion' — two people team up to create a lie.
words.root: Latin colludere, 'to play together, act in concert' (col- + ludere 'to play').
words.example: The two bidders were caught in collusion, agreeing in advance to keep the price low.

words.definition: A feeling of guilt or unease about doing something wrong.
words.mnemonic: A COMPUNCTION 'punctures' your conscience like a small prick of guilt.
words.example: He felt not the slightest compunction about eating the last slice of cake.

words.definition: A large and destructive fire.
words.mnemonic: CON + FLAG + RATION — imagine flags going up in a giant blaze.
words.root: Latin conflagratio, from conflagrare 'to burn up' (com- + flagrare 'to blaze').
words.example: A single dropped lantern started the conflagration that consumed half the city.

words.definition: Pleasant and agreeable, especially because of shared interests or temperament.
words.mnemonic: CONGENIAL people are 'genial' (kind) together, sharing the same 'genes' of taste.
words.example: They found the small bookstore a congenial place to spend a rainy afternoon.

words.definition: An expert judge in matters of taste, such as art, food, or wine.
words.mnemonic: A CONNOISSEUR truly 'knows' — the word hides the French 'connaître,' to know.
words.example: As a connoisseur of tea, she could name the region a leaf came from by its aroma.

words.definition: To declare something sacred or dedicate it to a solemn purpose.
words.mnemonic: CON + SACRED — to make fully sacred.
words.example: The old chapel was consecrated more than two hundred years ago.

words.definition: To interpret or understand a statement or action in a particular way.
words.mnemonic: How you CONSTRUE a sentence is how you 'construct' its meaning.
words.root: Latin construere, 'to build up, arrange' (com- + struere 'to pile').
words.example: Her silence could be construed as either agreement or quiet disapproval.

words.definition: To plan or create something cleverly, often by scheming or with difficulty.
words.mnemonic: To CONTRIVE is to 'con' up a clever 'drive' of a plan.
words.example: He contrived a reason to leave the party early without offending the host.

words.definition: Insulting or contemptuous language or treatment.
words.mnemonic: CONTUMELY sounds like 'contempt' plus 'unruly' — rude, scornful treatment.
words.example: The proud knight would rather duel than endure such public contumely.

words.definition: A small, exclusive group of people with shared interests.
words.mnemonic: A COTERIE gathers in a cozy 'coat' room — a tight little inner circle.
words.root: French coterie, originally an association of tenant farmers.
words.example: The poet was surrounded by a coterie of admirers who echoed his every opinion.

words.definition: To give approval to or tolerate something.
words.mnemonic: A teacher won't COUNTENANCE cheating — she won't put a calm 'face' on it (countenance also means face).
words.root: Latin continentia, 'demeanor, bearing,' via Old French.
words.example: The headmaster would not countenance any bullying, no matter the excuse.

words.definition: Belief in or acceptance of something as true.
words.mnemonic: CREDENCE shares 'cred' with 'credit' and 'believe' — giving something your belief.
words.example: New evidence lent credence to the theory that scientists had once dismissed.

words.definition: The most important or decisive point of a problem or issue.
words.mnemonic: The CRUX is the 'cross'-roads where the whole issue is decided (Latin crux = cross).
words.root: Latin crux, 'cross'; a difficulty that 'crosses' one.
words.example: The crux of the debate was whether freedom or safety mattered more.

words.definition: Excessive greed, especially for wealth or possessions.
words.mnemonic: CUPIDITY sounds like 'Cupid,' but it's desire for gold, not love — greedy craving.
words.root: Latin cupiditas, 'desire, greed,' from cupere 'to desire.'
words.example: His cupidity led him to cheat even his own relatives out of their inheritance.

words.definition: A person or thing that is the center of attention or admiration.
words.mnemonic: CYNOSURE was once the North Star ('dog's tail' in Greek) that all eyes followed for guidance.
words.root: Greek kynosoura, 'dog's tail,' the constellation containing the North Star.
words.example: In her bright red gown, she was the cynosure of every eye at the ball.

words.definition: To restrict or limit something within bounds.
words.mnemonic: CIRCUM (around) + SCRIBE (draw) — to draw a boundary line around and hold in.
words.root: Latin circumscribere, 'to draw a line around' (circum + scribere 'to write').
words.example: The new rules sharply circumscribed the students' freedom to leave campus.

words.definition: Disgusting or sickening because of excessive sweetness or sentimentality.
words.mnemonic: A CLOYING dessert 'clogs' your throat with too much sugar.
words.root: From Middle English acloien, 'to clog, obstruct.'
words.example: The movie's cloying happy ending was so sugary it felt fake.

words.definition: Roomy and comfortable; having plenty of space.
words.mnemonic: A COMMODIOUS home has room for all your 'commodities' (goods).
words.example: The new apartment was commodious enough to hold all their books and still feel open.

words.definition: Lacking sensitivity, refinement, or intelligence; coarse and rude.
words.mnemonic: CRASS rhymes with 'brass' — loud, cheap, and lacking class.
words.example: Making a joke at the funeral was a crass thing to do.

words.definition: confident, stylish, and charming in a carefree way
words.mnemonic: 'DEBONAIR' sounds like 'the bon air' (French: good air) — a man with such good, easy air about him.
words.root: From Old French 'de bon aire' meaning 'of good disposition/breeding.'
words.example: The debonair spy strolled into the ballroom, flashing an easy smile at everyone he passed.

words.definition: marked by moral decline or excessive, self-indulgent luxury
words.mnemonic: 'DECADEnt' contains 'decade' — imagine an empire rotting after a DECADE of over-the-top feasts.
words.root: From Latin 'decadere,' to fall away or decay.
words.example: The decadent banquet featured gold-dusted desserts while the peasants outside went hungry.

words.definition: to damage someone's good reputation by saying false things about them
words.mnemonic: 'DE-FAME' = to take away (de-) someone's FAME or good name.
words.root: From Latin 'diffamare,' to spread bad reports; fama = fame/reputation.
words.example: The tabloid tried to defame the senator by printing rumors that were later proven false.

words.definition: no longer existing, active, or in use
words.mnemonic: 'DE-FUNCT' — a machine that can no longer FUNCTion is defunct.
words.root: From Latin 'defungi,' to finish or discharge; functus = performed.
words.example: The old railway line has been defunct for decades, its tracks now buried under weeds.

words.definition: to do something one considers beneath one's dignity, as if doing a favor
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'DANE' — a haughty Great Dane will barely deign to greet a smaller dog.
words.root: From Latin 'dignari,' to deem worthy; dignus = worthy.
words.example: The famous actor would not even deign to answer questions from the small-town reporter.

words.definition: to describe or outline something precisely
words.mnemonic: 'DE-LINE-ate' — to draw the LINEs that clearly outline something.
words.root: From Latin 'delineare,' to sketch out; linea = line.
words.example: The manual carefully delineates each step so no beginner can get confused.

words.definition: a leader who wins support by stirring up people's emotions and prejudices rather than using reason
words.mnemonic: 'DEMA-GOGUE' — think of a 'drama' 'rogue' whipping up the crowd's feelings.
words.example: The demagogue promised easy answers to complex problems, blaming outsiders for every hardship.

words.definition: to object or hesitate, especially politely or mildly
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'the more' — the more you push, the more she demurs and holds back.
words.root: From Latin 'demorari,' to delay or linger.
words.example: When asked to lead the risky expedition, the cautious officer began to demur.

words.definition: an inhabitant or regular occupant of a particular place
words.mnemonic: 'DEN-izen' — a creature that lives in a DEN is a denizen of the forest.
words.root: From Anglo-French 'deinzein,' one who lives within.
words.example: Owls, foxes, and other denizens of the woodland came out only after dark.

words.definition: moral corruption or extreme wickedness
words.mnemonic: 'DE-PRAV-ity' — to be 'deprived' of all decency is to sink into depravity.
words.root: From Latin 'depravare,' to distort or corrupt; pravus = crooked.
words.example: The novel exposes the depravity hidden behind the villain's polished manners.

words.definition: scornful mockery or ridicule
words.mnemonic: Related to 'deride' — derision is the laughter that tears someone DOWN.
words.root: From Latin 'deridere,' to laugh at; ridere = to laugh.
words.example: His wild theory was met with derision from the other scientists, who laughed him out of the room.

words.definition: skillful with the hands or clever in handling things
words.mnemonic: From 'dexter' (right hand) — a dexterous person handles things as smoothly as their strong hand.
words.root: From Latin 'dexter,' meaning right-handed or skillful.
words.example: The dexterous pianist's fingers flew across the keys without a single mistake.

words.definition: so thin and delicate as to be almost transparent
words.mnemonic: 'DIA-PHAN' — light 'shines through' (phan-, like phantom) the fabric so you can nearly see through it.
words.root: From Greek 'diaphanes,' showing through; dia = through + phainein = to show.
words.example: Her diaphanous veil let the candlelight glow softly through it.

words.definition: a formal or authoritative statement or saying
words.mnemonic: 'DICT' = to say (as in dictate) — a dictum is an official saying.
words.root: From Latin 'dictum,' something said; dicere = to say.
words.example: The coach lived by the old dictum that hard work beats talent when talent doesn't work hard.

words.definition: to wander away from the main topic when speaking or writing
words.mnemonic: 'DI-GRESS' — like 'diverge' + 'progress' gone sideways; you step OFF the main path.
words.root: From Latin 'digredi,' to step away; gradi = to step.
words.example: The professor tended to digress into stories about his travels before returning to the lesson.

words.definition: to unsettle or throw someone off balance
words.mnemonic: 'DIS-CONCERT' — take the harmony out of a 'concert' and everything feels off and jarring.
words.example: The sudden silence in the room disconcerted the speaker, who lost his place.

words.definition: so unhappy that nothing can comfort you
words.mnemonic: 'DIS-CONSOLE-ate' — you cannot be CONSOLED, so you stay disconsolate.
words.example: After losing the championship by one point, the disconsolate team sat in stunned silence.

words.definition: a difference or inconsistency between things that should match
words.mnemonic: 'DIS-CREP' — two accounts that don't 'creep' together leave a gap, a discrepancy.
words.root: From Latin 'discrepare,' to sound differently; crepare = to rattle.
words.example: The accountant noticed a discrepancy between the receipts and the total in the ledger.

words.definition: not truly sincere; pretending to be honest while hiding real motives
words.mnemonic: 'DIS-INGENUOUS' — the opposite of ingenuous (innocent/frank), so secretly scheming.
words.example: It was disingenuous of him to claim surprise when he had planned the whole thing.

words.definition: calm and unaffected by emotion; impartial
words.mnemonic: 'DIS-PASSIONate' — remove the PASSION and you judge calmly and fairly.
words.example: A good judge must be dispassionate, weighing the facts without letting anger cloud the ruling.

words.definition: to drive away or make disappear, especially a feeling or belief
words.mnemonic: 'DIS-PEL' — like 'expel,' to push doubts or fears out and away.
words.root: From Latin 'dispellere,' to drive apart; pellere = to drive.
words.example: The captain's steady voice dispelled the crew's fear during the storm.

words.definition: to hide one's true feelings or intentions by pretending
words.mnemonic: 'DISS-EMBLE' — sounds like 'disassemble' the truth; you take it apart and hide it.
words.root: From Latin 'dissimulare,' to conceal; simulare = to pretend.
words.example: Rather than admit his jealousy, he dissembled and pretended to congratulate his rival.

words.definition: a person who openly disagrees with an official policy or government
words.mnemonic: 'DISS-IDENT' — one who 'disses' (defies) the identity/rules of those in power.
words.root: From Latin 'dissidere,' to sit apart; sedere = to sit.
words.example: The dissident was jailed for publishing pamphlets that criticized the ruling party.

words.definition: to persuade someone not to do something
words.mnemonic: 'DIS-SUADE' — the opposite of per-SUADE; you talk someone OUT of an idea.
words.example: Her parents tried to dissuade her from quitting school, but she had made up her mind.

words.definition: a state of low spirits or a period of inactivity and stagnation
words.mnemonic: 'DOLL-drums' — imagine a lifeless doll sitting motionless while dull drums thud slowly.
words.root: Likely related to 'dull'; originally calm ocean zones where sailing ships stalled.
words.example: The whole team fell into the doldrums after three losses in a row.

words.definition: full of sorrow; expressing deep sadness
words.mnemonic: 'DOLE-ful' — full of 'dole,' an old word for grief; a doleful face is full of sorrow.
words.root: From Latin 'dolere,' to grieve or feel pain.
words.example: The lost puppy gave a doleful whimper as it searched for its owner.

words.definition: temporarily inactive, as if sleeping; capable of reactivating later
words.mnemonic: 'DORM-ant' — a DORMitory is where you sleep, and something dormant is 'asleep.'
words.example: The volcano had been dormant for centuries before it suddenly erupted.

words.definition: sweet and soothing, especially to the ear
words.mnemonic: 'DULCE' means sweet in Spanish — dulcet tones are sweet to hear.
words.example: The dulcet notes of the harp drifted through the quiet hall.

words.definition: bubbly and full of lively enthusiasm
words.mnemonic: Like an 'effervescent' soda that fizzes over with bubbles, a person can bubble over with energy.
words.example: Her effervescent personality lit up the room the moment she walked in, laughing.

words.definition: the power to produce a desired result; effectiveness
words.mnemonic: 'Efficacy' sounds like 'efficiency' — both are about how well something actually works.
words.example: The scientists ran trials to test the efficacy of the new vaccine before approving it.

words.definition: a way out or the act of going out; an exit
words.mnemonic: 'E-' (out) + '-gress' (go), the opposite of 'progress' — a place to go OUT.
words.example: The fire marshal insisted every classroom have a clear egress in case of emergency.

words.definition: a mournful poem or song, especially for someone who has died
words.mnemonic: An 'elegy' sounds sad and 'ele-gant' — a graceful poem mourning a loss.
words.example: The poet wrote a moving elegy for his late grandmother, full of grief and tenderness.

words.definition: to draw out a response, answer, or reaction from someone
words.mnemonic: Don't confuse with 'illicit' (illegal); to 'elicit' is to 'e-licit' pull a reaction out of someone.
words.example: The comedian's clever joke elicited a burst of laughter from the entire audience.

words.definition: to secretly steal money that has been entrusted to your care
words.mnemonic: An accountant who 'embezzles' 'em-bezzles' the company's cash straight into his own pocket.
words.example: The treasurer was arrested after auditors discovered he had embezzled thousands from the charity.

words.definition: having a softening or soothing effect, especially on the skin or a tense situation
words.mnemonic: 'Emollient' contains 'mollify' (to soothe) — lotion that softens skin, or words that soften a mood.
words.example: His emollient tone calmed the furious customer within a few minutes.

words.definition: based on observation, experiment, or experience rather than pure theory
words.mnemonic: An 'empire' is built on real conquests you can see; 'empirical' knowledge is built on real evidence.
words.example: Rather than guessing, the researcher gathered empirical data by measuring hundreds of plants.

words.definition: a speech or piece of writing that praises someone highly
words.mnemonic: An 'encomium' 'encompasses' every good thing about a person in a glowing tribute.
words.example: At the retirement dinner, colleagues delivered one encomium after another about her decades of service.

words.definition: a feeling of listless boredom and dissatisfaction
words.mnemonic: 'Ennui' sounds like 'on-wee' sighing — the weary yawn of someone bored with everything.
words.root: French 'ennui,' from Latin 'in odio,' in hatred
words.example: By the third rainy day indoors, a heavy ennui had settled over the whole family.

words.definition: to settle comfortably and securely in a place
words.mnemonic: Picture yourself 'ensconced' 'in-a-sconce,' tucked snugly into a cozy nook by the wall.
words.example: She ensconced herself in the armchair with a blanket and a thick novel for the afternoon.

words.definition: to ask someone earnestly or anxiously to do something; to beg
words.mnemonic: To 'entreat' is to 'treat' someone very politely while begging them for a favor.
words.example: The villagers entreated the king to lower the taxes before the winter arrived.

words.definition: fair and impartial to everyone involved
words.mnemonic: 'Equitable' shares the 'equi-' (equal) root — treating everyone equally and fairly.
words.example: The siblings reached an equitable arrangement so that each did an equal share of the chores.

words.definition: straying from the proper course or standard; wandering
words.mnemonic: An 'errant' knight wanders off and makes 'errors,' straying from the right path.
words.example: One errant golf ball sailed over the fence and shattered a neighbor's window.

words.definition: being an artificial and usually inferior substitute for the real thing
words.mnemonic: 'Ersatz' sounds harsh and phony — an 'ers-otz' knockoff standing in for the genuine article.
words.example: During the war, families drank bitter ersatz coffee made from roasted acorns.

words.definition: to deliberately avoid or abstain from something
words.mnemonic: 'Eschew' sounds like a sneeze 'a-chew!' — you turn away from and avoid what makes you sneeze.
words.example: The monk chose to eschew all luxuries and live with only the barest necessities.

words.definition: to adopt and actively support a cause or belief
words.mnemonic: You 'espouse' a cause the way you commit to a 'spouse' — you fully embrace it.
words.example: The senator publicly espoused stricter environmental laws throughout her campaign.

words.definition: no longer close or affectionate; having become a stranger to someone
words.mnemonic: 'Estranged' contains 'strange' — a once-close person now feels like a stranger.
words.root: Latin 'extraneare,' to treat as a stranger
words.example: After years of silence, the estranged brothers finally reunited at their mother's funeral.

words.definition: the characteristic spirit, values, or beliefs of a person, group, or era
words.mnemonic: 'Ethos' is the root of 'ethics' — the guiding values and spirit of a community.
words.example: Teamwork and honesty were part of the company's ethos from the very beginning.

words.definition: bringing strong images, memories, or feelings to mind
words.mnemonic: An 'evocative' smell 'e-vocalizes' a memory, calling it out from deep in your mind.
words.example: The evocative scent of pine needles instantly transported her back to childhood camping trips.

words.definition: making great demands; requiring much care, effort, or precision
words.mnemonic: An 'exacting' boss demands the 'exact,' precise result with no room for error.
words.example: The watchmaker's exacting craft required steady hands and hours of intense focus.

words.definition: to clear someone from blame or guilt
words.mnemonic: 'Ex-' (out) + 'culpa' (fault) — to take someone OUT of fault, the opposite of 'culpable.'
words.example: New security footage exculpated the man, proving he was nowhere near the scene.

words.definition: extremely bad or unpleasant; deserving to be hated
words.mnemonic: 'Execrable' sounds like 'egg-wreck-able' — a spoiled, wrecked mess that everyone despises.
words.example: The critic called the play's dialogue execrable, calling it the worst he had heard in years.

words.definition: an urgent need or demand; a pressing situation
words.mnemonic: An 'exigency' 'exits urgently' — a pressing situation that demands immediate action.
words.example: The exigency of the flood forced the town to evacuate within hours.

words.definition: to make amends or atone for a wrong or sin
words.mnemonic: To 'expiate' guilt is to pay it off — think 'ex-pay-ate,' paying out for your mistakes.
words.example: He volunteered at the shelter for a year to expiate the harm he had caused.

words.definition: to reason earnestly with someone to protest or dissuade them
words.mnemonic: To 'expostulate' is to lay out your 'postulates' (arguments) forcefully in protest.
words.example: His father expostulated with him for hours, urging him not to drop out of school.

words.definition: still in existence; surviving
words.mnemonic: 'Extant' sounds like 'exist' with an ending — something that still exists today.
words.example: Only three copies of the ancient manuscript are still extant, all kept in museums.

words.definition: to free or release from a difficult or entangled situation
words.mnemonic: To 'extricate' is to get 'ex-' (out) of a 'tricky' situation.
words.example: It took the firefighters an hour to extricate the driver from the crushed car.

words.definition: lacking purpose, energy, or the ability to get things done; irresponsible
words.mnemonic: 'feck' sounds like a broken 'effect' — a feckless person has no effect on anything.
words.example: Her feckless brother drifted from job to job, never finishing a single task he started.

words.definition: highly fertile; producing offspring, growth, or ideas in abundance
words.mnemonic: 'fe-CUND' — think of a garden so rich it can 'second' every seed with a plant.
words.example: The novelist had a fecund imagination, spinning out three books a year.

words.definition: well-chosen and pleasingly apt; fortunate in effect
words.mnemonic: Related to 'felicity' (happiness) — a felicitous phrase makes everyone happy with how well it fits.
words.example: Her felicitous choice of words at the ceremony left the whole audience smiling.

words.definition: showing intense, burning passion or enthusiasm
words.mnemonic: 'fervid' shares a root with 'fever' — a fervid speaker seems to burn with feeling.
words.example: His fervid support for the team never cooled, even after ten losing seasons.

words.definition: a minor weakness or quirk in someone's character
words.mnemonic: 'foible' sounds like 'feeble' — a small, feeble flaw in an otherwise strong person.
words.example: We forgave his habit of humming during dinner as a harmless foible.

words.definition: to prevent something by taking action ahead of time
words.mnemonic: 'fore-' (before) + 'stall' — you stall a problem before it arrives.
words.example: The mayor released the report early to forestall rumors from spreading.

words.definition: a noisy quarrel or disorderly brawl
words.mnemonic: 'FRAY-cas' — a fracas is a loud 'fray' that cracks the peace.
words.example: A fracas broke out in the stands when fans of both teams collided.

words.definition: filled with something undesirable, such as tension or danger
words.mnemonic: 'fraught' rhymes with 'taut' — a fraught situation is stretched tight with worry.
words.example: The negotiation was fraught with mistrust, and neither side would speak first.

words.definition: to protest or criticize loudly and forcefully
words.mnemonic: Sounds like a mine 'full' of dynamite — a fulminating critic explodes with anger.
words.example: The editor would fulminate against sloppy writing for hours at a time.

words.definition: to understand something deeply after careful thought
words.mnemonic: A fathom measures ocean depth, so to fathom is to measure how deep an idea goes.
words.root: Old English 'fæðm' (outstretched arms; a depth measure)
words.example: I still cannot fathom why she gave up a scholarship to travel abroad.

words.definition: faithful loyalty or allegiance, especially to a leader or cause
words.mnemonic: 'FEE-alty' — a knight paid his 'fee' of loyalty by swearing fealty.
words.example: The soldiers pledged their fealty to the young queen at her coronation.

words.definition: a brief venture into unfamiliar territory or activity; a sudden raid
words.mnemonic: 'for-a-day' — a foray is a short trip 'for a day' into new territory.
words.example: Her first foray into painting produced a surprisingly bold portrait.

words.definition: to fail completely or sink, as a ship taking on water
words.mnemonic: A ship hits the ocean 'floor' and founders — don't confuse it with a company's founder.
words.root: Old French 'fondrer' (to sink to the bottom)
words.example: The startup foundered after its only major investor pulled out.

words.definition: skillful, subtle handling of a difficult situation
words.mnemonic: 'fine-ness' — handling something with fine, delicate skill is finesse.
words.example: She defused the argument with such finesse that no one felt insulted.

words.definition: fierce, cruel, or deadly in intent
words.mnemonic: One 'fell' swoop is a single deadly stroke — the same fierce 'fell' as a felon.
words.example: With one fell blow the storm flattened every barn in the valley.

words.definition: a calculated opening move or risky strategy meant to gain advantage
words.mnemonic: In chess you 'gamble a bit' by sacrificing a pawn — that opening is a gambit.
words.example: Offering a free trial was the company's gambit to lure new subscribers.

words.definition: to distort or confuse a message so it is hard to understand
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'gargle' — try talking while gargling and your words come out garbled.
words.example: The weak signal garbled his voicemail into a string of nonsense.

words.definition: to make lively or dramatic gestures, especially while speaking
words.mnemonic: Contains 'gesture' — to gesticulate is to gesture wildly.
words.example: He began to gesticulate excitedly, waving both arms to describe the crash.

words.definition: twilight; the dim light after sunset
words.mnemonic: A softened 'glowing' — the sky's last faint glow is the gloaming.
words.example: They walked home through the gloaming as the first stars appeared.

words.definition: rough, twisted, and knotted, like old wood or aged hands
words.mnemonic: The silent 'g' snarls like a knotted old branch — gnarled wood is all snarls.
words.root: Variant of 'knurl' (a small knot or lump)
words.example: The gnarled oak had survived two centuries of storms.

words.definition: to dig or scoop out roughly; to overcharge someone badly
words.mnemonic: 'GOWJ' — a scooping tool gouges wood, and a crook gouges your wallet.
words.example: The falling branch gouged a deep groove in the car's hood.

words.definition: a person of high rank or great importance
words.mnemonic: 'grand' + '-ee' — a grandee is a grand, important personage.
words.example: The banquet was packed with grandees from every noble family in the region.

words.definition: very serious, severe, or causing great suffering
words.mnemonic: Related to 'grief' — a grievous wound brings deep grief.
words.example: Ignoring the warning turned out to be a grievous mistake.

words.definition: a loud, hearty burst of laughter
words.mnemonic: 'guh-FAW' — the sound itself is a big, blurted laugh.
words.root: Imitative of the sound of loud laughter
words.example: His terrible pun drew a guffaw from the back of the room.

words.definition: bold initiative and resourceful common sense
words.mnemonic: Someone with 'gum' in their engine still finds the get-up to move — that's gumption.
words.example: It took real gumption to walk in and ask the CEO for a job.

words.definition: produced in the throat; harsh and rasping in sound
words.mnemonic: Contains 'gutt' like 'gut' — a guttural sound comes from deep in the gut/throat.
words.example: The bear gave a low, guttural growl from inside the cave.

words.definition: thin and bony from hunger, illness, or hardship
words.mnemonic: 'gaunt' rhymes with 'haunt' — a gaunt face looks haunted and starved.
words.example: Weeks lost in the mountains left the hikers gaunt and hollow-eyed.

words.definition: to stare with sullen anger
words.mnemonic: Rhymes with 'lower' — a glower lowers your brows into an angry stare.
words.example: The referee ignored the coach, who continued to glower from the sideline.

words.definition: deeply irritating and humiliating
words.mnemonic: A 'gall' is a painful sore — a galling insult rubs you raw.
words.example: It was galling to lose to a rival who had trained half as hard.

words.definition: an outward appearance or pretense that conceals the truth
words.mnemonic: 'guise' is the visible half of 'disguise' — the false face you put on.
