words.example: He entered the party under the guise of a delivery man.

words.definition: Looking exhausted, gaunt, and worn out, especially from stress or lack of sleep.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'had-guard' — a night guard who 'had' a brutal shift looks haggard by dawn.
words.root: Originally a falconry term for a wild-caught hawk, later 'wild-looking, worn.'
words.example: After three sleepless nights caring for the newborn, she looked pale and haggard.

words.definition: Extremely distressing, frightening, or agonizing to experience.
words.mnemonic: A 'harrow' is a spiked farm tool that rakes the soil — a harrowing story rakes painfully across your feelings.
words.root: From 'harrow,' a tool that breaks and tears the ground.
words.example: The survivor gave a harrowing account of the shipwreck that left the audience in stunned silence.

words.definition: A belief or opinion that sharply contradicts accepted or official doctrine.
words.mnemonic: 'Her-a-see' — she dared to SEE things differently from what the church allowed.
words.root: Greek 'hairesis,' a choosing or faction — later a chosen belief against orthodoxy.
words.example: In the 1600s, claiming the Earth orbited the Sun was condemned as dangerous heresy.

words.definition: To cut or shape something with heavy blows; also, to hold firmly to a rule or principle.
words.mnemonic: 'Hew' sounds like the swing of an axe — 'hew!' — chopping wood, or chopping to a strict line.
words.root: Old English 'heawan,' to strike or chop.
words.example: The sculptor spent weeks hewing a rough angel out of the granite block.

words.definition: Very old and grey with age; also, so familiar with age that it has grown stale.
words.mnemonic: Rhymes with 'story' — a hoary old story your grandfather has told a hundred times.
words.example: The professor repeated the same hoary joke he'd been telling classes for forty years.

words.definition: A long moralizing talk or sermon, often tediously preachy.
words.mnemonic: 'Home-ily' — the long moral lecture you get at HOME from a parent.
words.root: Greek 'homilia,' a discourse or conversation with a crowd.
words.example: Instead of just saying goodnight, my uncle launched into a homily about the value of hard work.

words.definition: The careful management of resources, especially the farming and breeding of animals and crops.
words.mnemonic: A 'husband' once meant a household manager — husbandry is managing the farm and its animals wisely.
words.root: Old Norse 'husbondi,' master of a household.
words.example: Good animal husbandry kept the herd healthy through the harsh winter.

words.definition: Dominance or controlling influence of one group or nation over others.
words.mnemonic: 'He-jam-on-y' — the top power jams its will on everyone else.
words.example: For decades the empire held economic hegemony over every trading port in the region.

words.definition: Haughty, arrogant superiority in manner.
words.mnemonic: From French 'haut' (high) — think 'oh-TUR' looking down his nose from on high.
words.example: She swept past the waiting reporters with the cool hauteur of someone who felt above questions.

words.definition: To cripple or severely hinder the effectiveness of someone or something.
words.mnemonic: Cutting the 'hamstring' tendon behind the knee stops you cold — to hamstring a plan is to disable it.
words.example: Budget cuts hamstrung the research team before they could even begin the study.

words.definition: Never changing and impossible to change.
words.mnemonic: 'Im-' (not) + 'mutable' (changeable, like a 'mutation') — not able to mutate.
words.example: He treated the coach's rules as immutable laws that could never be questioned.

words.definition: Showing no emotion or reaction; expressionless.
words.mnemonic: 'Im-' (not) + 'passive'? No — think 'im-PASSION-ive,' with no passion showing on the face.
words.example: The guard remained impassive, his face betraying nothing as the crowd shouted at him.

words.definition: Unable to be upset or agitated; remarkably calm.
words.mnemonic: 'Im-' (not) + 'perturb' (to disturb) — nothing can perturb this person.
words.example: Even as the plane hit violent turbulence, the pilot's voice stayed imperturbable.

words.definition: A force or influence that drives something forward; a stimulus.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'im-PET-us' — a pet dog lunging forward gives the leash sudden impetus.
words.root: Latin 'impetus,' an attack or rushing forward.
words.example: The scandal gave fresh impetus to the campaign for stricter safety laws.

words.definition: Boldly disrespectful or rude, especially toward someone deserving respect.
words.mnemonic: 'Im-PUDE-nt' — no shame ('pudency' means modesty); an impudent kid has zero shame talking back.
words.example: The impudent student rolled his eyes and told the principal to mind her own business.

words.definition: Just beginning to develop or exist; in an early stage.
words.mnemonic: Shares roots with 'inception' (the beginning) — an incipient idea is one just being conceived.
words.example: The doctor caught the incipient infection early, before it could spread.

words.definition: Glowing brightly with heat; also, brilliantly radiant or passionately intense.
words.mnemonic: An incandescent bulb glows white-hot — 'in-CANDLE-scent,' bright as a candle's flame.
words.example: The dying embers left the sky an incandescent orange at dusk.

words.definition: To formally accuse or charge someone with a serious crime.
words.mnemonic: Spelled oddly but say 'in-DITE' — the grand jury writes ('dict-') the charge against you.
words.example: The grand jury voted to indict the executive on charges of fraud.

words.definition: Impossible to defeat, subdue, or discourage.
words.mnemonic: 'In-' (not) + 'domit-' (to tame, as in 'domesticate') — a spirit that cannot be tamed.
words.example: Despite losing everything, she faced the future with an indomitable will.

words.definition: Too great, beautiful, or intense to be described in words.
words.mnemonic: 'In-' (not) + 'eff-' (to speak, from Latin 'fari') — so overwhelming you cannot speak it.
words.example: Standing at the canyon's edge, she felt an ineffable sense of awe she could never put into words.

words.definition: Harmful, hostile, or unfriendly to something.
words.mnemonic: Hidden inside is 'enemy' (Latin 'inimicus') — inimical means enemy-like.
words.example: The harsh, salt-laden air proved inimical to the delicate garden plants.

words.definition: Gross injustice or wickedness; a sinful act.
words.mnemonic: 'In-EQUITY' — 'equity' means fairness, so in-iquity is the absence of fairness: wickedness.
words.example: The reformer spent his life exposing the iniquity of the corrupt court.

words.definition: An indirect, sly hint or suggestion, usually about something improper.
words.mnemonic: 'In-your-window' — a sneaky suggestion slipped in sideways, never said outright.
words.root: Latin 'innuere,' to nod to or hint.
words.example: Rather than accusing him directly, she attacked his reputation through whispered innuendo.

words.definition: Narrow-minded and cut off from outside ideas; like an isolated island.
words.mnemonic: Latin 'insula' means island (think 'peninsula') — an insular mind is stranded on its own little island.
words.example: Growing up in such an insular village, he had never questioned the customs he was raised with.

words.definition: Seemingly endless, especially because tedious or annoying.
words.mnemonic: 'In-' (not) + 'terminable' (able to be terminated/ended) — it will not end.
words.example: The interminable meeting dragged on for four hours with no decisions made.

words.definition: Very hard to control, manage, or solve; stubborn.
words.mnemonic: 'In-' (not) + 'tract-' (to pull/lead, as in 'tractor') — you can't pull or lead it where you want.
words.example: Poverty proved an intractable problem that resisted every quick fix the city tried.

words.definition: To protest or complain strongly and bitterly against something.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'in-VAY' — you march IN and let loose a fierce verbal barrage against something.
words.root: Latin 'invehi,' to attack with words — 'in-' + 'vehere' (to carry).
words.example: The senator rose to inveigh against the proposed tax as a betrayal of working families.

words.definition: Impossible to undo, reverse, or take back.
words.mnemonic: 'Ir-' (not) + 'revoke' — a decision you cannot revoke once it's made.
words.example: Once she signed the contract, the sale of the house was irrevocable.

words.definition: A joke or clever prank meant to amuse.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'jape' = a joking ape swinging around pulling pranks.
words.example: The knight's grim face finally cracked at the jester's harmless jape.

words.definition: A trip taken for pleasure, especially one paid for by someone else.
words.mnemonic: 'Junk-it': the officials junked their duties for a fancy free junket to the coast.
words.root: From Latin juncus 'reed basket,' which came to mean a feast, then a pleasure trip.
words.example: Voters were angry that the senator's 'fact-finding junket' was really a beach vacation.

words.definition: A group, usually of military officers, that rules a country after seizing power by force.
words.mnemonic: 'HOON-ta' sounds like 'hunter' — a pack of generals hunting for control of the country.
words.root: Spanish for 'council,' from Latin iungere 'to join.'
words.example: After the coup, a military junta suspended the constitution and ruled by decree.

words.definition: Having a lively, cheerful, and self-confident manner.
words.mnemonic: A jaunty walk is a 'jaunt' with a spring in your step — bouncy and carefree.
words.example: He tipped his hat at a jaunty angle and whistled as he strolled down the lane.

words.definition: A long, mournful complaint or bitter lament about the state of things.
words.mnemonic: Named for the prophet Jeremiah, who wailed endlessly in the biblical Lamentations.
words.root: From the prophet Jeremiah, author of the sorrowful book of Lamentations.
words.example: His speech turned into a jeremiad about how nothing worked the way it used to.

words.definition: An insulting or mocking remark; a taunt.
words.mnemonic: A jibe jabs like a verbal knife — 'jibe' = 'jab.'
words.example: She ignored his cruel jibe about her accent and kept reading her essay aloud.

words.definition: A huge, powerful force or organization that cannot be stopped.
words.mnemonic: Picture a giant unstoppable cart ('naut') crushing everything in its path.
words.root: From Sanskrit Jagannatha 'lord of the world,' whose massive festival cart flattened all before it.
words.example: The small studio's film became a box-office juggernaut that no rival could match.

words.definition: The study, science, or philosophy of law.
words.mnemonic: 'Juris' (law) + 'prudence' (wisdom) = the wisdom of the law.
words.example: Her senior thesis explored how ancient Roman jurisprudence still shapes modern courts.

words.definition: Fate or destiny; the idea that events are predetermined.
words.mnemonic: 'Kiss-met': meeting the person you kiss was simply kismet — meant to be.
words.example: Meeting his future business partner on a delayed train felt like pure kismet.

words.definition: To behave in an excessively servile or submissive way toward someone.
words.mnemonic: 'Cow-tow': you bow so low you could tow a cow with your forehead.
words.root: From Chinese ketou 'knock the head' — a deep bow touching the ground.
words.example: A good manager listens to staff without expecting them to kowtow to his every whim.

words.definition: A small, rounded hill or mound.
words.mnemonic: 'Knoll' rhymes with 'roll' — a little hill you could roll down.
words.example: They spread the picnic blanket on a grassy knoll overlooking the valley.

words.definition: One's friends, acquaintances, and neighbors.
words.mnemonic: 'Kith and kin': kith are the friends you'd 'kiss' hello, kin are your blood relatives.
words.root: Old English cyth 'known people'; survives mainly in 'kith and kin.'
words.example: Far from home, the soldier missed his kith and kin more than the comforts of the town.

words.definition: Similar in nature or character; related.
words.mnemonic: Kindred = 'kind' + 'red' as in your 'kind' — people of the same kind or blood.
words.example: The two poets were kindred spirits who finished each other's stanzas.

words.definition: To complain persistently and often about small things.
words.mnemonic: 'Kvetch' sounds like a scratchy 'ehhtch' of endless whining.
words.example: Instead of fixing the schedule, he just kvetched about it to anyone who would listen.

words.definition: Perfectly clear and transparent.
words.mnemonic: A limpid pool is so clear a 'limp' fish looks like it's floating in air.
words.example: The limpid mountain stream let them count every pebble on its bed.

words.definition: A long, tedious, repetitive list or recital.
words.mnemonic: A litany drones on like a 'little Annie' reciting her complaints one by one.
words.root: Greek litaneia 'prayer' — originally a series of repeated petitions.
words.example: Rather than one clear point, his email was a litany of minor grievances.

words.definition: Graceful, supple, and easily bent.
words.mnemonic: A lithe dancer moves so smoothly she seems to 'slither' across the stage.
words.example: The lithe gymnast folded her body into shapes that looked impossible.

words.definition: Reluctant or unwilling to do something.
words.mnemonic: If you 'loathe' a task, you're loath (reluctant) to do it — same feeling, no 'e.'
words.root: Old English lath 'hostile'; the adjective, not the verb 'loathe.'
words.example: She was loath to admit that her rival's plan was actually better than her own.

words.definition: To publicly mock or ridicule through sharp satire.
words.mnemonic: A cartoonist aims a satirical 'harpoon' — a lampoon — at a puffed-up politician.
words.root: From French lampons 'let us drink,' a refrain sung in mocking songs.
words.example: The student newspaper loves to lampoon the principal's long-winded assemblies.

words.definition: The crime of stealing another person's property; theft.
words.mnemonic: 'Larceny' hides 'Larry' — imagine Larry sneaking off with your stuff.
words.example: He was charged with petty larceny after pocketing candy from the store.

words.definition: Skillful trickery or sleight of hand, especially in magic or deception.
words.mnemonic: 'Ledger-de-main': a magician's main hand swaps the ledger before your eyes.
words.root: From French leger de main 'light of hand.'
words.example: With a bit of legerdemain, the magician made the coin vanish from his palm.

words.definition: Cautious or wary because of suspicion.
words.mnemonic: Being leery makes you 'leer' sideways at anything that seems off.
words.example: After the first scam, she grew leery of any deal that sounded too generous.

words.definition: A published false statement that damages someone's reputation.
words.mnemonic: Libel is in a 'label' or article — printed lies you can be 'liable' for.
words.root: Latin libellus 'little book' — a written, published defamation.
words.example: The actor sued the magazine for libel after it printed a fabricated story.

words.definition: Lacking moral discipline, especially in sexual matters.
words.mnemonic: Taking a 'license' to do anything you want becomes licentious behavior.
words.root: Latin licentia 'freedom' — freedom pushed into immorality.
words.example: The novel scandalized readers with its portrait of a wealthy, licentious court.

words.definition: Overly eager to argue or settle disputes through lawsuits; quarrelsome.
words.mnemonic: A litigious person drags every 'little' gripe into 'litigation' (court).
words.root: Latin litigare 'to go to law, quarrel.'
words.example: Their litigious neighbor sued over a fence that was two inches too tall.

words.definition: Money or profit, especially when gained in a dishonorable way.
words.mnemonic: 'LOO-ker' sounds like 'looker' greedily eyeing the cash — filthy lucre.
words.root: Latin lucrum 'gain'; often paired as 'filthy lucre.'
words.example: He abandoned his ideals for the easy lucre of the arms trade.

words.definition: Lacking brightness, energy, or excellence; dull.
words.mnemonic: Literally 'lacking luster' — no shine, no sparkle.
words.example: Critics called the sequel a lackluster follow-up to the thrilling original.

words.definition: Slim, supple, and graceful in movement.
words.mnemonic: 'Lissome' = 'lithe-some' — full of lithe, limber grace.
words.example: The lissome cat slipped through the fence rails without breaking stride.

words.definition: Lacking energy, enthusiasm, or interest.
words.mnemonic: With no 'list' of desires left, you feel list-less — drained and indifferent.
words.example: The summer heat left the whole class listless and staring out the window.

words.definition: disturbing and horrifying because it deals with death and injury
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'ma-CAWB-ra' — picture a crow (caw) circling a graveyard at midnight.
words.root: From French 'danse macabre,' the medieval 'dance of death.'
words.example: The old house was decorated with a macabre collection of skulls and coffins.

words.definition: a powerful whirlpool; a scene of great confusion or turbulence
words.mnemonic: 'MALE-strom' — imagine a storm so strong it sucks ships into a spinning drain.
words.example: After the scandal broke, the senator was caught in a maelstrom of accusations.

words.definition: a disease or ailment; a serious problem within a system
words.mnemonic: 'MAL' (bad) + 'lady' — a bad condition that makes a lady take to her sickbed.
words.example: The doctor could not name the strange malady that left him weak for months.

words.definition: wrongdoing, especially misconduct by a public official
words.mnemonic: 'MAL' (bad) + 'feasance' (doing) — bad doing while in office.
words.example: The mayor was removed from office for malfeasance after misusing city funds.

words.definition: a person who is dissatisfied and rebellious
words.mnemonic: 'MAL' (bad) + 'content' — someone whose contentment is broken; always grumbling.
words.example: A handful of malcontents refused to accept the new rules and stirred up trouble.

words.definition: a person's look or manner, especially one showing mood or character
words.mnemonic: Sounds exactly like 'mean' — but it's how you 'mean' to appear: your outward air.
words.example: Despite the crisis, the captain kept a calm and confident mien.

words.definition: a timid, feeble person who is easily dominated
words.mnemonic: 'Milk toast' — soggy bread in milk, so soft and bland it can't stand up for itself.
words.root: From Caspar Milquetoast, a meek 1920s comic-strip character.
words.example: Everyone expected a milquetoast, but the new manager turned out to be surprisingly firm.

words.definition: a person who behaves badly or breaks the law
words.mnemonic: 'MIS' (wrong) + 'creant' (believing) — originally a heretic; now any bad actor.
words.example: The night watchman chased off the miscreants who had been vandalizing the shop.

words.definition: a letter or written message, especially a long or official one
words.mnemonic: A missive is something you 'send off' — same root as 'missile,' but words instead of weapons.
words.example: She received a formal missive from the university inviting her to enroll.

words.definition: having no practical importance because a decision has already been made or the situation has changed
words.mnemonic: If a question is 'moot,' arguing it is 'mute' — no one will act on your point.
words.root: Old English 'gemot,' an assembly for debate; a 'moot' point was one merely for discussion.
words.example: Once the trip was canceled, the argument about what to pack became moot.

words.definition: made up of many different and clashing kinds; varied
words.mnemonic: A court jester wore a 'motley' coat of mismatched patches — a jumble of colors.
words.root: Middle English, referring to the multicolored cloth of a jester.
words.example: A motley crew of sailors, students, and drifters gathered at the dock.

words.definition: to treat someone with excessive care and protection; to pamper
words.mnemonic: 'Molly' + 'coddle' — cuddling someone so much they never toughen up.
words.example: The coach refused to mollycoddle his players, pushing them to train harder.

words.definition: having or showing great authority; commanding respect
words.mnemonic: Contains 'magister' (Latin for 'master') — spoken like a master teacher no one questions.
words.example: The professor's magisterial lecture silenced even the most talkative students.

words.definition: to talk or move in a rambling, aimless way
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'meander' — your speech wanders off with no destination.
words.root: Uncertain; possibly from 'maunder' meaning to grumble or beg.
words.example: Grandpa would maunder on about his youth until we forgot the original question.

words.definition: a person of great wealth or high importance
words.mnemonic: 'NAY-bob' — a bobbing nobleman dripping in riches.
words.root: From Urdu/Hindi 'nawwab,' a governor in the Mughal Empire.
words.example: The film festival attracted every Hollywood nabob and their entourage.

words.definition: to chatter idly about unimportant things
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'chatter' with an N — endless small talk.
words.example: The two neighbors would natter over the fence for hours about nothing in particular.

words.definition: so surprised or confused that one does not know how to react
words.mnemonic: 'Non' + 'plus' — your brain can add 'no more' thoughts; it just freezes.
words.root: Latin 'non plus' — 'no more' (nothing further can be done).
words.example: The teacher was nonplussed when the shy student suddenly began to argue loudly.

words.definition: a quack medicine or a dubious remedy for problems
words.mnemonic: 'NOS-trum' — a shady salesman's drum-beating cure for everything.
words.root: Latin 'nostrum' (our own), as in 'our own secret remedy.'
words.example: Politicians offered easy nostrums that could never solve the deep-rooted crisis.

words.definition: of little or no value; trifling or ineffective
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'nougat' — soft, fluffy candy with no real substance.
words.example: His objections were nugatory and had no effect on the committee's decision.

words.definition: a newly coined word or expression
words.mnemonic: 'NEO' (new) + 'log' (word) — a brand-new word.
words.example: 'Selfie' was once a neologism that quickly spread into everyday speech.

words.definition: the unfair practice of giving jobs or favors to relatives and friends
words.mnemonic: Contains 'nephew' — hiring your nephew instead of the best candidate.
words.root: Latin 'nepos' (nephew); popes once favored their nephews.
words.example: Critics accused the CEO of nepotism when she named her son vice president.

words.definition: unwilling to give or spend; stingy
words.mnemonic: A 'niggardly' host gives you a 'meager' portion — hold that image of a tiny plate.
words.example: Despite his fortune, he was so niggardly that he reused tea bags for a week.

words.definition: the belief that life has no meaning, purpose, or value
words.mnemonic: 'NIHIL' is Latin for 'nothing' — a belief in nothing at all.
words.example: His teenage nihilism faded once he found a cause he truly cared about.

words.definition: having no equal; matchless
words.mnemonic: 'Non' + 'pareil' (French for 'equal') — no equal exists.
words.example: As a violinist she was nonpareil, admired by every musician who heard her.

words.definition: suggesting the presence of a spiritual or mysterious power; awe-inspiring
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'luminous' — a glowing, sacred aura that fills you with awe.
words.example: Standing alone in the ancient cathedral, she felt a numinous stillness settle over her.

words.definition: causing annoyance or difficulty; irritating
words.mnemonic: A nettle is a stinging plant — a nettlesome problem stings and irritates.
words.example: The lawyers spent weeks untangling one nettlesome clause in the contract.

words.definition: a person or thing of no importance or significance
words.mnemonic: 'Non' + 'entity' — barely an entity at all; a nobody.
words.example: Once a powerful minister, he became a political nonentity after the scandal.

words.definition: a timid, weak, or ineffectual person
words.mnemonic: 'NEB-ish' — a nervous, meek person who fades into the wallpaper.
words.example: The hero of the novel is a nebbish who slowly finds his courage.

words.definition: A gesture or attitude of deep respect or submission, like a bow.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'obey-ance' — showing you obey by bowing low.
words.root: From Old French 'obeissance,' from Latin 'oboedire' (to obey).
words.example: The servants made a low obeisance as the king swept into the hall.

words.definition: To scold or rebuke someone harshly.
words.mnemonic: 'Ob-JERK-ate' — you call someone a jerk while chewing them out.
words.example: The coach objurgated the players for their careless mistakes during the final.

words.definition: An offering, especially one made to God or a deity.
words.mnemonic: 'Ob-LATION' sounds like 'oblige-ation' — a gift you feel obliged to give the gods.
words.example: The priests laid the harvest fruits on the altar as an oblation.

words.definition: Strong public criticism or verbal abuse; disgrace that results from it.
words.mnemonic: 'Ob-lo-quy' — people 'talk' (loquy, as in loquacious) badly about you, so you're low.
words.root: From Latin 'obloqui' (to speak against), from 'loqui' (to speak).
words.example: After the scandal, the mayor faced months of obloquy in the newspapers.

words.definition: Funeral rites or ceremonies.
words.mnemonic: 'Obsequies' hides 'sequ-' (to follow, as in sequel) — the procession that follows the dead to the grave.
words.root: From Latin 'obsequiae,' related to 'sequi' (to follow) — the procession that follows the dead.
words.example: Hundreds gathered to observe the obsequies of the beloved general.

words.definition: To block, close up, or obstruct a passage or opening.
words.mnemonic: 'Oc-CLUDE' rhymes with 'exclude' — to shut something out by blocking the way.
words.root: From Latin 'occludere' (to shut up), from 'claudere' (to close).
words.example: A blood clot can occlude an artery and cut off circulation.

words.definition: Hidden, secret, or relating to supernatural or mysterious forces.
words.mnemonic: An 'occult' cult meets in secret to study hidden mysteries.
words.example: The old library held books on alchemy and other occult subjects.

words.definition: Intense hatred or disgust, especially toward someone widely condemned.
words.mnemonic: 'Odium' is like an 'odious' feeling — a stadium full of hatred.
words.example: The traitor lived out his years in odium, shunned by everyone.

words.definition: The waste or discarded parts of a butchered animal; refuse or garbage.
words.mnemonic: 'Offal' sounds like 'awful' — the awful leftover bits nobody wants.
words.root: From Middle English 'off' + 'fall' — the parts that fall off in butchering.
words.example: The scraps and offal from the market were fed to the pigs.

words.definition: A miscellaneous mixture or hodgepodge of unrelated things.
words.mnemonic: 'Olio' — imagine a bowl of 'olive oil' with everything tossed in together.
words.example: His essay was an olio of quotations, jokes, and half-finished ideas.

words.definition: Knowing everything; having unlimited knowledge.
words.mnemonic: 'Omni' (all) + 'scient' (knowing, like science) — knowing all.
words.example: The novel is told by an omniscient narrator who knows every character's thoughts.

words.definition: Having unlimited or very great power; all-powerful.
words.mnemonic: 'Omni' (all) + 'potent' (powerful) — all-powerful.
words.example: Ancient myths often portray the gods as omnipotent rulers of the world.

words.definition: To state or hold an opinion.
words.mnemonic: 'Opine' hides 'opin'-ion — to give your opinion out loud.
words.example: The critic opined that the film was overrated and far too long.

words.definition: To fight against, oppose, or call into question.
words.mnemonic: 'Op-PUNE' contains 'pugn' (fight, as in pugnacious) — to fight against.
words.example: Several scientists rose to oppugn the researcher's controversial claims.

words.definition: An opening or hole, especially in the body.
words.mnemonic: 'Or-i-fice' — an 'office' door is an opening you pass through.
words.example: Water drained slowly through a narrow orifice at the bottom of the tank.

words.definition: Bad-tempered, stubborn, and difficult to deal with.
words.mnemonic: 'Ornery' sounds like 'ordinary' gone sour — a grumpy old mule.
words.root: American dialect alteration of 'ordinary' (meaning coarse or mean).
words.example: The ornery old farmer chased the kids off his land with a broom.

words.definition: Of a voice: full, rich, and resonant; of speech: pompous or overblown.
words.mnemonic: 'Oro' (mouth) + 'rotund' (round) — a big, round, booming voice.
words.example: The announcer's orotund voice filled every corner of the stadium.

words.definition: To harden into bone; figuratively, to become rigid and unable to change.
words.mnemonic: 'Ossi' (bone, as in osseous) + '-fy' — to turn into bone.
words.example: Over the decades, the club's traditions had ossified into unbreakable rules.

words.definition: Stated or appearing to be true, but not necessarily so; apparent rather than real.
words.mnemonic: 'Ostensible' shares 'osten-' with 'ostentation' — what's shown on the surface, not the real reason.
words.example: The ostensible purpose of the trip was business, but he mainly wanted a vacation.

words.definition: Serving no useful purpose; pointless or idle.
words.mnemonic: 'O-tee-ose' sounds like 'oat dose' that does nothing — a useless, lazy addition.
words.example: Half the committee's rules were otiose, repeating what the law already required.

words.definition: Showing excessive pride, confidence, or arrogance.
words.mnemonic: 'Over-weening' — thinking way 'over' what you deserve; overblown ego.
words.example: His overweening ambition led him to promise far more than he could deliver.

words.definition: To force oneself or one's ideas on others in an unwelcome way; to push forward.
words.mnemonic: 'Ob-TRUDE' like 'intrude' — to push in where you're not wanted.
words.root: From Latin 'obtrudere' (to thrust against), from 'trudere' (to push).
words.example: She tried not to obtrude her opinions on a group that hadn't asked for them.

words.definition: The front or main side of something (like the 'heads' of a coin); the opposite or counterpart of a fact.
words.mnemonic: 'Ob-verse' — the side that's turned 'toward' you; the reverse's opposite.
words.example: The obverse of the coin showed the queen's profile, the reverse a lion.

words.definition: A burden, duty, or responsibility placed on someone.
words.mnemonic: 'Onus' sounds like 'own us' — the responsibility you own falls on you.
words.example: The onus is on the prosecution to prove the defendant's guilt.

words.definition: A drug that dulls the senses; anything that soothes, numbs, or lulls people into calm.
words.mnemonic: 'Opiate' comes from 'opium' — something that puts you into a numb daze.
words.example: Some critics called mindless television an opiate that keeps people from thinking.

words.definition: Oily or greasy; figuratively, excessively smooth or falsely charming.
words.mnemonic: 'Olea' is Latin for olive (oil) — an oily, slick manner.
words.root: From Latin 'oleaginus' (of the olive tree), from 'oleum' (oil).
words.example: The salesman's oleaginous flattery made everyone instantly distrust him.

words.definition: Unusual and shocking; violating convention or propriety; bizarre.
words.mnemonic: 'Outré' looks like 'out there' — behavior that's way out there.
words.root: From French 'outré' (exceeded, exaggerated), from 'outrer' (to push beyond).
words.example: Her outré outfit of feathers and neon stunned the formal dinner guests.

words.definition: A government or organization controlled by a small group of people.
words.mnemonic: 'Oligo' (few) + 'archy' (rule) — rule by the few.
words.example: After the revolution, power fell into the hands of a wealthy oligarchy.

words.definition: Showing shifting, rainbow-like colors like an opal.
words.mnemonic: 'Opal-escent' — glowing with the milky rainbow shimmer of an opal.
words.example: The soap bubble glowed with opalescent swirls of pink and green.

words.definition: To dull, blunt, or reduce the sharpness of a sensation or feeling.
words.mnemonic: 'Ob-TUND' sounds like 'obtuse' + 'stunned' — to make dull and blunted.
words.root: From Latin 'obtundere' (to beat against, blunt), from 'tundere' (to beat).
words.example: The medication was given to obtund the patient's pain before the procedure.

words.definition: a small, relatively unimportant fault or sin
words.mnemonic: 'pecca' sounds like 'pecker' pecking a tiny bit — a small peck of a sin.
words.example: His habit of stealing office pens was a mere peccadillo compared to the fraud his boss committed.

words.definition: transparently clear, either in substance or in meaning
words.mnemonic: 'pel-LUCID' contains 'lucid' — pellucid water is perfectly lucid and clear.
words.example: Her pellucid explanation made the tangled math problem suddenly obvious to everyone.

words.definition: traveling from place to place; constantly moving around
words.mnemonic: Aristotle taught while walking a 'path' — peri-PATH-etic means always on a path.
words.root: Greek 'peripatetikos' (walking about), from 'peri-' around + 'patein' to walk
words.example: The peripatetic musician never stayed in one city for more than a week before touring on.

words.definition: insisting on obedience and allowing no refusal or discussion
words.mnemonic: A 'peremptory' order 'preempts' any argument — no back-talk allowed.
words.example: With a peremptory wave of his hand, the general silenced the officers' objections.

words.definition: stubbornly holding to a purpose, opinion, or course of action
words.mnemonic: 'pertinacious' hides 'tenacious' — both mean gripping on and refusing to let go.
words.example: Her pertinacious questioning finally forced the witness to admit the truth.

words.definition: a speech or piece of writing that praises someone or something highly
words.mnemonic: 'PANE-gyric' — imagine a full 'panel' of people singing someone's praises.
words.root: Greek 'panegyrikos' (of a public assembly), from 'pan-' all + 'agyris' assembly
words.example: The retirement dinner turned into a panegyric, with speaker after speaker praising her career.

words.definition: a sudden, violent outburst of emotion or action
words.mnemonic: 'para-OX-ysm' — picture an ox suddenly bucking in a wild fit.
words.example: At the punchline she collapsed into a paroxysm of laughter and couldn't catch her breath.

words.definition: narrow or limited in outlook or concern; confined to a small area
words.mnemonic: A 'parish' is a small local area — parochial thinking never leaves the parish.
words.root: Latin 'parochialis' (of a parish), from Greek 'paroikia' neighborhood
words.example: His parochial views on other cultures came from never having left his hometown.

words.definition: a work made by imitating or piecing together the styles of others
words.mnemonic: 'pastiche' sounds like 'pasted' — bits of others' styles pasted together.
words.example: The film was a clever pastiche of 1950s horror movies, borrowing scenes from a dozen classics.

words.definition: a long, wandering journey, especially on foot
words.mnemonic: A 'peregrine' falcon roams far — a peregrination is a far-roaming trek.
words.example: His peregrinations across Asia lasted three years and filled a dozen journals.

words.definition: a strong verbal or written attack on someone's opinions or beliefs
words.mnemonic: 'POLE-mic' — grab the mic and go to war (Greek 'polemos') against an idea.
words.example: Her essay was a fierce polemic against standardized testing in schools.

words.definition: slow and clumsy because of great weight; dull and labored
words.mnemonic: 'ponderous' shares 'ponder' and 'pound' — heavy enough to weigh many pounds.
words.example: The elephant's ponderous steps shook the ground with each slow movement.

words.definition: to be a sign or warning that something (often bad) is going to happen
words.mnemonic: 'por-TEND' — the dark clouds 'tend' toward a storm, warning you ahead of time.
words.root: Latin 'portendere' (to foretell), from 'por-' forth + 'tendere' to stretch
words.example: The sudden silence of the birds seemed to portend the coming earthquake.

words.definition: to cause something to happen suddenly or too soon
words.mnemonic: 'pre-CIP-itate' — like something falling off a cliff (precipice) suddenly.
words.example: A single careless comment precipitated a full-blown argument between the two friends.

words.definition: to prevent something from happening or make it impossible
words.mnemonic: 'pre-CLUDE' — close (Latin 'claudere') the door beforehand so it can't happen.
words.root: Latin 'praecludere' (to shut off), from 'prae-' before + 'claudere' to close
words.example: A prior commitment precluded her from attending the weekend conference.

words.definition: a natural preference or special liking for something
words.mnemonic: 'pre-di-LECTION' — you pick (Latin 'legere') a favorite beforehand.
words.example: She had a lifelong predilection for old jazz records over modern pop.

words.definition: having knowledge of events before they happen; foresighted
words.mnemonic: 'PRE-science' — knowing (science) something before it occurs.
words.root: Latin 'praescire' (to know beforehand), from 'prae-' before + 'scire' to know
words.example: His prescient warning about the market crash saved the company millions.

words.definition: recklessly wasteful with money or resources; shamelessly immoral
words.mnemonic: 'pro-FLIG-ate' — flinging money away flagrantly and freely.
words.example: His profligate spending on luxury cars left him bankrupt within a year.

words.definition: to increase or spread rapidly in numbers
words.mnemonic: 'pro-LIFE-erate' — life multiplying and spreading rapidly.
words.example: Coffee shops proliferated across the city until there was one on every corner.

words.definition: to make an idea, law, or belief widely known officially
words.mnemonic: 'pro-MUL-gate' — push (pro) an idea out the gate to the whole world.
words.example: The new government quickly promulgated laws banning corruption in office.

words.definition: correctness of behavior or morals; the state of being proper
words.mnemonic: 'propriety' sounds like 'proper' — behaving in a proper, well-mannered way.
words.example: She questioned the propriety of texting during her grandmother's funeral.

words.definition: to forbid something by law or authority
words.mnemonic: 'PRO-scribe' — officials scribe (write) your name on the banned list.
words.example: The school proscribed cell phones in classrooms after too many distractions.

words.definition: showing a lack of courage; timid and cowardly
words.mnemonic: 'pusil-lanimous' — a 'puny' soul (Latin 'animus') too weak to be brave.
words.example: His pusillanimous refusal to speak up let the bully continue unchallenged.

words.definition: to steal something, especially in a sneaky way
words.mnemonic: 'pur-LOIN' — a thief sneaks off with a stolen loin of meat.
words.root: Anglo-French 'purloigner' (to put at a distance, remove)
words.example: He tried to purloin a cookie from the jar while his mother wasn't looking.

words.definition: sounding sad and mournful
words.mnemonic: 'plaintive' relates to 'complaint' — a sad, complaining sound.
words.root: Old French 'plaintif' (lamenting), from Latin 'plangere' to lament
words.example: The lost puppy let out a plaintive whine that broke everyone's heart.

words.definition: an expression of praise or approval
words.mnemonic: 'PLAUD-it' shares a root with 'applaud' — clapping in praise.
words.example: Her debut novel earned plaudits from critics across the country.

words.definition: feeling or showing sorrow for having done wrong
words.mnemonic: 'PENITENT' — doing 'penance' because you feel sorry for a sin.
words.example: Looking penitent, he apologized to his sister for breaking her phone.

words.definition: pleasantly sharp or spicy in taste; agreeably stimulating to the mind
words.mnemonic: 'PIQUANT' sounds like 'pick-ant' — a sharp bite like an ant's, but pleasant.
words.example: The piquant salsa gave the bland dish a welcome kick of flavor.

words.definition: to talk on and on in a foolish or trivial way
words.mnemonic: 'PRATTLE' rhymes with 'rattle' — mouth rattling on about nothing.
words.example: The children prattled about their day while their tired parents nodded along.

words.definition: a difficult, messy situation that is hard to escape; also literally soft, boggy ground
words.mnemonic: 'quag' sounds like a squishy bog + 'mire' = mud — you're stuck in the mud, unable to get out
words.example: After borrowing from three friends to pay one debt, he sank into a financial quagmire he couldn't climb out of.

words.definition: to drink something heartily, in large gulps
words.mnemonic: 'quaff' sounds like a big satisfied 'aaah' after chugging a mug — quaff = quick + laugh of a drinker
words.root: origin uncertain, possibly imitative of the sound of gulping
words.example: The weary knights quaffed tankards of ale the moment they returned from battle.

words.definition: to put an end to or suppress something completely; to officially reject as invalid
words.mnemonic: 'quash' sounds like 'squash' — you squash a rumor or a rebellion flat
words.root: from Old French 'quasser' (to annul), related to Latin 'cassare' (to nullify)
words.example: The principal moved quickly to quash the rumor before it spread through the school.

words.definition: to shake or tremble, especially of a voice
words.mnemonic: 'quaver' = quiver + waver; a nervous voice quavers like a plucked string
words.example: Her voice began to quaver as she read the farewell letter aloud.

words.definition: the minimum number of members needed to be present for a meeting's decisions to count
words.mnemonic: 'quorum' — 'four of them' showed up, just enough to vote; a quorum is the required core
words.root: Latin 'quorum' (of whom), from legal commissions naming members required to be present
words.example: Only eight council members arrived, one short of the quorum, so no votes could be taken.

words.definition: a traitor who collaborates with an enemy occupying their country
words.mnemonic: named after Vidkun Quisling, who helped the Nazis rule Norway — his name became the word for traitor
words.root: from Vidkun Quisling, Norwegian politician who aided the German occupation in WWII
words.example: The resistance fighters despised the quisling who fed the invaders their secrets.

words.definition: aggressively greedy; grasping for more than one needs
words.mnemonic: 'rapacious' contains 'rap' like a raptor (hawk) that snatches prey — a rapacious person snatches wealth
words.root: Latin 'rapere' (to seize, snatch), same root as 'rapture' and 'raptor'
words.example: The rapacious landlord raised the rent every few months, squeezing tenants for all they had.

words.definition: to cause lasting annoyance or bitterness
words.mnemonic: 'rankle' sounds like 'rankled ankle' — an old injury that keeps aching, just as an insult keeps stinging
words.root: from Old French 'rancler' (to fester), related to 'draoncle' (festering sore)
words.example: The unfair grade continued to rankle him for weeks after the test was returned.

words.definition: completely absorbed and fascinated by something
words.mnemonic: 'rapt' = wrapped up in attention, as if gift-wrapped by the story you're hearing
words.example: The children sat in rapt silence as the storyteller described the dragon's cave.

words.definition: to formally approve or confirm an agreement, making it official
words.mnemonic: 'rat-ify' — think of officials signing a treaty to make it 'rate' as official law
words.example: The senate voted to ratify the treaty, binding both nations to its terms.

words.definition: to reject or refuse someone bluntly and abruptly
words.mnemonic: 'rebuff' — she buffed him off, brushing away his offer coldly
words.root: from Old French 'rebuffer', from Italian 'ribuffo' (a check, reproof)
words.example: He tried to apologize, but she rebuffed him and walked away without a word.

words.definition: to draw back suddenly in fear, disgust, or shock
words.mnemonic: a spring re-coils, snapping backward — you recoil, jerking back from something horrible
words.example: She recoiled in horror when she saw the spider crawling across her pillow.

words.definition: dealing with subjects so deep or obscure that few people understand them
words.mnemonic: 'recondite' hides knowledge like something 're-condensed' and locked away in a hidden vault
words.root: Latin 'reconditus' (hidden, put away), from 'recondere' (to store, conceal)
words.example: His dissertation on recondite points of ancient grammar baffled everyone but a handful of scholars.

words.definition: the setting right of a wrong; compensation for a harm or grievance
words.mnemonic: 're-dress' a wound — to redress a wrong is to bandage and fix an injustice
words.root: Old French 'redrecier' (to set upright again), from 're-' + 'drecier' (to straighten)
words.example: The workers demanded redress for the wages they had been unfairly denied.

words.definition: stubbornly resistant to control, authority, or treatment
words.mnemonic: a 'refractory' student refracts (bends away) every rule instead of obeying it
words.example: The refractory colt refused every command and bolted whenever the trainer approached.

words.definition: careless or negligent in doing one's duty
words.mnemonic: if you 're-miss' your responsibilities again and again, you are remiss
words.example: It would be remiss of me not to thank the volunteers who made the event possible.

words.definition: to protest or argue forcefully against something
words.mnemonic: 're-monstrate' — to demonstrate again and again in objection, showing your strong disapproval
words.root: Latin 'remonstrare' (to show, point out), from 're-' + 'monstrare' (to show)
words.example: The customer remonstrated with the manager over the broken product and the refusal to refund.

words.definition: quick, clever, and witty conversation or replies
words.mnemonic: 'repartee' sounds French and fancy — the sparkling wit traded at a lively party ('partee')
words.root: French 'repartie' (a quick reply), from 'repartir' (to reply promptly)
words.example: Their dinner-table repartee was so quick and clever that the guests could barely keep up.

words.definition: a state of restful calm, peace, or relaxation
words.mnemonic: 're-pose' — to strike a still, restful pose; repose is peaceful stillness
words.root: Latin 'repausare' (to cause to rest), from 're-' + 'pausare' (to pause)
words.example: After the long journey, she found repose in a quiet cottage by the lake.

words.definition: an act of retaliation, especially for an injury or attack
words.mnemonic: 're-prisal' — to re-seize (re-'prise') what was taken, striking back in reprisal
words.root: Old French 'reprisaille', from Latin 'reprehendere' (to seize again)
words.example: Fearing reprisal from the gang, the witness refused to testify in court.

words.definition: extremely distasteful and offensive; causing disgust
words.mnemonic: 'repugnant' contains 'pug' + 'nant' — imagine a snarling pug so gross it makes you recoil
words.root: Latin 'repugnare' (to fight against, resist), from 're-' + 'pugnare' (to fight)
words.example: The idea of betraying his best friend was repugnant to him.

words.definition: required or necessary for a particular purpose
words.mnemonic: 'requisite' shares 'requi-' with 'require' — a requisite is what's required
words.example: She lacked the requisite experience for the job and was passed over for a senior candidate.

words.definition: rising again, becoming strong or popular after a decline
words.mnemonic: 're-surge' — a wave that surges up again; a resurgent team surges back to the top
words.example: After years of losing seasons, the resurgent team stormed into the playoffs.

words.definition: deserved punishment for a wrong done; payback for evil
words.mnemonic: 're-tribut-ion' — paying back a 'tribute' of punishment for what someone deserves
words.root: Latin 'retribuere' (to pay back), from 're-' + 'tribuere' (to assign, pay)
words.example: The villain feared that retribution for his crimes would one day catch up with him.

words.definition: moving backward; returning to a worse or more primitive state
words.mnemonic: 'retro' (backward) + 'grade' (step) — a retrograde step moves you backward
words.example: Critics called the new law a retrograde move that undid decades of progress.

words.definition: a state of pleasant daydreaming; being lost in one's thoughts
words.mnemonic: 'reverie' sounds like 'revery' of a dreamer drifting far away in his own private world
words.example: The teacher's question snapped him out of a reverie about summer vacation.

words.definition: referring to humor that is crude, vulgar, and irreverent about sex
words.mnemonic: 'ribald' sounds like 'ribbing bald' — the rowdy, off-color jokes told at a raucous tavern
words.example: The old sailor entertained the crew with ribald tales unfit for polite company.

words.definition: a quick, sharp reply or counterattack in conversation
words.mnemonic: in fencing a 'riposte' is the instant return thrust — a verbal riposte thrusts back a witty reply
words.example: Stung by the insult, she shot back a riposte so clever the whole room laughed.

words.definition: so ridiculous as to provoke laughter; laughable
words.mnemonic: 'risible' comes from Latin 'ridere' (to laugh) — the same root as 'ridiculous' and 'derision'; something risible makes you burst out laughing
words.example: His excuse for missing the deadline was so risible that even his boss struggled not to smile.

words.definition: having a healthy reddish complexion; ruddy
words.mnemonic: 'rubicund' contains 'rubi-' like 'ruby' — a ruby-red, rosy face
words.example: The rubicund innkeeper, red-cheeked from the fire, greeted every traveler with a hearty laugh.

words.definition: regarded as too important or holy to be criticized, changed, or interfered with
words.mnemonic: 'sacro-' (sacred) + 'sanct' (holy) = doubly holy, hands off
words.example: In their household, Sunday dinner was sacrosanct, and no one dared schedule anything else during it.

words.definition: most noticeable or important; standing out
words.mnemonic: a 'sail' on the horizon jumps out at you — the salient point
words.root: Latin 'salire' (to leap), so it 'leaps out' at you
words.example: She skimmed the report and highlighted only the salient facts for the board.

words.definition: having a sickly yellowish or pale complexion
words.mnemonic: sounds like 'shallow' — a shallow, sallow, sickly face with no healthy color
words.example: After weeks of illness indoors, his skin had turned sallow and thin.

words.definition: to satisfy a desire or appetite fully, often to excess
words.mnemonic: sate = the root of 'satisfy'; eat until you are sated
words.example: No amount of praise could sate his craving for attention.

words.definition: a sharp split or division within a group over beliefs or aims
words.mnemonic: 'sciss-' like scissors cutting a group in two — a schism
words.example: A deep schism formed in the club when members disagreed over the new rules.

words.definition: a young descendant of a notable family; also a shoot grafted onto a plant
words.mnemonic: a 'scion' is the young 'son' of an important line
words.root: Old French 'cion' (a plant shoot or twig)
words.example: As the scion of a banking dynasty, he was expected to run the firm one day.

words.definition: a paid job or position that requires little or no actual work
words.mnemonic: 'sine' (without) + 'cure' (care/work) — a job without real care or labor
words.example: The title of honorary chairman was a sinecure that paid well but demanded nothing.

words.definition: having many curves and turns; winding and graceful
words.mnemonic: a 'sine' wave from math winds up and down — sinuous
words.example: The sinuous river coiled through the valley like a dropped ribbon.

words.definition: morally low and dishonorable; also dirty and squalid
words.mnemonic: 'sordid' sounds like 'sorted into dirt' — grimy and disgraceful
words.example: The tabloid exposed all the sordid details of the politician's secret dealings.

words.definition: bad-tempered, irritable, and spiteful
words.mnemonic: the spleen was once thought to hold anger — a splenetic person vents that spleen
words.root: Greek 'splen' (spleen), the organ once linked to ill temper
words.example: The splenetic old critic seemed to hate every play he was sent to review.

words.definition: to make dull, useless, or ineffective; to cause to feel stupid and drained
words.mnemonic: 'stult-' like 'stultus' (fool) — to make someone feel like a stupefied fool
words.example: Years of mindless paperwork had stultified his once-restless imagination.

words.definition: lying flat on the back; also passive and failing to act when action is needed
words.mnemonic: 'su-PINE' — you lie on a soft PINE bed on your back, doing nothing
words.example: The senate was criticized as supine for accepting the ruling without protest.

words.definition: to take the place of someone or something, often by force or scheming
words.mnemonic: 'supplant' = plant yourself in someone else's spot and push them out
words.example: Streaming services have largely supplanted the old rental video stores.

words.definition: to guess or conclude something from limited evidence
words.mnemonic: 'sir, my eyes' saw only a clue, so I surmise the rest
words.example: From the muddy boots by the door, she surmised that he had been hiking.

words.definition: to break or split apart, often violently
words.mnemonic: to tear a-'sunder' means to rip completely apart
words.example: No argument could sunder the bond the two sisters had built over decades.

words.definition: comfort or relief in a time of sadness or distress
words.mnemonic: a 'solace' feels 'so less' painful — it eases your grief
words.example: After the loss, she found solace in long walks and old letters from her mother.

words.definition: made up of short, sharp, disconnected sounds or movements
words.mnemonic: in music, staccato notes are 'stuck-and-cut-off' — short and detached
words.example: He answered in staccato bursts, as if every word cost him effort.

words.definition: a fixed or limited period spent doing something
words.mnemonic: a short 'stint' is a set stretch — don't 'stint' (skimp) on it
words.example: After a two-year stint teaching abroad, she returned home to study law.

words.definition: to demand or state a requirement as part of an agreement
words.mnemonic: a contract's 'stipulations' are the strict 'staples' holding the deal together
words.example: The lease stipulated that no pets could live in the apartment.

words.definition: dull, old-fashioned, and boringly conventional
words.mnemonic: 'stodgy' oatmeal is heavy and boring — like a stodgy, stuffy person
words.example: The students found the stodgy old textbook impossible to stay awake through.

words.definition: a dazed state in which one can barely think or respond
words.mnemonic: 'stupor' makes you look 'stupid' — glassy-eyed and unresponsive
words.example: He sat in a stupor after the long exam, unable to form a single sentence.

words.definition: seeking to secretly undermine or overthrow an established system
words.mnemonic: 'sub-' (under) + 'vers' (turn) — turning things over from underneath
words.example: The banned novel was considered subversive for mocking the government.

words.definition: to damage or stain the purity or reputation of something
words.mnemonic: to 'sully' is to make dirty — one lie can sully a good name
words.example: He refused to sully his family's honor by cheating to win.

words.definition: to overcome a difficulty or obstacle
words.mnemonic: 'sur-' (over) + 'mount' — you climb up and over the obstacle
words.example: With practice and grit, she surmounted her fear of speaking in public.

words.definition: Cloudy, muddy, or unclear because of stirred-up sediment.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'turbo mud' — a turbo churns the water turbid.
words.example: After the storm the once-clear river ran turbid with silt and debris.

words.definition: A principle or belief that a person or group firmly holds.
words.mnemonic: A 'tenet' is a belief a group holds — like a 'tenant' holding an apartment.
words.example: Nonviolence is a central tenet of the movement she joined.

words.definition: Only slightly relevant; straying off the main point.
words.mnemonic: A tangent line barely touches a circle — a tangential remark barely touches the topic.
words.example: His answer was so tangential that the class forgot what question was even asked.

words.definition: Saying the same thing twice using different words; needless repetition.
words.mnemonic: 'Taut-ology' — the idea is stretched taut and repeated to fill space.
words.example: 'A free gift' is a tautology, since every gift is already free.

words.definition: To change something completely into a different form or substance.
words.mnemonic: 'Trans-mute' — move across (trans) into a mutated new shape.
words.example: Alchemists dreamed of a formula that could transmute lead into gold.

words.definition: Sharp, forceful, and clearly expressed; cutting.
words.mnemonic: A 'trench' is dug by a sharp blade — trenchant criticism cuts deep.
words.example: Her trenchant analysis exposed the flaw everyone else had missed.

words.definition: Able to be defended or maintained against objection.
words.mnemonic: If a position can be 'held' (tenere), it is tenable — you can keep it.
words.example: Once the new evidence appeared, his theory was no longer tenable.

words.definition: Dark, shadowy, and gloomy.
words.mnemonic: 'Tenebrous' hides a 'ten-ebony' color — as dark as ebony wood.
words.example: They crept through the tenebrous cellar with only a flickering candle.

words.definition: Lasting only a short time; not permanent.
words.mnemonic: Like a 'transit' passenger, it's just passing through — transitory.
words.example: Fame can be transitory, vanishing as quickly as it arrived.

words.definition: To happen or take place; also, to become known.
words.mnemonic: 'Trans-pire' — like sweat that seeps through skin, the truth of what happened leaks out.
words.example: No one knew what had truly transpired during the closed-door meeting.

words.definition: A person who deserts one side or cause to join the opposite one.
words.mnemonic: Someone who literally turns their coat inside out to switch sides — a turncoat.
words.root: From 'turn' + 'coat', the image of reversing a garment to show a new allegiance.
words.example: The general was branded a turncoat when he joined the enemy's army.

words.definition: Very hot and dry; also, intensely passionate.
words.mnemonic: 'Torrid' sounds like 'toasted' — the torrid desert toasts everything.
words.example: They crossed the torrid plains at dawn to avoid the punishing midday heat.

words.definition: Swollen or bulging; also, pompous and inflated in style.
words.mnemonic: 'Tumid' hides 'tummy' — a swollen tummy is tumid.
words.example: His tumid ankle ballooned after he twisted it on the trail.

words.definition: Expressing a strong, biased point of view rather than being neutral.
words.mnemonic: It has a 'tendency' to push one side — tendentious.
words.example: The article was so tendentious that it read more like propaganda than reporting.

words.definition: A harsh, overbearing, quarrelsome person, especially a woman.
words.mnemonic: 'Term-again-t' — she keeps giving you terms and orders again and again.
words.root: From 'Tervagant', an imaginary violent deity in medieval mystery plays.
words.example: The old landlady was a termagant who scolded every tenant who dared arrive late.

words.definition: The state of being under someone's power or control; bondage.
words.mnemonic: Held in 'thrall' sounds like held in a 'wall' — trapped and controlled.
words.example: The audience sat in thrall, unable to look away from the magician's act.

words.definition: To speak badly of someone in order to damage their reputation.
words.mnemonic: 'Tra-duce' — dragged down and reduced by lies.
words.root: Latin 'traducere' (to lead across, expose to shame).
words.example: He threatened to sue anyone who tried to traduce his good name.

words.definition: To restrict, hamper, or confine someone's freedom or movement.
words.mnemonic: A 'tram' runs on rails that trammel it — it can't leave the tracks.
words.root: Latin 'tremaculum' (a net), later a shackle-like restraint.
words.example: She felt trammeled by rules that dictated every hour of her day.

words.definition: To give in weakly and submissively to someone more powerful.
words.mnemonic: A small 'truckle' bed rolls under the big bed — the lesser one gives way.
words.root: From 'truckle bed' (a low bed on wheels stored beneath a higher one).
words.example: He refused to truckle to the bully's demands, no matter the threats.

words.definition: A statement so obviously true that it hardly needs saying.
words.mnemonic: A 'true-ism' — a plain truth stated as if it were wisdom.
words.example: "Hard work pays off" is a truism repeated in every graduation speech.

words.definition: A private, often secret meeting between lovers.
words.mnemonic: 'Tryst' sounds like 'trust' — you must trust the person you meet in secret.
words.example: They arranged a midnight tryst in the garden, hidden from prying eyes.

words.definition: Sluggish, inactive, and lacking energy.
words.mnemonic: A 'torpid' animal is like a 'torpedo' stuck in the mud — going nowhere.
words.root: Latin 'torpidus' (numb), from 'torpere' (to be stiff or numb).
words.example: The bear grew torpid as winter set in and finally sank into hibernation.

words.definition: Great suffering, hardship, or a cause of such distress.
words.mnemonic: 'Tribulation' contains 'trouble' — a season of deep trouble.
words.root: Latin 'tribulare' (to press, afflict), from 'tribulum' (a threshing sledge).
words.example: After years of tribulation, the refugees finally found a place to call home.

words.definition: A cave dweller; figuratively, a person who is old-fashioned or deliberately ignorant.
words.mnemonic: 'Trog-lo-dyte' — a low-down 'trog' who lives in a cave and won't come out.
words.example: He called anyone who refused to use email a hopeless troglodyte.

words.definition: To delay or stall in order to gain time or avoid deciding.
words.mnemonic: To 'temporize' is to play for more 'time' (tempo).
words.example: Rather than answer, the senator temporized, promising a decision "soon."

words.definition: Stormy, turbulent, and full of violent emotion.
words.mnemonic: A 'tempest' is a storm, so a tempestuous mood is a storm of feeling.
words.example: Their tempestuous relationship swung between passionate love and furious fights.

words.definition: A song, poem, or speech of mourning for the dead.
words.mnemonic: 'Thren-ody' sounds like a sorrowful 'melody' sung at a funeral.
words.example: The composer wrote a haunting threnody to honor the victims of the disaster.

words.definition: A person who flatters and serves the powerful to win favor.
words.mnemonic: An old 'toad-eater' pretended to eat toads to please his master — shortened to toady.
words.root: From 'toad-eater', a quack's assistant who feigned eating poisonous toads.
words.example: The manager was surrounded by toadies who agreed with everything he said.

words.definition: An alarm bell or signal warning of danger.
words.mnemonic: 'Tocsin' sounds like 'toxin' — a bell that warns of a toxic danger coming.
words.example: The village tocsin rang out the moment the enemy's ships appeared on the horizon.

words.definition: The final point or end of something, especially a route or line.
words.mnemonic: A bus 'terminal' is the terminus — where the line terminates.
words.example: The train slowed as it reached the terminus, the last stop on the line.

words.definition: kept hidden; existing beyond what is openly admitted, usually a secret motive
words.mnemonic: 'ulterior' sounds like 'interior' — a motive kept on the inside where no one can see it.
words.root: Latin 'ulterior' = farther, more distant (beyond the surface).
words.example: She seemed generous, but her ulterior motive was to be named in the will.

words.definition: modest and not drawing attention to oneself
words.mnemonic: 'un-assuming' = does NOT assume it's better than you; quietly humble.
words.example: Despite winning three national prizes, the unassuming scientist never mentioned them.

words.definition: free from restrictions or control
words.mnemonic: a 'fetter' is a chain on the ankle — 'un-fettered' means the chains are off.
words.root: un- + fetter (a shackle for the feet).
words.example: With unfettered access to the archive, the historian read documents no one else had seen.

words.definition: awkward and clumsy in movement or appearance
words.mnemonic: 'un-gainly' — a baby giraffe gains no grace when it walks; all legs and wobble.
words.example: The ungainly teenager tripped over his own feet crossing the stage.

words.definition: never stopping or relaxing; relentless
words.mnemonic: 'un-remitting' — a fever that won't 'remit' (let up) keeps burning nonstop.
words.example: After weeks of unremitting rain, the fields had turned to swamp.

words.definition: not aware of what is happening; unknowing
words.mnemonic: 'un-witting' — without your 'wits' about you, you don't know what's going on.
words.example: He became an unwitting accomplice when he lent his car to the thieves.

words.definition: unusual; not what is customary or expected
words.mnemonic: 'un-wonted' — NOT what you're 'wont' (accustomed) to do; strangely out of habit. Don't confuse with 'unwanted.'
words.example: The normally silent boy spoke with unwonted enthusiasm about the trip.

words.definition: leaving no doubt; completely clear and firm
words.mnemonic: 'un-equi-vocal' — no two equal 'voices' to argue; only one clear answer.
words.root: un- + equi- (equal) + voc (voice) — not speaking two ways.
words.example: The jury's verdict was an unequivocal 'not guilty.'

words.definition: remaining calm and composed under pressure
words.mnemonic: a bird 'flaps' when panicked — 'un-flappable' means you never flap; steady no matter what.
words.example: Even as alarms blared, the unflappable pilot calmly guided the plane down.

words.definition: beyond doubt or criticism; completely trustworthy
words.mnemonic: you can't 'impeach' (accuse/charge) it — the record is spotless.
words.root: un- + impeach (to accuse, cast doubt on).
words.example: Her sources were unimpeachable, so the editor printed the story without hesitation.

words.definition: not attractive or impressive; making a dull first impression
words.mnemonic: it doesn't 'pre-possess' you — it fails to grab you in advance; forgettable.
words.example: The unprepossessing little shop turned out to sell the best noodles in the city.

words.definition: a person who has newly risen to wealth or power and behaves arrogantly
words.mnemonic: someone who 'started up' suddenly and struts around like they own the place.
words.root: up + start — one who has sprung up abruptly.
words.example: The old families sneered at the upstart who had made his fortune overnight.

words.definition: charging illegally or unfairly high interest on a loan
words.mnemonic: a loan shark 'uses you' with usurious rates that bleed you dry.
words.example: The usurious lender charged 40 percent interest, trapping borrowers in endless debt.

words.definition: aiming for an ideal but impossibly perfect society or plan
words.mnemonic: 'Utopia' = 'no-place' in Greek; a perfect world that exists nowhere real.
words.example: His utopian scheme to abolish all money sounded noble but could never work.

words.definition: achieving nothing; useless or futile
words.mnemonic: 'un-availing' — it does not 'avail' (help) you at all; effort wasted.
words.example: Their desperate, unavailing efforts to bail out the sinking boat came too late.

words.definition: excessively proud of oneself; boastful in an empty way
words.mnemonic: 'vain' + 'glory' — someone chasing empty glory just to feed their vanity.
words.example: The vainglorious general kept retelling his single victory as if he had won the whole war.

words.definition: expressing a farewell; relating to a goodbye
words.mnemonic: the 'valedictorian' gives the goodbye speech — 'vale' + 'dict' = 'saying farewell.'
words.example: In her valedictory address, the retiring principal thanked forty years of students.

words.definition: the leading position or front group in a movement or advance
words.mnemonic: the 'van-guard' guards the front — the troops out ahead of everyone else.
words.root: Old French 'avant-garde' — the advance guard of an army.
words.example: These young artists were in the vanguard of a whole new style of painting.

words.definition: an unexpected change or shift in fortune or circumstances
words.mnemonic: life's 'vicissitudes' visit you like a see-saw — up, down, and never staying still.
words.example: Through all the vicissitudes of his career, from bankruptcy to fame, she stood by him.

words.definition: a short, vivid scene or descriptive sketch
words.mnemonic: a little 'vine' framing a small picture — a vignette is a brief framed glimpse.
words.root: French, diminutive of 'vigne' (vine) — decorative vine border around small images.
words.example: The memoir is really a series of vignettes, each capturing a single childhood afternoon.

words.definition: thick, sticky, and slow-flowing (of a liquid)
words.mnemonic: 'viscous' sounds like 'this cuss' — cold honey so thick it makes you cuss waiting for it to pour.
words.example: The viscous tar oozed slowly down the barrel and clung to everything it touched.

words.definition: a person's face or facial expression
words.mnemonic: 'vis' = to see (as in 'vision') — the visage is the part of you people see: your face.
words.root: Latin visus (sight, appearance) via Old French.
words.example: His stern visage softened the moment he saw the puppy.

words.definition: to spoil or weaken the quality or effectiveness of something
words.mnemonic: add a 'vice' and you 'vitiate' it — one flaw ruins the whole thing.
words.example: A single false statement can vitiate an entire legal contract.

words.definition: to bring to life; to make more lively or vivid
words.mnemonic: 'viv' = life (as in 'survive') — to vivify is to pump life into something.
words.example: A few splashes of red paint vivified the otherwise gray and lifeless mural.

words.definition: a devoted follower or enthusiastic supporter of someone or something
words.mnemonic: one who has taken a 'vow' — a votary is bound by devotion to a cause.
words.root: Latin votum (a vow) — same root as 'devote.'
words.example: A lifelong votary of the theater, she attended every opening night for fifty years.

words.definition: to grant or give something, often condescendingly or as a favor
words.mnemonic: 'vouch' + 'safe' — to safely vouch for someone by granting them a favor from on high.
words.root: Middle English 'vouchen sauf' — to warrant as safe/acceptable.
words.example: The busy director finally vouchsafed a single nod of approval and swept out.

words.definition: (of a fault or sin) minor and easily forgiven
words.mnemonic: 'venial' vs. 'venal': a VENIAL sin is a minor one you can be forgiven for — think 'vanilla,' plain and harmless.
words.example: Forgetting to sign the card was a venial slip that everyone quickly forgave.

words.definition: a position or place that gives a good view or advantage
words.mnemonic: clip 'ad-vantage' — a vantage point is the spot that gives you the advantage of seeing everything.
words.example: From his vantage atop the hill, the scout could see the enemy camp for miles.

words.definition: a strong, restless desire to travel and explore the world
words.mnemonic: WANDER + LUST = a burning 'lust' to 'wander' far from home
words.example: Gripped by wanderlust, she quit her office job to backpack across three continents.

words.definition: easily irritated and sharply spiteful in speech
words.mnemonic: like a WASP, always ready to sting you with a bitter remark
words.example: His waspish comments about her cooking left everyone at the table uncomfortable.

words.definition: a confused, tangled mass or jumble of things
words.mnemonic: a 'welter' sounds like 'swelter'—a hot, sweaty, chaotic mess of stuff
words.example: Buried in a welter of receipts and half-finished forms, he could not find the tax document.

words.definition: to travel or proceed slowly along a winding course
words.mnemonic: you WEND your way home, bending around every WINDING turn
words.example: Each evening the tired hikers would wend their way back down the mountain trail.

words.definition: a coiled or spiral arrangement, as of lines or petals
words.mnemonic: WHORL rhymes with 'swirl'—picture the swirling lines of a fingerprint
words.example: The detective studied the tiny whorl at the center of the fingerprint under a magnifier.

words.definition: to ambush or intercept someone in order to attack or detain them
words.mnemonic: lie in WAIT by the WAY (road) to LAY a trap for travelers
words.root: way + lay (to lie in wait along the way)
words.example: Bandits would waylay merchants on the lonely road through the pass.

words.definition: intensely angry; filled with wrath
words.mnemonic: WROTH is just WRATH with an 'o'—both mean boiling anger
words.example: The king was wroth when he learned the messenger had lied to his face.

words.definition: to argue or dispute noisily and at length
words.mnemonic: you get TANGLED up when you WRANGLE over petty details
words.example: The committee wrangled for hours over a single line in the budget.

words.definition: shaped or worked into form, especially by hammering
words.mnemonic: WROUGHT iron is iron that was WORKED and beaten into shape
words.example: The gate's delicate, wrought vines showed the blacksmith's years of skill.

words.definition: to move or carry gently and lightly through the air
words.mnemonic: a WAFT of scent softly floats to you, like a feather 'aloft'
words.example: The smell of fresh bread wafted from the bakery and filled the quiet street.

words.definition: a clever, amusing remark
words.mnemonic: a WITTY criticism polished into a sharp little joke
words.root: blend of 'witty' + 'criticism', coined by John Dryden
words.example: She defused the tense meeting with a well-timed witticism that made everyone laugh.

words.definition: ordinary, practical, and unexciting; everyday
words.mnemonic: a plain WORK-A-DAY: just another dull day of work
words.example: After the glamour of the tour, she struggled to settle back into workaday routines.

words.definition: well-being, prosperity, or the general good
words.mnemonic: WEAL sounds like 'wealth'—the good fortune of a people
words.example: A just ruler governs for the common weal, not for private gain.

words.definition: to obtain something by clever or slightly dishonest means
words.mnemonic: WANGLE = 'wrangle' meets 'angle'—you angle craftily to get what you want
words.example: Somehow he wangled front-row tickets to the sold-out concert.

words.definition: to sing with trilling, quavering notes, like a bird
words.mnemonic: a WARBLER bird WARBLES its wobbling, rippling song
words.example: A thrush warbled in the hedge as the morning fog lifted.

words.definition: to grow larger, stronger, or more intense over time
words.mnemonic: the moon WAXES (grows) like a candle gaining more WAX
words.example: As the debate wore on, the senator's confidence waxed while his rival's waned.

words.definition: looking sad, miserable, or run-down
words.mnemonic: WOE has BEGONE and left you—so you look utterly miserable
words.example: The lost puppy sat woebegone in the rain, whimpering for its owner.

words.definition: the smallest possible amount; a tiny particle
words.mnemonic: not one WHIT—not even a tiny 'bit' the size of a wheat grain
words.example: He did not care a whit what the critics thought of his painting.

words.definition: characterized by very dry conditions; needing little moisture
words.mnemonic: XERIC starts like 'Xerox'—but think of a dry desert, not a copier
words.example: Cacti and succulents thrive in the xeric climate of the high desert.

words.definition: relating to hospitality between a host and a guest
words.mnemonic: XENIAL shares its root with 'xeno' (stranger)—being kind to strangers as guests
words.example: Ancient Greek custom demanded xenial treatment of any traveler who knocked at the door.

words.definition: a person who is attracted to foreign peoples, cultures, or customs
words.mnemonic: XENO (foreign) + PHILE (lover)—the opposite of a xenophobe
words.example: A lifelong xenophile, she filled her home with art and recipes from every country she visited.

words.definition: to swing or turn unsteadily off a straight course
words.mnemonic: a ship YAWs when it 'wanders' side to side, jaw dropping like YAWn
words.root: origin uncertain; nautical use from 16th c.
words.example: The small plane began to yaw sharply as the crosswind gusted over the runway.

words.definition: a strong longing or craving for something
words.mnemonic: you have a YEN—a deep 'yearning'—for chocolate late at night
words.example: After years in the city, he felt a sudden yen for the quiet of his childhood farm.

words.definition: time long past; former days
words.mnemonic: 'days of YORE' sounds like 'years before'—the distant past
words.example: In days of yore, knights jousted before the castle for the queen's favor.

words.definition: a hardworking, dependable person; historically, a small landowner or attendant
words.mnemonic: a YEO-MAN was a solid 'young man' who owned his own farm and worked it well
words.example: The old sergeant did yeoman's work, quietly keeping the whole unit running.

words.definition: to talk loudly, persistently, and often complainingly
words.mnemonic: YAMMER sounds like a 'jabber' hammering on and on in your ear
words.example: The passengers yammered about the delay until the gate agent finally spoke up.

words.definition: filled with intense, eager enthusiasm for a cause
words.mnemonic: a ZEALOUS fan is 'jealous' of anyone who loves the cause less than they do
words.example: The zealous volunteers knocked on every door in the neighborhood before dusk.

words.definition: having a full, rounded, pleasingly plump figure
words.mnemonic: ZAFTIG sounds soft and 'juicy'—which is exactly its Yiddish meaning
words.example: The painter favored zaftig models, celebrating fullness rather than thinness.

words.definition: a figure of speech in which one word governs two others in different senses
words.mnemonic: ZEUGMA yokes two ideas to one verb—its Greek root literally means 'a yoking'
words.example: 'She broke his car and his heart' is a classic zeugma, since 'broke' works two ways.
